verification: every item fact-checked by a per-chunk skeptic agent; verified field = confirmed|corrected|unchecked

obligations.title: Treat Obligations as the journey-agnostic core
obligations.summary: The obligations model is to be the journey-agnostic core of the system; journeys (presentations) are adapters layered over it. The spec being defined must be precise enough to judge paradigm fit and potentially retrofit existing spikes.
obligations.quote: treat it as the **journey-agnostic core**, with journeys as adapters over it
obligations.novel_why: No existing spike separates a journey-agnostic obligations core from journey adapters; each spike models the journey directly.

obligations.title: Obligation is pure JSON-encodable data
obligations.summary: An obligation is a discrete unit contributing to journey completion, represented as pure data that is light and JSON-encodable, with no logic embedded in it.
obligations.quote: The obligation itself is **pure data** — light and JSON-encodable.
obligations.novel_why: Obligations as first-class pure-data records do not exist in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Isolate applicability logic in a sync pure deterministic evaluation engine
obligations.summary: All applicability and optionality logic lives outside the obligation data, in a separate evaluation engine that must be synchronous, pure, and deterministic.
obligations.quote: All applicability/optionality logic lives in a separate **evaluation engine** (sync, pure, deterministic)
obligations.novel_why: A dedicated obligation-evaluation engine distinct from journey logic goes beyond the spikes' selector/guard/rule mechanisms.

obligations.title: Wrap the engine in an I/O-handling orchestrator
obligations.summary: The pure evaluation engine is wrapped by an orchestrator component that is responsible for all I/O; the engine itself performs none.
obligations.quote: wrapped by an **orchestrator** that handles I/O
obligations.novel_why: An explicit engine/orchestrator split with I/O confined to the orchestrator is not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Define obligations declaratively in obligations.json
obligations.summary: Each obligation is declared in a dedicated obligations.json file, making the obligations set a declarative artefact.
obligations.quote: a discrete unit the user can or must satisfy to complete the journey. Defined declaratively in `obligations.json`.
obligations.novel_why: A dedicated obligations.json artefact is a new file/concept beyond the spikes' journey/model configs.

obligations.title: Give each obligation a persistent opaque UUID id
obligations.summary: Every obligation carries an id that is a persistent, opaque UUID, stable across renames of the human-meaningful name.
obligations.quote: a persistent, opaque identifier (UUID) attached to each obligation
obligations.novel_why: Stable opaque ids decoupled from symbolic names are part of the new obligations/persistence design, absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Use obligation id as the persistence key for fulfilments
obligations.summary: Stored fulfilments are keyed by the obligation id (not the name), so persisted data survives renames of the obligation name.
obligations.quote: The persistence key for stored fulfilments; stable across renames of the meaningful `name`.
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilment persistence keyed by stable obligation ids is a new concept; spikes key state by field name only.

obligations.title: Never display ids; generate them with tooling at authoring time
obligations.summary: Obligation ids are never shown to users and are generated by authoring-time tooling rather than written by hand.
obligations.quote: Never displayed; generated by tooling at authoring time.
obligations.novel_why: Authoring-time id-generation tooling is new; no spike has model-authoring tooling.

obligations.title: Make obligation name unique within the Obligations model
obligations.summary: The obligation name is the meaningful, code-facing symbolic identifier and must be unique within the Obligations model. Uniqueness is what lets the same string map from HTML form input through logical Page to Obligation.
obligations.quote: **Unique within the Obligations model** — uniqueness is what allows the same string to map from HTML form input → logical Page → Obligation.
obligations.novel_why: An explicit identity contract tying form input name, page, and obligation together via model-wide uniqueness is new.

obligations.title: Use the obligation name everywhere developers touch obligations
obligations.summary: The obligation name is the handle used in code, tests, i18n keys, templates, and as the HTML form input name attribute wherever the journey layer renders the obligation.
obligations.quote: in code, tests, i18n keys, templates, and as the form input `name="fullName"` where the journey layer renders them
obligations.novel_why: A single symbolic name spanning code, i18n, templates and form inputs is part of the new obligations identity contract.

obligations.title: Allow renaming obligation names between deployments
obligations.summary: Because persistence uses the separate UUID id, the obligation name may safely be renamed between deployments without losing stored fulfilments.
obligations.quote: **May be renamed between deployments** because persistence uses the separate `id`.
obligations.novel_why: Rename-safe persistence via id/name separation is new model-versioning behaviour no spike supports.

obligations.title: Model fulfilments with single or indexed cardinality
obligations.summary: A fulfilment is a record of one filling-in of an obligation. A single-cardinality obligation has at most one fulfilment; an indexed-cardinality obligation has zero or more.
obligations.quote: A single-cardinality obligation has at most one fulfilment; an indexed-cardinality obligation has zero or more.
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments as first-class records and indexed-cardinality obligations are both flagged-novel concepts absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Set fulfilmentId equal to obligationId for single-cardinality
obligations.summary: For single-cardinality obligations the fulfilment identifier is identical to the obligation id, since the obligation id alone addresses the at-most-one fulfilment.
obligations.quote: For **single-cardinality** obligations: `fulfilmentId === obligationId`.
obligations.novel_why: FulfilmentId addressing scheme is part of the new fulfilments record model.

obligations.title: Give indexed fulfilments per-fulfilment ids whose shape depends on source
obligations.summary: For indexed-cardinality obligations, each fulfilment gets its own id within the obligation's collection, and the shape of that id depends on the obligation's source (detailed in the Fulfilments storage section).
obligations.quote: For **indexed-cardinality** obligations: a per-fulfilment id within the obligation's collection. Shape depends on `source`
obligations.novel_why: Per-fulfilment ids and source-dependent id shapes are new; spikes have no indexed fulfilment addressing.

obligations.title: Support incomplete, complete, and stale fulfilment states
obligations.summary: A fulfilment can be in one of three states: incomplete (partially answered), complete, or stale.
obligations.quote: Fulfilments can be incomplete (partially answered), complete, or stale.
obligations.novel_why: Staleness as an explicit fulfilment state goes beyond the spikes' answer/invalidation handling.

obligations.title: Support system-handled obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation may be fulfilled by the system rather than the user; these are called system-handled obligations and their fulfilments are recorded the same way.
obligations.quote: by a user (or by the system, for system-handled obligations)
obligations.novel_why: System-fulfilled obligations have no counterpart in the spikes, where all answers come from user form input.

obligations.title: Bind each Service to one Obligations definition and one-or-more Flows
obligations.summary: A Service (the thing the user is trying to do) has exactly one Obligations definition acting as its data contract, and one or more Flows presenting that contract.
obligations.quote: A Service has exactly **one** Obligations definition (the data contract) and **one or more** Flows
obligations.novel_why: The Service/Obligations/Flows layering with multiple presentations of one contract is new architecture beyond the single-journey spikes.

obligations.title: Define Flow as a static presentation description
obligations.summary: A Flow statically describes how a Service is delivered: which pages exist, their order, how obligations are grouped, per-page hard/soft mandate, copy/labels/hints, and acquisition mechanisms.
obligations.quote: which pages exist, in what order, how obligations are grouped, per-page hard/soft mandate, copy/labels/hints, acquisition mechanisms
obligations.novel_why: Per-page hard/soft mandates and acquisition mechanisms as Flow-level data are novel concepts.

obligations.title: Allow multiple coexisting Flows referencing the same Obligations
obligations.summary: Multiple Flows can coexist for one Service (e.g. a skeleton Flow for rapid validation alongside a polished production Flow), and all Flows must reference the same underlying Obligations.
obligations.quote: All Flows of a Service reference the same underlying Obligations.
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow equivalence over one obligations contract is explicitly beyond anything the four spikes implement.

obligations.title: Model Journey as a runtime instance of one Flow with its own id
obligations.summary: A Journey is a runtime instance of exactly one Flow being navigated by a user, and each Journey has its own identifier.
obligations.quote: a runtime instance of **one Flow** being navigated by a user
obligations.novel_why: Journey as an identified runtime instance of a chosen Flow is new; spikes have only session-scoped state.

obligations.title: Scope the fulfilments map to the Journey, keyed by obligation id
obligations.summary: The Journey owns the map of fulfilments, and that map is keyed by obligation id, not by name or session.
obligations.quote: Owns the **fulfilments map** keyed by obligation id.
obligations.novel_why: Journey-owned, obligation-id-keyed fulfilment storage replaces the spikes' session answer bags.

obligations.title: Isolate concurrent Journeys; user/session is not the uniqueness scope
obligations.summary: Multiple Journeys can coexist for the same user (for example in different browser tabs) and each is isolated; neither the user nor the session is the uniqueness scope for Journey state.
obligations.quote: "user" and "session" are explicitly NOT the uniqueness scope
obligations.novel_why: Multi-tab isolated journeys break from the spikes' single session-scoped state model.

obligations.title: Give each Page an ordered presents list of obligations plus mandate
obligations.summary: A Page is a leaf Container in a Flow holding an ordered list of presents entries, each pairing an obligation with a mandate, that the page asks the user about.
obligations.quote: Has an ordered list of `presents` entries (obligations + mandate) that it asks the user about.
obligations.novel_why: Pages presenting obligations with per-entry mandates is new; spikes attach fields to pages without a mandate concept.

obligations.title: Map each Page 1:1 to an HTML artefact
obligations.summary: Every Page in a Flow corresponds one-to-one with an HTML artefact (template/rendered page) in the implementation.
obligations.quote: Maps 1:1 to an HTML artefact in the implementation.

obligations.title: Allow Sections and SubSections to nest arbitrarily
obligations.summary: Sections and SubSections are Group containers holding an ordered list of child Containers (Pages or further SubSections), and they may nest to arbitrary depth.
obligations.quote: Sections and SubSections nest arbitrarily
obligations.novel_why: Arbitrary-depth section nesting goes beyond the spikes' flat hub-with-sections task list.

obligations.title: Show top-level Sections on the Task List by default
obligations.summary: The Task List (hub) page displays the Flow's top-level Sections by default.
obligations.quote: top-level Sections are what appear on the Task List by default

obligations.title: Structure a Flow's content as a tree of Containers
obligations.summary: Container is the collective term for Pages and Groups (Sections/SubSections); a Flow's entire content is a tree of Containers.
obligations.quote: A Flow's content is a tree of Containers.
obligations.novel_why: A recursive Container tree is a new structural model versus the spikes' flat page/section lists.

obligations.title: Derive Page Fulfilled from in-scope mandatory obligations
obligations.summary: A Page is Fulfilled if and only if every in-scope mandatory obligation it presents is fulfilled.
obligations.quote: A Page is Fulfilled iff every in-scope mandatory obligation it presents is fulfilled.
obligations.novel_why: Recasts page completion in terms of in-scope mandatory obligations rather than the spikes' per-page answer checks.

obligations.title: Derive Group Fulfilled recursively from applicable children
obligations.summary: A Group (Section/SubSection) is Fulfilled if and only if every applicable child Container is Fulfilled; non-applicable children are excluded from the calculation.
obligations.quote: A Group is Fulfilled iff every applicable child Container is Fulfilled.
obligations.novel_why: Recursive fulfilment over an arbitrary Container tree with applicability filtering exceeds the spikes' flat task-status derivation.

obligations.title: Enable Submit on CYA only when the Journey is Fulfilled
obligations.summary: A Journey is Fulfilled if and only if every top-level Section is Fulfilled, and only at that point can the user Submit on the check-your-answers page.
obligations.quote: A Journey is Fulfilled iff every top-level Section is Fulfilled — at which point the user can Submit on the CYA page.
obligations.novel_why: An explicit Submit action gated on Journey fulfilment introduces a submit lifecycle absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Freeze the Journey after Submit
obligations.summary: Submitted means the Journey has been finalised by the user clicking Submit on CYA; after that no further changes to the Journey are allowed.
obligations.quote: the Journey has been finalised: the user has clicked Submit on CYA. No further changes.
obligations.novel_why: A terminal Submitted state that locks the journey is new submit-lifecycle behaviour.

obligations.title: Avoid the term Complete in the vocabulary
obligations.summary: The vocabulary deliberately excludes 'Complete' as a separate term because it confusingly overlapped both Fulfilled and Submitted; only Fulfilled and Submitted are used.
obligations.quote: We deliberately avoid "Complete" as a separate term — it overlapped Fulfilled and Submitted confusingly.
obligations.novel_why: A settled terminology rule specific to the new obligations vocabulary, not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Implement ObligationEvaluator as a pure sync single-call function
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator is a single pure, synchronous function operating on the Obligations layer, taking the Obligations definition and the current Fulfilments as its inputs.
obligations.quote: the pure, sync function that operates on the Obligations layer. Given `(Obligations, Fulfilments)`
obligations.novel_why: A single-call obligation-layer evaluator is a new runtime component distinct from all four spikes' engines.

obligations.title: Have ObligationEvaluator prune fulfilments to fit the current model
obligations.summary: Part of the ObligationEvaluator's output is an amended fulfilments set, pruned so stored fulfilments fit the current obligations model (dropping ones that no longer apply).
obligations.quote: returns amended fulfilments (pruned to fit the current model)
obligations.novel_why: Centralised pruning of fulfilments against the current model (including model change) goes beyond the spikes' dependent-answer invalidation.

obligations.title: Return per-obligation state including authored reasons
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator also returns per-obligation state: whether each obligation is in-scope, mandatory, fulfilled, what remains, and any authored reasons explaining why it is demanded.
obligations.quote: per-obligation state (in-scope, mandatory, fulfilled, remaining, authored reasons)
obligations.novel_why: A uniform per-obligation state report with authored-reason surfacing is a flagged-novel obligations concept (only spike-c has reasons, and not in this shape).

obligations.title: Keep ObligationEvaluator Service-scoped and Flow-agnostic
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator is scoped to the Service and must know nothing about Flows; all Flow-specific reasoning is layered elsewhere.
obligations.quote: Service-scoped; knows nothing about Flows. Single-call function.
obligations.novel_why: A strict obligations/Flow layering boundary in the evaluator does not exist in the spikes.

obligations.title: Implement JourneyEvaluator as small pure Flow-layer primitives
obligations.summary: The JourneyEvaluator is a collection of pure, synchronous functions on the Flow layer; each is a small primitive answering one specific question about Flow state, taking the ObligationEvaluator's output as input.
obligations.quote: a collection of pure, sync functions that operate on the Flow layer. Each is a small primitive answering a specific question
obligations.novel_why: A two-layer evaluator architecture (obligation evaluator feeding flow-level primitives) is new relative to all spikes.

obligations.title: Converge future implementations on fulfilments vocabulary
obligations.summary: The existing prototype's answers/quote state-bag naming is a legacy convention; any future implementation aligned with the obligations model must converge on the fulfilments vocabulary rather than keeping both.
obligations.quote: a future implementation aligned with the obligations model would converge on one vocabulary
obligations.novel_why: Mandates replacing the spikes' answers/quote state naming with the new fulfilments abstraction.

obligations.title: Support hard and soft per-page mandates in the Flow
obligations.summary: A Flow's per-page mandate is not a single boolean: the definition distinguishes hard vs soft mandates, specified per page in the Flow (alongside copy/labels/hints and acquisition mechanisms). Existing items DEF-18/DEF-23 record that a mandate exists per presents entry, but none captures the two-strength hard/soft distinction the model must represent.
obligations.quote: how obligations are grouped, per-page hard/soft mandate, copy/labels/hints, acquisition mechanisms.
obligations.novel_why: No mandate concept exists in any spike ('mandate' appears only in obligations.md); Spike C's require rules demand fields with no hard/soft strength distinction.

obligations.title: Derive Journey Fulfilled from top-level Sections
obligations.summary: The Journey level has its own explicit Fulfilled rule: a Journey is Fulfilled iff every top-level Section is Fulfilled. DEF-29 covers Group recursion and DEF-30 gates Submit on Journey Fulfilled, but neither states the Journey-level derivation rule that connects them.
obligations.quote: A Journey is Fulfilled iff every top-level Section is Fulfilled — at which point the user can Submit on the CYA page.

obligations.title: Layer JourneyEvaluator on ObligationEvaluator output
obligations.summary: JourneyEvaluator primitives must consume the ObligationEvaluator's output as an input rather than recomputing obligation-layer state; the Flow layer depends on the Obligations layer, never the reverse.
obligations.quote: Flow-scoped; uses ObligationEvaluator output as an input.
obligations.novel_why: No spike has a two-layer evaluator split; Spike C's closest primitive is a single rules engine with no separate flow-layer evaluator consuming its output.

obligations.title: Fulfilments hold the canonical data
obligations.summary: Fulfilling an obligation means providing the canonical data for it — the fulfilment record stores the canonical form, not a presentation- or acquisition-specific representation.
obligations.quote: the user (or the system) fulfils an obligation by providing the canonical data
obligations.novel_why: The spikes' answers/quote state bags store raw form answers with no canonical-data notion distinct from the acquisition mechanism.

obligations.title: Spec must be precise enough to judge paradigm fit and retrofit spikes
obligations.summary: The obligation concept is being iteratively defined with the goal of nailing a spec precise enough to (a) judge paradigm fit, (b) potentially retrofit one or more of the existing spikes, and (c) treat it as the journey-agnostic core, with journeys as adapters over it.
obligations.quote: nailing a precise enough spec to (a) judge paradigm fit, (b) potentially retrofit one or more spikes
obligations.novel_why: A meta-goal about evaluating and retrofitting the four spikes; no spike contains or anticipates this assessment criterion.

obligations.title: Support two broad obligation shapes
obligations.summary: The data model must classify every obligation as one of two shapes: user-facing (satisfied by the user) or system-handled (satisfied by the runtime).
obligations.quote: Obligations come in two broad shapes
obligations.novel_why: Obligations as first-class records with a shape taxonomy do not exist in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Render user-facing obligations via the journey layer
obligations.summary: User-facing obligations are satisfied by the user answering a question, uploading a file, or accepting a notice, and it is the journey layer's job to render them.
obligations.quote: the user satisfies them by answering a question, uploading a file, accepting a notice, etc. Rendered by the journey layer.
obligations.novel_why: Assigning rendering responsibility per obligation shape presumes first-class obligation records the spikes lack.

obligations.title: Satisfy system-handled obligations via a runtime callback
obligations.summary: System-handled obligations are fulfilled by the runtime on the user's behalf using a callback pattern. Sub-journey receipts and external API lookups are both of this shape.
obligations.quote: the runtime satisfies them on the user's behalf via a callback pattern. Sub-journey receipts and external API lookups are both of this shape.
obligations.novel_why: No spike has system-fulfilled obligations, sub-journey receipts, or an external-lookup callback pattern.

obligations.title: Do not render system-handled obligations; orchestrator writes results to state
obligations.summary: System-handled obligations are not rendered to the user, or at most shown as a 'checking…' state. The orchestrator triggers the work and writes the result back into journey state.
obligations.quote: Not rendered (or only rendered as a "checking…" state) — the orchestrator triggers the work and writes the result back to state.
obligations.novel_why: An orchestrator that fulfils obligations asynchronously and writes results back is beyond all four spikes.

obligations.title: Keep obligations.json pure JSON-portable data
obligations.summary: The obligations definition file must be pure data that is portable as JSON, containing no code.
obligations.quote: obligations.json — pure data, JSON-portable
obligations.novel_why: A portable, code-free obligations catalogue is a new artifact; spikes embed field definitions inside per-paradigm config or schema.

obligations.title: Carry both a persistent id and a meaningful name on every obligation
obligations.summary: Each obligation record has two identifiers: a persistent UUID `id` used as the persistence key, and a meaningful `name` used by code, templates, and i18n.
obligations.quote: Each obligation carries both a persistent `id` (the persistence key — UUID) and a meaningful `name` (the code / template / i18n surface).
obligations.novel_why: Dual-identifier (UUID + name) obligation records exist in none of the spikes.

obligations.title: Carry indexedBy metadata on indexed obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation with indexed cardinality declares an `indexedBy` object describing where the index comes from and its mutability, e.g. source 'user' with mutability 'edit-add-remove'.
obligations.quote: "indexedBy": { "source": "user", "mutability": "edit-add-remove" }
obligations.novel_why: Indexed obligations with declared index source and mutability are a novel construct beyond the spikes' add-another loop.

obligations.title: Reference sub-journeys via subJourneyRef on sub-journey-receipt obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation of type 'sub-journey-receipt' carries a `subJourneyRef` field naming the sub-journey (e.g. 'add-driver-v1') whose receipt fulfils it.
obligations.quote:
"type": "sub-journey-receipt",
    "cardinality": "single",
    "subJourneyRef": "add-driver-v1"
obligations.novel_why: Sub-journey receipts as obligation fulfilments do not appear in any spike.

obligations.title: Reference external lookups via lookupRef on lookup-result obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation of type 'lookup-result' carries a `lookupRef` field naming the external lookup (e.g. 'fraud-check-v1') whose result fulfils it.
obligations.quote:
"type": "lookup-result",
    "cardinality": "single",
    "lookupRef": "fraud-check-v1"
obligations.novel_why: External-lookup results modelled as obligations are absent from all four spikes.

obligations.title: Use a stable opaque UUID id as the persistence key
obligations.summary: Settled: every obligation's `id` is an opaque UUID and is the persistence key. It stays stable even when the meaningful `name` is renamed.
obligations.quote: Every obligation has a stable `id` — an opaque UUID; the persistence key. Stable across renames of the meaningful `name`.
obligations.novel_why: Rename-stable UUID persistence keys for fields are a model-versioning concern no spike addresses.

obligations.title: Enforce name uniqueness within the Obligations model
obligations.summary: Settled: every obligation's meaningful `name` (e.g. 'fullName') must be unique within the Obligations model. This uniqueness is what lets the same string map from HTML form input to logical Page to Obligation.
obligations.quote: **Unique within the Obligations model** — uniqueness is what allows the same string to map from HTML form input → logical Page → Obligation.
obligations.novel_why: A global name-uniqueness rule tying form inputs to obligation records is specific to the new obligations model.

obligations.title: Allow obligation names to be renamed between deployments
obligations.summary: Settled: an obligation's meaningful `name` may be renamed between deployments without breaking persisted data, because persistence keys on the separate UUID `id`.
obligations.quote: May be renamed between deployments (persistence uses the separate `id`).
obligations.novel_why: Deployment-safe renaming via id/name separation is a model-versioning capability no spike has.

obligations.title: Give each obligation a single type from an open type space
obligations.summary: Settled: every obligation has exactly one data type describing what gets produced, and the type space is open — e.g. date, string, email, file, boolean, address, payment-receipt, sub-journey-receipt, lookup-result.
obligations.quote: A single data type (the type of what gets produced). Open type space — `date`, `string`, `email`, `file`, `boolean`, `address`, `payment-receipt`, `sub-journey-receipt`, `lookup-result`, etc.
obligations.novel_why: An open, extensible type space including receipt and lookup-result types goes beyond the spikes' fixed form-field types.

obligations.title: Support single and indexed cardinality
obligations.summary: Settled: obligation cardinality is either 'single' (one fulfilment per user, e.g. date of birth) or 'indexed' (a collection of fulfilments, e.g. address history or multi-select with per-item follow-ups).
obligations.quote: `single` (one fulfilment per user, e.g. date of birth) or `indexed` (a collection of fulfilments, e.g. address history, multi-select with per-item follow-ups)
obligations.novel_why: Indexed obligations as a first-class cardinality generalise beyond the spikes' single hard-coded add-another loop.

obligations.title: Keep scoping and mandate status off the obligation record
obligations.summary: Settled: in-scope/out-of-scope, mandatory/optional, and reasons are not stored on the obligation. They are computed by the evaluation engine.
obligations.quote: NOT on the obligation. Computed by the evaluation engine — see §The evaluation engine.
obligations.novel_why: Separating computed scoping/mandates from the obligation record is a novel architectural split of the obligations model.

obligations.title: Apply most-restrictive-wins when multiple reasons fire
obligations.summary: Settled: when several reasons bring an obligation into scope with different mandate levels, the most restrictive wins — if any reason says mandatory, the obligation is mandatory for journey completion.
obligations.quote: "Most restrictive wins" — if any reason says mandatory, the obligation is mandatory for journey completion.
obligations.novel_why: Combining multiple authored mandate reasons with a most-restrictive rule is beyond every spike's single-source requirement logic.

obligations.title: Allow mandatory/optional flips while an obligation stays in scope
obligations.summary: Settled: an obligation can move between mandatory and optional based on later fulfilments without ever leaving scope.
obligations.quote: An obligation can move between mandatory and optional based on later fulfilments, without leaving scope.
obligations.novel_why: In-scope mandate flips driven by later fulfilments are a mandate behaviour no spike models.

obligations.title: Preserve data across mandatory/optional status flips
obligations.summary: Settled: when an obligation flips between mandatory and optional while still in scope, any data already captured for it is preserved.
obligations.quote: Data is preserved across status flips.
obligations.novel_why: Distinguishing status flips (preserve data) from scope exit (clear data) is a fulfilment-lifecycle rule absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Actively clear data when an obligation exits scope
obligations.summary: Settled: when an obligation goes fully out of scope, its captured data is actively deleted, consistent with the canvas 'delete conditional data' steer.
obligations.quote: When the obligation is fully out of scope, its data is **actively cleared** (consistent with the canvas "delete conditional data" steer).

obligations.title: Model convergent obligations as one obligation activated for several reasons
obligations.summary: Settled: when a field is needed for multiple reasons, there is still exactly one obligation — the scoping function activates it for several reasons. There are never two obligations on the same field.
obligations.quote: There are NOT two obligations on the same field; there's one obligation that the scoping function activates for several reasons.
obligations.novel_why: Convergent multi-reason activation of a single obligation record is a novel de-duplication rule.

obligations.title: Keep convergent obligation data unitary with propagating edits
obligations.summary: Settled: a convergent obligation's data remains a single value — editing it from one presentation context propagates to all other places it appears.
obligations.quote: The data remains unitary — edits from one presentation propagate to others.
obligations.novel_why: Multi-presentation edit propagation over one shared fulfilment is not exercised by any spike.

obligations.title: Support three completion-policy modes for unanswered optional obligations
obligations.summary: Settled: the system must support three completion-policy modes per journey — silently-skipped, must-address, and gate-collected at the end.
obligations.quote: Three modes need supporting per journey: silently-skipped / must-address / gate-collected at end.
obligations.novel_why: The spikes only implement mandatory-field enforcement, not a three-mode completion policy.

obligations.title: Resolve completion policy as per-journey default with per-obligation override
obligations.summary: Settled: the completion policy is configured as a default at journey level, and individual obligations can override it.
obligations.quote: Resolved per-journey default with per-obligation override.
obligations.novel_why: Layered journey-default/obligation-override policy configuration presumes obligation records the spikes lack.

obligations.title: Integrate sub-journeys via a callback returning a single lookup id
obligations.summary: Settled (closes former open question G): a sub-journey integrates via a callback that returns one lookup id. The obligation's canonical data is that id, and the orchestrator dereferences it when the underlying data is needed.
obligations.quote: A sub-journey integrates via a callback that returns a single lookup id. The obligation's canonical data IS the id; the orchestrator dereferences when needed.
obligations.novel_why: Sub-journey integration by receipt-id with orchestrator dereferencing exists in no spike.

obligations.title: Disallow multi-field fan-out from a sub-journey
obligations.summary: Settled: a sub-journey's result never fans out into multiple obligation fields — one sub-journey maps to exactly one obligation.
obligations.quote: There is no multi-field fan-out at the obligation level — one sub-journey = one obligation.
obligations.novel_why: A one-sub-journey-one-obligation invariant is a novel constraint on the new obligations model.

obligations.title: Keep the kind taxonomy open — new kinds are new type values
obligations.summary: Settled: the obligation kind taxonomy is open and extensible. New kinds such as agreement, payment, identity-verification, schedule, or third-party-action are added as new `type` values without changing the obligation's outer shape.
obligations.quote: New kinds (agreement, payment, identity-verification, schedule, third-party-action …) can be added without changing the obligation's outer shape — they appear as new `type` values.
obligations.novel_why: An extensibility guarantee over the obligation envelope has no counterpart in the fixed field vocabularies of the spikes.

obligations.title: Restrict the obligation to declaring canonical data only
obligations.summary: The obligation declares only what data is canonical (id, name, type, cardinality, optional system-handler reference). It must not declare when it is in scope, whether it is mandatory, which pages present it, or how it is presented — those live elsewhere.
obligations.quote: It does **not** declare _when_ it's in scope, _whether_ it's mandatory, _which_ pages present it, or _how_ user-facing obligations are presented
obligations.novel_why: A hard boundary keeping scoping, mandates, page membership and presentation out of the data record is the new model's defining split.

obligations.title: Implement scoping as a function, not a declarative rule language
obligations.summary: A thought experiment established that scoping cannot stay purely declarative (JSON conditions or a rule language like JSON Logic) without significant cost, so scoping must be implemented as a function.
obligations.quote: tested whether scoping could stay purely declarative (JSON conditions or a rule language like JSON Logic). It can't — at least not without significant cost.
obligations.novel_why: This is a settled rejection of spike-c-style declarative rules for the new model's scoping engine.

obligations.title: Support algorithm-shaped scoping rules (address date-range coverage)
obligations.summary: The scoping function must handle algorithm-shaped rules such as 'the union of address date ranges must cover the last 5 years with no gaps', which is interval algebra rather than a query.
obligations.quote: the union of address date ranges must cover the last 5 years with no gaps". This is interval algebra, not a query.
obligations.novel_why: No spike implements interval-algebra completeness rules over an indexed collection.

obligations.title: Support indexed sub-obligation templating projected from state
obligations.summary: The model must support projecting the fulfilment set from state as a template: if the user selects K modifications, a cost fulfilment per item is mandatory, and a professionallyFitted fulfilment per item becomes mandatory only when that item's cost exceeds £500.
obligations.quote: for each selected modification a `cost[item]` fulfilment is mandatory and a `professionallyFitted[item]` becomes mandatory only if cost > £500
obligations.novel_why: Dynamically projecting per-item sub-obligations with value-conditional mandates goes beyond the spikes' static modifications add-on.

obligations.title: Support external state as an input to scoping
obligations.summary: Scoping decisions can depend on external state such as reference-data lookups, historical-policy checks, and fraud-flag signals from upstream services.
obligations.quote: **External state.** Reference-data lookups, historical-policy checks, fraud-flag signals from upstream services.
obligations.novel_why: None of the spikes consume upstream-service state in their journey logic.

obligations.title: Keep scoping synchronous; async lives in the orchestrator
obligations.summary: Settled: the scoping/evaluation function stays synchronous. All asynchronous I/O needed for scoping is owned by the orchestrator, not the evaluator.
obligations.quote: **scoping stays sync; async lives in the orchestrator**
obligations.novel_why: A sync-evaluator/async-orchestrator split is an architectural rule none of the spikes needed.

obligations.title: Deliver external state via the system-handled-obligation callback pattern
obligations.summary: External state reaches the evaluator through the same system-handled-obligation callback pattern used for sub-journey receipts, landing in fulfilments before the evaluator runs.
obligations.quote: The external state arrives via the system-handled-obligation callback pattern, identical to how sub-journey receipts arrive.
obligations.novel_why: Unifying external lookups and sub-journey receipts under one callback fulfilment mechanism is new.

obligations.title: Evaluator declares absent lookups in-scope; orchestrator fires the call
obligations.summary: If the external state a scoping decision needs is not yet in fulfilments, the evaluator declares the corresponding lookup obligation in-scope, and the orchestrator responds by firing the external call.
obligations.quote: the evaluator declares the lookup obligation in-scope and the orchestrator fires the call
obligations.novel_why: Using in-scope lookup obligations as the trigger for orchestrator-side async work is a novel evaluator/orchestrator contract.

obligations.title: Allow an optional "checking…" interim rendering for system-handled obligations
obligations.summary: System-handled obligations are normally not rendered, but the model explicitly permits one exception: a transient "checking…" state may be shown while the orchestrator performs the work. This softens the absolute no-render rule (SHAPE-4) into no-render-or-pending-indicator-only.
obligations.quote: Not rendered (or only rendered as a "checking…" state) — the orchestrator triggers the work and writes the result back to state.
obligations.novel_why: No spike (A/B/C/D) models system-handled obligations at all, so none has a pending/checking interim rendering state for system work.

obligations.title: Use the obligation name as the single string mapping HTML form input → logical Page → Obligation
obligations.summary: Name uniqueness is not just an integrity rule — its purpose is a cross-layer contract: the exact same string must serve as the HTML form input name, the Page-level reference, and the Obligation key, so the layers resolve to each other by string identity.
obligations.quote: uniqueness is what allows the same string to map from HTML form input → logical Page → Obligation
obligations.novel_why: No spike defines a formal three-layer symbol contract; field names exist ad hoc in forms/rules but nothing guarantees the same string spans input, page and model layers.

obligations.title: Persist the sub-journey lookup id as the obligation's canonical data; orchestrator dereferences on demand
obligations.summary: For sub-journey-receipt obligations, the canonical stored fulfilment value is exactly the single lookup id returned by the callback — not the dereferenced sub-journey data. Whenever the underlying data is needed, the orchestrator (not the model or evaluator) performs the dereference.
obligations.quote: The obligation's canonical data IS the id; the orchestrator dereferences when needed.
obligations.novel_why: No spike has sub-journeys or a receipt/lookup-id indirection in its state bag; all spike state stores answer values directly.

obligations.title: Use the obligation name as the template and i18n surface
obligations.summary: Beyond mapping form input to Page to Obligation, the meaningful name is also the key that templates and i18n content bind to — content is keyed off name, not the UUID id.
obligations.quote: a meaningful `name` (the code / template / i18n surface)
obligations.novel_why: No spike (a-d) keys templates or i18n content off an obligation name; grep for i18n across all four spikes returns nothing.

obligations.title: Support value-dependent per-item mandates inside indexed templates
obligations.summary: Within an indexed sub-obligation template, one per-item fulfilment can become mandatory conditionally on the value of a sibling per-item fulfilment (professionallyFitted[item] only when cost[item] exceeds £500) — not just projection of the fulfilment set.
obligations.quote: a `professionallyFitted[item]` becomes mandatory only if cost > £500
obligations.novel_why: Spikes a-d model repeating groups (claims) but none implements a per-item mandate conditioned on another per-item value; claimAmount appears only as inert fixture data.

obligations.title: Build three cooperating evaluation components
obligations.summary: The evaluation engine consists of exactly three components: two pure evaluators covering different layers of the model (Service layer and Flow layer), plus one side-effecting orchestrator that wraps them and handles all I/O.
obligations.quote: The system has **three cooperating components** — two pure evaluators covering different layers of the model, and a side-effecting orchestrator
obligations.novel_why: The four spikes each use a single evaluation paradigm; a layered obligation-evaluator/journey-evaluator/orchestrator split is new.

obligations.title: Implement ObligationEvaluator as pure sync single-call Service-layer function
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator is a pure, synchronous, single-call function that answers Service-layer questions: given Obligations and Fulfilments, what is the state of each obligation. It is Service-scoped and must know nothing about Flows.
obligations.quote: ObligationEvaluator — pure, sync. Answers Service-layer questions: given Obligations and Fulfilments, what's the state of each obligation? Service-scoped; knows nothing about Flows.
obligations.novel_why: Obligations and fulfilments as first-class evaluated records do not exist in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Implement JourneyEvaluator as pure sync Flow-layer evaluator over ObligationEvaluator output
obligations.summary: The JourneyEvaluator is pure and synchronous, answers Flow-layer questions (container status, first applicable page, which page presents an obligation), is Flow-scoped, and takes the ObligationEvaluator's output as one of its inputs.
obligations.quote: JourneyEvaluator — pure, sync. Answers Flow-layer questions ... Flow-scoped; uses ObligationEvaluator output as an input.
obligations.novel_why: A two-tier evaluation where Flow logic consumes a separate obligation-state layer goes beyond the spikes' single-engine designs.

obligations.title: Implement side-effecting orchestrator for system-handled obligations
obligations.summary: The orchestrator is the only side-effecting component. It triggers system-handled obligations such as sub-journey callbacks and external lookups, collects their callbacks, and writes the results into the Fulfilments map.
obligations.quote: Orchestrator — side-effecting. Triggers system-handled obligations (sub-journey callbacks, external lookups); collects callbacks; writes results into Fulfilments
obligations.novel_why: System-handled obligations (lookups, sub-journeys) with callback-driven fulfilment writes do not appear in any spike.

obligations.title: Concentrate all async in the orchestrator
obligations.summary: Both evaluators must remain pure and synchronous; every asynchronous operation is concentrated in the orchestrator, upstream of the evaluators.
obligations.quote: Both evaluators are pure sync — all async is concentrated in the orchestrator, upstream of the evaluators.
obligations.novel_why: The spikes are wholly synchronous with no async orchestration layer to constrain.

obligations.title: Keep both evaluators independently unit-testable with plain fixtures
obligations.summary: Each evaluator must be unit-testable in isolation using plain fixture data in and plain output out, with no I/O or environment setup required.
obligations.quote: Both are independently unit-testable with plain fixture in / plain output out.

obligations.title: Define evaluateObligations(obligations, fulfilments) signature
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator's entry point is a function taking an array of Obligations and a FulfilmentsMap and returning an EvaluationResult. No other inputs exist today.
obligations.quote: function evaluateObligations(obligations: Obligation[], fulfilments: FulfilmentsMap): EvaluationResult
obligations.novel_why: The obligations/fulfilments contract as the evaluator input is unique to this model.

obligations.title: Fulfilments map holds both user inputs and system results
obligations.summary: The Journey's Fulfilments map is a single store containing both user-entered inputs and the results of system-handled obligations, such as lookup results and sub-journey receipts.
obligations.quote: Includes user inputs AND the results of system-handled obligations (lookup results, sub-journey receipts).
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments as first-class records unifying user answers with system-produced values go beyond the spikes' answer stores.

obligations.title: Defer configuration injection as a foreseen extension
obligations.summary: Injecting Configuration into the ObligationEvaluator is explicitly foreseen (see the doc's section I) but deliberately not modelled yet; the current signature stays bare until there is real config to inject.
obligations.quote: Configuration is a foreseen extension point — see §I. Deliberately not modelled today; the signature above is bare because we have no config to inject yet.
obligations.novel_why: A configuration layer feeding obligation evaluation is not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Return amended fulfilments pruned to the current model
obligations.summary: The EvaluationResult includes a fulfilments field: the input FulfilmentsMap amended (pruned) so it fits the current obligations model, returned alongside the per-obligation state.
obligations.quote: fulfilments: FulfilmentsMap,             // amended: pruned to fit the current model
obligations.novel_why: Staleness pruning of stored answers against a changed model is a novel behaviour.

obligations.title: Expose per-obligation inScope boolean
obligations.summary: The result's per-obligation state, keyed by obligation id, carries an inScope boolean saying whether the obligation currently applies.
obligations.quote: [obligationId: string]: { inScope: boolean,
obligations.novel_why: Per-obligation scope flags are part of the new obligations record shape.

obligations.title: Make mandatory/optional status meaningful only when in scope
obligations.summary: Each obligation's state has an optional status of 'mandatory' or 'optional' (a mandate), which is only meaningful when inScope is true.
obligations.quote: status?: 'mandatory' | 'optional',   // only meaningful if inScope
obligations.novel_why: Mandates (mandatory vs optional obligations) are called out as new relative to the spikes.

obligations.title: Shape reasons as coded records with a dotted naming convention
obligations.summary: Each obligation state may carry a reasons array; each reason has a code string following a dotted naming convention such as 'obligation.modification.type.applicable.reason.hasModification' (rationale in section J of the doc).
obligations.quote: code: string,                      // e.g. "obligation.modification.type.applicable.reason.hasModification"
obligations.novel_why: Structured, machine-addressable reason codes extend beyond spike-c's authored reason strings.

obligations.title: Keep reason explanation developer-facing only
obligations.summary: A reason's optional explanation field is freeform text intended for developers and must never be shown to end users.
obligations.quote: explanation?: string,              // freeform, developer-facing; not shown to end users
obligations.novel_why: A dev-facing/user-facing split in reason surfacing is new versus spike-c's single authored reason.

obligations.title: Carry interpolation values on reasons for resolved UI messages
obligations.summary: A reason's optional values object holds interpolation values that a UI layer substitutes into the message resolved from the reason code.
obligations.quote: values?: object,                   // interpolation values for the resolved UI message
obligations.novel_why: Code-plus-interpolation reason rendering is not part of any spike.

obligations.title: Return per-fulfilment state only for indexed cardinality
obligations.summary: An obligation's state includes a fulfilments array of FulfilmentState entries only when the obligation has indexed cardinality (repeating instances); scalar obligations omit it.
obligations.quote: fulfilments?: Array<FulfilmentState> // only for indexed cardinality
obligations.novel_why: Indexed obligations with per-instance fulfilment state are explicitly novel relative to the spikes' add-another loop.

obligations.title: Keep ObligationEvaluator deterministic with zero I/O
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator must never perform I/O and must be deterministic: identical inputs always yield identical output, making it replayable and auditable.
obligations.quote: The ObligationEvaluator never performs I/O. Given the same inputs it always returns the same output.

obligations.title: Author all reasons in the ObligationEvaluator only
obligations.summary: Provenance/reasons are authored solely in the ObligationEvaluator, which is the single source of truth for why an obligation is required; every other component may surface those reasons but must not author its own.
obligations.quote: Provenance / reasons are authored here — the single source of truth for "why is this obligation required?"; other components surface reasons but don't author their own.
obligations.novel_why: Single-source authored-reason surfacing across components is listed as beyond the spikes.

obligations.title: Support amending the obligation model mid-Journey (tolerate-and-amend)
obligations.summary: The obligation model can be amended and a new service version deployed at any time while a Journey is ongoing, so stored fulfilments may predate the current model (renames, removals, cardinality changes) and the system must tolerate that.
obligations.quote: We can amend the obligation model and deploy a new version of the service at any time while a Journey is ongoing.
obligations.novel_why: Mid-journey model versioning and tolerance is explicitly novel territory.

obligations.title: Silently drop fulfilments that map to no current obligation
obligations.summary: When stored fulfilments no longer map to any obligation in the current model, the evaluator silently drops them, passes the rest through, and returns the amended set as part of its output.
obligations.quote: The evaluator silently drops fulfilments that don't map to any current obligation, passes through the rest, and returns the amended set as part of its output.
obligations.novel_why: Stale-fulfilment pruning under model change is a new behaviour beyond the spikes' dependent-answer invalidation.

obligations.title: Persist the amended fulfilment set as the new source of truth
obligations.summary: The orchestrator writes the evaluator's amended fulfilments back to storage; once persisted, the amended set becomes the new source of truth for the Journey.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator persists the amended set — it becomes the new source of truth.
obligations.novel_why: Persisting evaluator-amended state is part of the novel model-versioning story.

obligations.title: Make model-change amendment idempotent, converging in one step
obligations.summary: Re-running the evaluator after the amended fulfilments have been persisted must produce no further changes: the amendment is idempotent and converges in a single step.
obligations.quote: On subsequent runs the amendment is idempotent (converges in one step).
obligations.novel_why: One-step convergence of stale-data amendment is a new correctness property to test.

obligations.title: Expose JourneyEvaluator as individual primitives, not one evaluate() call
obligations.summary: The JourneyEvaluator is a collection of small pure functions, each answering one specific question given the ObligationEvaluator output plus Fulfilments and the Flow definition; callers invoke the specific primitive they need rather than one monolithic evaluate() call.
obligations.quote: Not a single `evaluate()` call — callers invoke the primitive they need.

obligations.title: Implement firstApplicablePage as status-blind depth-first search
obligations.summary: Provide firstApplicablePage(root): a depth-first traversal returning the first Page in declared order, ignoring completion status (status-blind).
obligations.quote: firstApplicablePage(root) — depth-first to first Page in declared order, status-blind.

obligations.title: Implement firstUnfulfilledPage over obligation state
obligations.summary: Provide firstUnfulfilledPage(root, obligationState): a depth-first traversal returning the first Page whose status is Not Started or In Progress.
obligations.quote: firstUnfulfilledPage(root, obligationState) — depth-first to first Page with status Not Started or In Progress.

obligations.title: Implement firstPagePresentingObligation for CYA change links
obligations.summary: Provide firstPagePresentingObligation(flow, obligationName): the primitive that resolves a check-your-answers Change link to the page that presents a given obligation.
obligations.quote: firstPagePresentingObligation(flow, obligationName) — the CYA Change-link primitive.
obligations.novel_why: Resolving change-link targets dynamically by obligation name (dynamic page membership) rather than static page references is new.

obligations.title: Implement containerStatus with four-value propagation
obligations.summary: Provide containerStatus(container, obligationState): returns Not Applicable, Not Started, In Progress, or Fulfilled for a Container according to the doc's status-propagation rules.
obligations.quote: containerStatus(container, obligationState) — returns Not Applicable / Not Started / In Progress / Fulfilled per the propagation rules.

obligations.title: Implement journeyState including the Submitted lifecycle state
obligations.summary: Provide journeyState(flow, obligationState, submitted): returns Not Started, In Progress, Fulfilled, or Submitted for the Journey as a whole, taking an explicit submitted flag (see the doc's Fulfilled-to-Submitted lifecycle section).
obligations.quote: journeyState(flow, obligationState, submitted) — returns Not Started / In Progress / Fulfilled / Submitted for the Journey as a whole.
obligations.novel_why: A whole-journey Submitted state and submit lifecycle are absent from the four spikes.

obligations.title: Follow the common shape for future JourneyEvaluator primitives
obligations.summary: New Flow-layer functions added over time must follow the established shape: a small pure function taking a Flow subtree and obligationState plus any extras, returning one specific answer.
obligations.quote: small pure function; takes (Flow subtree, obligationState) plus any extras; returns a specific answer

obligations.title: Test JourneyEvaluator against arbitrary hand-built obligation output
obligations.summary: The JourneyEvaluator must be testable against any arbitrary ObligationEvaluator output; Flow-logic tests must not need to run the real ObligationEvaluator, which isolates test failures per layer.
obligations.quote: The JourneyEvaluator can be tested against an arbitrary ObligationEvaluator output — you don't need to run the real ObligationEvaluator to test Flow logic.
obligations.novel_why: Layer-isolated testing depends on the novel two-evaluator split.

obligations.title: Render user-facing in-scope obligations and await input
obligations.summary: In the orchestrator loop, each in-scope user-facing obligation is rendered in the journey UI and the loop awaits the user's input for it.
obligations.quote: if user-facing: render in the journey UI; await user input

obligations.title: Trigger system-handled obligations once and write callbacks into fulfilments
obligations.summary: For each in-scope system-handled obligation (sub-journey, lookup, etc.), the orchestrator triggers it only if it is not already in flight, and when its callback arrives it writes the result into the fulfilments map.
obligations.quote: if system-handled (sub-journey / lookup / …): trigger if not already in flight on callback: write result into fulfilments
obligations.novel_why: In-flight deduplication and callback-to-fulfilment writing for system obligations have no spike equivalent.

obligations.title: Yield when blocked and re-evaluate when fulfilments change
obligations.summary: When no progress is possible (waiting on user input or on outstanding callbacks) the orchestrator yields; whenever fulfilments have changed since the last evaluation it loops and re-evaluates.
obligations.quote: if no progress possible (waiting for user OR for callbacks): yield if fulfilments changed since last evaluate: goto loop
obligations.novel_why: A yield/re-evaluate loop reacting to fulfilment changes is new orchestration behaviour.

obligations.title: Terminate the loop when all in-scope obligations are satisfied
obligations.summary: The orchestrator loop's termination condition is that no in-scope obligation remains unsatisfied.
obligations.quote: done when no in-scope obligation remains unsatisfied
obligations.novel_why: Completion defined over obligation satisfaction rather than page/task completion is specific to the obligations model.

obligations.title: Review the orchestrator loop sketch with Sam
obligations.summary: The pseudocode sketch of the orchestrator loop is explicitly marked as needing review with Sam before it is treated as settled.
obligations.quote: TODO review the above sketch with Sam ^^

obligations.title: Run evaluation as a fixed-point computation
obligations.summary: The orchestrator's evaluation is a fixed-point computation: re-evaluate repeatedly until the result is stable, the same pattern as React Suspense, Redux-saga, Elixir GenServer trees, and Bazel's action graph.
obligations.quote: This is a **fixed-point computation** — re-evaluate until the result is stable.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes re-run evaluation to a fixed point; they compute state once per request.

obligations.title: Support property-based testing of the evaluators
obligations.summary: The pure evaluators should be exercised with property-based tests: generate random fulfilment combinations and assert that the result conforms to the expected shape.
obligations.quote: Property-based testing is feasible (generate random fulfilment combinations and assert the result shape).
obligations.novel_why: Property-based testing over random fulfilment inputs is a test approach not used by any spike.

obligations.title: Use fulfilments as the only cache for system-handler results
obligations.summary: System-handler results are cached implicitly by living in the fulfilments map; there must be no separate cache layer with its own freshness policy.
obligations.quote: Caching is implicit. System-handler results live in `fulfilments`. No separate cache layer with its own freshness policy.
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments doubling as the cache for system results is part of the novel fulfilment record design.

obligations.title: Support replaying recorded fulfilments to reconstruct past state
obligations.summary: It must be possible to replay the recorded fulfilments through the evaluators to reconstruct exactly what the user saw at any point in the Journey.
obligations.quote: Replay the recorded fulfilments through the evaluators to reconstruct what the user saw at any point.
obligations.novel_why: Replayable fulfilment history is a capability none of the spikes provide.

obligations.title: Treat lookup failures as unsatisfied obligations
obligations.summary: A failed external lookup is represented simply as an unsatisfied lookup-obligation; the orchestrator handles it with the same patterns as any stuck obligation — retry, surface to the user, or fall back to defaults.
obligations.quote: A failed lookup is just an unsatisfied lookup-obligation. The orchestrator can retry, surface to the user, fall back to defaults
obligations.novel_why: Uniform failure handling via obligation state has no counterpart in the lookup-free spikes.

obligations.title: Record each system-handled satisfaction as an audit event
obligations.summary: Every satisfaction of a system-handled obligation is a recordable audit event (for example 'ran fraud-flag check at 10:03, result clear'), stored alongside user-answer events as a first-class audit trail.
obligations.quote: Each system-handled-obligation satisfaction is a recordable event ("ran fraud-flag check at 10:03, result clear"), alongside user-answer events.
obligations.novel_why: A first-class audit trail of obligation satisfactions is beyond anything in the spikes.

obligations.title: Unify sub-journeys and lookups as one obligation mechanism
obligations.summary: Sub-journeys and external lookups are the same thing: both are orchestrator-handled obligation types whose callback writes a value into state; no separate mechanisms may be built for them.
obligations.quote: Both are orchestrator-handled obligation types with a callback that writes a value into state. The mechanism is unified.
obligations.novel_why: Sub-journeys and lookups do not exist in the spikes, let alone as a unified obligation type.

obligations.title: Bundle static reference data with the obligations file
obligations.summary: Most reference data is bundled statically inside the obligations file so no I/O is needed to use it; lookup obligations are reserved for genuinely dynamic data only.
obligations.quote: Most reference data is bundled with the obligations file (static, no I/O needed). Lookups are reserved for genuinely dynamic data.
obligations.novel_why: An obligations data file carrying bundled reference data is part of the new portable model.

obligations.title: Accept that evaluators are not cross-language portable
obligations.summary: The evaluators themselves are accepted as not portable across languages: a non-JS consumer (for example a Python validator or a native mobile runtime) must write its own ObligationEvaluator and JourneyEvaluator implementations.
obligations.quote: The evaluators are **not portable across languages** in the way the obligations data is. A non-JS consumer ... needs its own ObligationEvaluator + JourneyEvaluator implementations.
obligations.novel_why: Cross-language contract portability with per-runtime evaluator reimplementation is a concern no spike addresses.

obligations.title: Keep the data contract portable; runtimes own when/why and async
obligations.summary: What stays portable across runtimes is the contract: the obligations and flow data files describe what data must exist and how it is presented. Each runtime independently decides when and why, and orchestrates its own async.
obligations.quote: the obligations and flow data files describe what data must exist and how it's presented; each runtime decides when and why, and orchestrates its own async.
obligations.novel_why: A portable data contract with runtime-owned orchestration semantics goes beyond the spikes' single-runtime designs.

obligations.title: Orchestrator handles stuck obligations with uniform recovery patterns
obligations.summary: Beyond treating a failed lookup as an unsatisfied obligation (EVAL-40), the orchestrator must support uniform recovery actions for any stuck obligation: retry, surface the failure to the user, or fall back to defaults — the same patterns regardless of obligation type.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator can retry, surface to the user, fall back to defaults — same patterns as any other obligation that's stuck.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four option spikes (declarative-selectors, statechart, rules-engine, schema-first) describe failure recovery — no retry, user-surfacing, or default-fallback handling appears in any of them.

obligations.title: Record user-answer events alongside system-handled audit events
obligations.summary: The audit trail is not limited to system-handled satisfactions (EVAL-41): user answers are also first-class recordable events, and system-handled events must sit alongside them in the same trail.
obligations.quote: is a recordable event ("ran fraud-flag check at 10:03, result clear"), alongside user-answer events.
obligations.novel_why: No option spike mentions an audit trail or event recording at all; the notion of user answers as recordable events alongside system events appears only in this obligations doc.

obligations.title: Reserve lookup obligations for genuinely dynamic data
obligations.summary: The complement of bundling static reference data (EVAL-43): lookup obligations (with their I/O and orchestrator handling) must only be used for data that is genuinely dynamic — static data must not be modelled as lookups.
obligations.quote: Lookups are reserved for genuinely dynamic data.
obligations.novel_why: The four option spikes do not draw a static-vs-dynamic reference-data boundary or constrain when external lookups are permitted; this design rule is introduced here.

obligations.title: Support single-cardinality obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation with cardinality 'single' produces exactly one canonical value in the fulfilments map (e.g. a dateOfBirth obligation yields fulfilments.dateOfBirth = '1985-03-27').
obligations.quote: A `single` obligation produces one canonical value
obligations.novel_why: Obligations/fulfilments as first-class records with a cardinality field go beyond the four existing spikes.

obligations.title: Support indexed-cardinality obligations
obligations.summary: An obligation with cardinality 'indexed' produces a collection of fulfilments stored as a map keyed by generated fulfilmentIds (e.g. ULID-style keys), each entry holding its own value object.
obligations.quote: An `indexed` obligation produces a collection of fulfilments
obligations.novel_why: Indexed obligations with a fulfilmentId-keyed collection are a new construct not present in any existing spike.

obligations.title: Key stored fulfilments by obligation id, not name
obligations.summary: Real storage keys the fulfilments map by obligation id (UUID), not obligation name; names appear as outer keys only in the doc's examples for readability.
obligations.quote: real storage uses obligation `id` (see §Persistence → Obligation identifiers)
obligations.novel_why: Persistence keyed by stable obligation identifiers is part of the new obligations model, not any existing spike.

obligations.title: Model indexedBy as source × mutability
obligations.summary: The indexedBy shape has two independent dimensions — where index keys come from (source) and who can change them (mutability). Every realistic collection pattern is a combination of these two dimensions.
obligations.quote: Where the index keys come from and who can change them are two independent dimensions; together they describe every realistic pattern.
obligations.novel_why: The source × mutability decomposition of indexed collections is a new data-model design absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Define source=user semantics
obligations.summary: For source 'user', the collection starts empty and the user creates fulfilmentIds through add/remove actions.
obligations.quote: `user` (user creates fulfilmentIds via add/remove; starts empty)
obligations.novel_why: User-sourced fulfilmentId creation is part of the new indexedBy vocabulary, beyond the spikes' ad-hoc add-another loop.

obligations.title: Define source=derived semantics
obligations.summary: For source 'derived', the collection's fulfilmentIds come from another obligation's answer rather than direct user add/remove actions.
obligations.quote: `derived` (fulfilmentIds come from another obligation's answer)
obligations.novel_why: Collections whose membership is derived from another answer (dynamic page membership) do not exist in the four spikes.

obligations.title: Define source=seeded semantics
obligations.summary: For source 'seeded', the initial fulfilmentIds come from an external system at bootstrap; the user may subsequently mutate the collection according to its declared mutability.
obligations.quote: `seeded` (initial fulfilmentIds come from an external system at bootstrap; user may then mutate per `mutability`)
obligations.novel_why: Externally seeded collections (renewal/broker handoff) are a new capability not covered by any spike.

obligations.title: Define mutability=edit-only semantics
obligations.summary: For mutability 'edit-only', the user can edit values within a fixed collection shape but cannot add or remove indices.
obligations.quote: `edit-only` (user can edit values within a fixed collection shape; can't add or remove indices)
obligations.novel_why: A declared mutability contract on collections is part of the new indexedBy model.

obligations.title: Define mutability=edit-add-remove semantics
obligations.summary: For mutability 'edit-add-remove', the user can edit values, add new indices, and remove existing ones.
obligations.quote: `edit-add-remove` (user can edit values, add new indices, remove existing ones)
obligations.novel_why: A declared mutability contract on collections is part of the new indexedBy model.

obligations.title: Defer intermediate mutability values until needed
obligations.summary: The mutability vocabulary is limited to edit-only and edit-add-remove for now; intermediate values such as edit-add or edit-add-remove-reorder are explicitly deferred until a real case arises.
obligations.quote: Extend if intermediate cases (e.g. `edit-add`, `edit-add-remove-reorder`) become real.
obligations.novel_why: An explicitly extensible mutability vocabulary is part of the new indexedBy design.

obligations.title: Support pattern 1: user-driven collections
obligations.summary: User-driven collections such as address history or additional drivers on a new policy are expressed as source 'user' with mutability 'edit-add-remove'.
obligations.quote: **1 — user-driven** | address history, additional drivers (new policy) | `user` | `edit-add-remove`
obligations.novel_why: The spikes have an add-another claims loop, but expressing it as a declared source/mutability combination on an indexed obligation is new.

obligations.title: Support pattern 2: collections derived from another obligation
obligations.summary: Collections derived from another obligation's answer, such as a per-modification cost after a multi-select, are expressed as source 'derived' with mutability 'edit-only'. All shape changes (add/remove of entries) happen by mutating the controlling obligation, never the derived collection directly.
obligations.quote: `derived` | `edit-only` (mutating the controller drives all shape changes)
obligations.novel_why: Per-modification derived collections with controller-driven shape changes are dynamic page membership, absent from all spikes.

obligations.title: Support pattern 3: externally seeded collections
obligations.summary: Externally seeded collections, such as renewal endorsements or a broker-quote handoff, are expressed as source 'seeded' with mutability 'edit-add-remove', so a typical renewal can edit entries, remove old ones, and add new ones.
obligations.quote: **3 — externally seeded** | renewal endorsements; broker-quote handoff | `seeded` | `edit-add-remove` (typical renewal: edit, remove old, add new)
obligations.novel_why: Seeding collection state from an external system at bootstrap is a new capability not in any spike.

obligations.title: Include controllingObligation in derived indexedBy
obligations.summary: A derived indexed obligation's indexedBy object names the controlling obligation whose answer supplies the fulfilmentIds, via a 'controllingObligation' field (e.g. modCost controlled by 'modifications').
obligations.quote: "source": "derived", "controllingObligation": "modifications", "mutability": "edit-only"
obligations.novel_why: An explicit controller reference in the data model is part of the new derived-collection design.

obligations.title: Include seedHandler in seeded indexedBy
obligations.summary: A seeded indexed obligation's indexedBy object names the handler that fetches its initial data, via a 'seedHandler' field (e.g. 'rollover-drivers-v1').
obligations.quote: "source": "seeded", "seedHandler": "rollover-drivers-v1", "mutability": "edit-add-remove"
obligations.novel_why: Named seed handlers referenced from the portable model are a new construct.

obligations.title: Implement seeding via existing orchestrator machinery (settled)
obligations.summary: Settled: the seeded pattern requires no new machinery. Fetching initial fulfilmentIds/data from an external system is structurally identical to a lookup-result or sub-journey-receipt and is handled by the orchestrator.
obligations.quote: Pattern 3 (seeded) doesn't need new machinery: fetching initial fulfilmentIds / data from an external system is structurally identical to a lookup-result or sub-journey-receipt.
obligations.novel_why: Orchestrator-handled seeding of obligation state is new; the spikes have no external bootstrap concept.

obligations.title: Fire seedHandler at journey start or scope entry
obligations.summary: The orchestrator fires a seeded obligation's seedHandler at journey start, or whenever the obligation enters scope; the handler's callback writes the seed values into journey state.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator fires the `seedHandler` at journey start (or whenever the obligation enters scope); the callback writes the seed values into state.
obligations.novel_why: Timing and callback semantics for seeding are new orchestrator behaviour not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Keep the evaluator seeding-agnostic
obligations.summary: After seeding, the evaluator must treat a seeded obligation exactly like any other indexed obligation — it simply sees that fulfilments exist and some are satisfied, with no seeding-specific evaluation logic.
obligations.quote: From the evaluator's perspective the seeded obligation just becomes "fulfilments exist; some are satisfied" the way any other indexed obligation would.
obligations.novel_why: A seeding-agnostic evaluator contract is part of the new obligations engine design.

obligations.title: Add fresh blank fulfilment when controller adds an id (settled)
obligations.summary: Settled derived-lifecycle rule: when the controlling obligation's answer gains a fulfilmentId (e.g. the user adds 'suspension' to a modifications multi-select), a fresh blank fulfilment of the derived obligation comes into scope.
obligations.quote: **FulfilmentId added** (e.g. user adds `suspension` to the modifications selection) → a fresh blank fulfilment of the derived obligation comes into scope.
obligations.novel_why: Automatic scope entry of derived fulfilments driven by a controller answer is new behaviour beyond the spikes' invalidation rules.

obligations.title: Wipe derived fulfilment data when controller removes an id (settled)
obligations.summary: Settled derived-lifecycle rule: when the controlling obligation's answer loses a fulfilmentId (e.g. the user removes 'alloys'), the corresponding derived fulfilment drops out of scope and its data is wiped, consistent with the existing scope-exit rule.
obligations.quote: **FulfilmentId removed** (e.g. user removes `alloys`) → that fulfilment drops out of scope; its data is wiped, consistent with the existing scope-exit rule.
obligations.novel_why: Per-fulfilment scope-exit wiping inside a derived collection extends the spikes' whole-answer invalidation to indexed entries.

obligations.title: No rehydration on re-added fulfilmentId (settled)
obligations.summary: Settled derived-lifecycle rule: if a fulfilmentId is re-added after being removed, the derived fulfilment starts fresh and blank — its previous data is not rehydrated. This matches the 'delete conditional data' steer and the yes→no→yes round-trip already specified in the E2E suite.
obligations.quote: **FulfilmentId re-added** after a previous remove → fresh blank again (no rehydration), consistent with the canvas "delete conditional data" steer
obligations.novel_why: The spikes cover yes→no→yes for single answers; applying no-rehydration to individual entries of a derived indexed collection is new.

obligations.title: Store fulfilments in a journey-scoped map
obligations.summary: Fulfilments are stored in a map scoped to a single journey. The journey — not the user or session — is the uniqueness scope for fulfilment storage.
obligations.quote: Fulfilments live in a **journey-scoped map**. The journey is the uniqueness scope (not the user, not the session
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments as a first-class journey-scoped storage structure is not present in any of the four existing spikes.

obligations.title: Support multiple simultaneous journeys per user
obligations.summary: A single user may have several journeys open at once (for example in different browser tabs), so fulfilment storage must not collide across journeys belonging to the same user or session.
obligations.quote: a single user might have several journeys open simultaneously, e.g. in different browser tabs
obligations.novel_why: The existing spikes assume one session-backed answers object; concurrent per-user journeys are a new constraint.

obligations.title: Key the fulfilments map by obligation id
obligations.summary: Within a journey, the fulfilments map is keyed by the obligation's id (the identifier defined in the Persistence / Obligation identifiers section), not by name.
obligations.quote: the **fulfilments map** is keyed by obligation `id` (see §Persistence → Obligation identifiers)
obligations.novel_why: Keying stored answers by obligation id is part of the new obligations/fulfilments model absent from the spikes.
