obligations.title: Map entries are single value or nested fulfilmentId map
obligations.summary: Each fulfilments-map entry is either a single value for a single-cardinality obligation, or a nested map from fulfilmentId to value for an indexed obligation.
obligations.quote: Each entry is either a single value (for single-cardinality obligations) or a nested map of fulfilmentId → value (for indexed obligations)
obligations.novel_why: The indexed-obligation nested-map storage shape goes beyond the flat answer stores in the spikes.

obligations.title: Real storage uses id even though examples show name
obligations.summary: The document's examples use obligation name values as outer keys only for readability; the implementation must use obligation id as the actual storage key.
obligations.quote: Examples in this section show obligation `name` values as outer keys for readability; real storage uses `id`.
obligations.novel_why: The name-vs-id distinction is specific to the new obligation-identifier scheme.

obligations.title: Single-cardinality: fulfilmentId equals obligationId
obligations.summary: A single-cardinality obligation needs no separate per-instance identifier: by convention fulfilmentId === obligationId, and the obligation id alone addresses its at-most-one fulfilment.
obligations.quote: `fulfilmentId === obligationId` by convention, and the obligation `id` alone addresses the (at most one) fulfilment
obligations.novel_why: The fulfilmentId addressing convention is part of the new fulfilments model.

obligations.title: Indexed fulfilmentId shape varies by source
obligations.summary: For indexed obligations, the shape of the inner map's per-fulfilment identifiers depends on the obligation's source (user, derived, or seeded).
obligations.quote: For indexed obligations, the inner map's keys are per-fulfilment identifiers whose shape varies by `source`.
obligations.novel_why: Source-dependent fulfilment-key shapes are unique to the indexed-obligations design.

obligations.title: user-source fulfilments get orchestrator-generated opaque ids
obligations.summary: For indexed obligations with source = user, the orchestrator generates an opaque id (such as a ULID or UUID) each time the user adds a fulfilment; those ids serve as the inner-map keys.
obligations.quote: opaque ids generated by the orchestrator each time the user adds a fulfilment (e.g. ULID/UUID)
obligations.novel_why: Orchestrator-minted opaque per-instance ids replace the spikes' hacked array indexing in the claims loop.

obligations.title: Opaque ids must survive reorder, delete and reload
obligations.summary: The orchestrator-generated fulfilment ids are meaningless to the user but must remain stable identifiers across reordering, deletion of siblings, and page/journey reload.
obligations.quote: The keys are meaningless to the user but stable for the orchestrator. Survive reorder, delete, reload.
obligations.novel_why: Stability guarantees for per-fulfilment ids are a new requirement beyond the spikes' array-based claims loop.

obligations.title: derived-source fulfilments keyed by controller answer values
obligations.summary: For indexed obligations with source = derived, the inner-map keys are the controlling obligation's answer values themselves (semantic, meaningful keys), e.g. modificationCost keyed by "turbo" and "alloys".
obligations.quote: the controlling obligation's answer values themselves are the keys; meaningful and semantic
obligations.novel_why: Deriving fulfilment keys from a controller obligation's values is part of the novel seeded/derived model.

obligations.title: Adding a controller value spawns a blank derived fulfilment
obligations.summary: When a new value is added to the controlling obligation (e.g. "suspension" added to modifications), the engine must create a fresh blank fulfilment for the derived indexed obligation keyed by that value.
obligations.quote: Adding `"suspension"` to the controller spawns a fresh blank `modificationCost["suspension"]` fulfilment
obligations.novel_why: Automatic spawning of derived fulfilment instances from controller changes goes beyond the spikes' invalidation behaviour.

obligations.title: Removing a controller value drops and wipes its derived fulfilment
obligations.summary: When a value is removed from the controlling obligation (e.g. "alloys" removed), the corresponding derived fulfilment must be dropped and its stored data wiped, per the derived-lifecycle rule defined in the Lifecycle section.
obligations.quote: removing `"alloys"` drops `modificationCost["alloys"]` and wipes its data (the lifecycle rule from §Lifecycle for derived)
obligations.novel_why: Deterministic drop-and-wipe of derived fulfilment instances is a new lifecycle rule for the fulfilments model.

obligations.title: seeded-source fulfilments keyed by external-source ids
obligations.summary: For indexed obligations with source = seeded, the fulfilmentIds come from the external source that seeded them — database ids, policy reference numbers, or domain codes (e.g. "POL-2024-DRV-001").
obligations.quote: fulfilmentIds come from the external source (DB ids, policy reference numbers, domain codes)
obligations.novel_why: Seeded fulfilments with externally-provided ids are explicitly part of the novel seeded-vs-derived design.

obligations.title: Allow heterogeneous id map when seeded obligation is user-mutable
obligations.summary: When a seeded obligation also allows user mutation, its inner map is heterogeneous by design: seeded fulfilments keep their source-provided ids while user-added fulfilments get orchestrator-generated opaque ids in the same map.
obligations.quote: the map is **heterogeneous** by design — seeded fulfilments keep their source- provided ids; user-added fulfilments get orchestrator-generated opaque ids
obligations.novel_why: Mixed seeded/user-added fulfilments in one map is a new capability none of the spikes model.

obligations.title: Model file need not know about id-origin mixing
obligations.summary: Each fulfilment's id reflects its own origin (seeded vs user-added), and nothing in the authored model file needs to be aware that a seeded obligation's map contains mixed id styles.
obligations.quote: Each fulfilment's id reflects its origin; nothing in the model file needs to know about the mixing.
obligations.novel_why: Keeping the portable model file agnostic of runtime id provenance is a new separation-of-concerns rule.

obligations.title: Support within-fulfilment activation logic
obligations.summary: Within a single fulfilment of an indexed obligation, the same activation (conditional applicability) logic available to single obligations must be supported.
obligations.quote: Within a fulfilment you can have the same activation logic as single obligations.
obligations.novel_why: Per-instance activation inside indexed fulfilments extends conditionality beyond the spikes' page-level reveals.

obligations.title: Support cross-fulfilment quantifier rules via scoping functions
obligations.summary: Rules that quantify across fulfilments (e.g. "any address with country not UK") must be expressible; this is part of why scoping must be implemented as a function, as detailed in the evaluation-engine section.
obligations.quote: Across fulfilments you can have quantifier-shaped rules ("any address with country ≠ UK") that are part of why scoping needs to be a function
obligations.novel_why: Quantifier-shaped cross-instance rules and function-based scoping are beyond the per-answer predicates in the spikes.

obligations.title: Map claims loop and addons subtasks onto indexedBy
obligations.summary: The existing prototype's claims loop (user-driven, via arrayKey 'claims' plus done 'claimsDone') and addons subtasks (derived from the addons multi-select) are ungeneralised precedents; both should map cleanly onto the new indexedBy shape (patterns 1 and 2 respectively).
obligations.quote: Neither is generalised; both would map cleanly onto this `indexedBy` shape.
obligations.novel_why: Generalising the hacked-in claims/addons mechanisms into a declared indexedBy construct is the new model's job.

obligations.title: Staleness applies to all answers, not just lookups
obligations.summary: Both user-entered and system-handled answers can go stale — for example an old manually-entered phone number, a changed address, or an NCD count needing refresh at renewal — so staleness is not a lookup-only concern.
obligations.quote: Answers — both user-entered and system-handled — can go stale. This is **not** a lookup-only concern
obligations.novel_why: Staleness of stored answers is not addressed by any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Make the staleness mechanism uniform across answer types
obligations.summary: Whatever form the staleness mechanism takes (TTL, age-based activation predicate, per-obligation freshness policy), it must apply equally to user-entered answers (typed values, file uploads, selections) and system-handled results (sub-journey receipts, lookup results, dereferenced data).
obligations.quote: the staleness mechanism needs to be **uniform across all answer types**
obligations.novel_why: A uniform freshness mechanism spanning user and system answers is entirely new relative to the spikes.

obligations.title: Staleness shape: per-obligation freshness rule (open)
obligations.summary: The likely but unsettled design is that each obligation can declare a freshness rule; when a stored answer fails it, the obligation re-enters the needs-satisfying state and the orchestrator re-fires system-handled types or re-presents user-facing types. The doc explicitly marks this as an open question.
obligations.quote: The likely shape: each obligation can declare a freshness rule; when the stored answer fails the rule, the obligation re-enters the "needs satisfying" state.
obligations.novel_why: Freshness-driven re-entry into a needs-satisfying state, with re-fire/re-present orchestration, is a novel staleness design.

obligations.title: Stale answer re-enters needs-satisfying; orchestrator re-fires or re-presents by type
obligations.summary: When a stored answer fails its freshness rule the obligation transitions back to the needs-satisfying state, and the orchestrator responds per type: re-fires system-handled obligations, re-presents user-facing ones.
obligations.quote: the obligation re-enters the "needs satisfying" state. The orchestrator then either re-fires (for system-handled types) or re-presents (for user-facing types)
obligations.novel_why: No existing spike has a staleness-driven state transition or a type-differentiated orchestrator response (re-fire vs re-present); the prototype's claims/addons patterns have no freshness handling at all.

obligations.title: Persist only fulfilments plus minimal metadata
obligations.summary: The persisted Journey document contains only the Fulfilments map and a minimal metadata envelope. No other per-Journey data is stored.
obligations.quote: Only the Fulfilments map, plus a minimal metadata envelope.
obligations.novel_why: Treats fulfilments as the sole first-class persisted records, a concept absent from the four existing spikes.

obligations.title: Recompute all derived state on load
obligations.summary: All derived state (per-obligation status, per-Container status, navigation outputs, Journey state) is never persisted; it is recomputed on load by running the evaluators over stored fulfilments and the current model.
obligations.quote: All derived state — per-obligation status, per-Container status, navigation-primitive outputs, Journey state — is recomputed on load
obligations.novel_why: Defines a seeded-vs-derived persistence split driven by the obligation evaluators, not present in the spikes.

obligations.title: Keep evaluators pure and synchronous
obligations.summary: Both evaluators must remain pure synchronous functions so that recomputing derived state on every load stays cheap; this is what justifies not persisting derived state.
obligations.quote: Recomputing is cheap (both evaluators are pure sync)

obligations.title: Define the Journey document shape
obligations.summary: The persisted Journey document has the fields journeyId, flowId, status, createdAt, updatedAt, submittedAt (nullable), and a fulfilments map. No other fields exist in the prototype.
obligations.quote: "journeyId": "…uuid…", // opaque; generated at start
obligations.novel_why: Specifies a concrete persisted-document schema keyed by obligation fulfilments, beyond the in-memory session state the spikes use.

obligations.title: Generate journeyId as opaque UUID at start
obligations.summary: Each Journey gets an opaque UUID journeyId generated when the Journey starts.
obligations.quote: "journeyId": "…uuid…", // opaque; generated at start
obligations.novel_why: Introduces a persistent Journey identity lifecycle not modelled in the existing spikes.

obligations.title: Record flowId on the Journey document
obligations.summary: The Journey document stores a single flowId value identifying which Flow the Journey uses.
obligations.quote: "flowId": "polished-car-insurance", // which Flow this Journey uses
obligations.novel_why: Journey-to-Flow binding via a persisted flowId is new relative to the spikes' single hardcoded journey.

obligations.title: Model Journey status as in-progress or submitted
obligations.summary: The Journey document's status field takes exactly two values: 'in-progress' or 'submitted'. submittedAt is null until submission.
obligations.quote: "status": "in-progress" | "submitted",
obligations.novel_why: A persisted submit lifecycle (status + submittedAt) goes beyond the spikes, which have no submit state.

obligations.title: Key fulfilments by obligation id
obligations.summary: The fulfilments map in the Journey document is keyed by obligation id (the stable UUID), not by name or page.
obligations.quote: keyed by obligation id — see §Obligation identifiers
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments-as-records keyed by obligation UUIDs is a core novel concept of the obligations model.

obligations.title: Omit serviceId from the Journey document
obligations.summary: The Journey document carries no serviceId, because a runtime knows about exactly one Service (its own), making the field meaningless.
obligations.quote: No `serviceId` — a runtime knows about exactly one Service (its own)
obligations.novel_why: A settled decision about the novel Journey-document schema, absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Defer userId user-association field
obligations.summary: A userId field to pin Journey ownership is a forward extension for when authentication lands; it is explicitly not needed for the prototype.
obligations.quote: **User association** — when auth lands, a `userId` field pins Journey ownership.
obligations.novel_why: Concerns extension of the novel persisted Journey document.

obligations.title: Defer additional date metadata
obligations.summary: Additional timestamps for staleness, audit, or lifecycle policies are a deferred forward extension, not part of the prototype's Journey document.
obligations.quote: **Date metadata** — additional timestamps if we need them for staleness (§Staleness), audit, or lifecycle policies.
obligations.novel_why: Ties into the novel staleness concept and the novel persisted document.

obligations.title: Keep operational storage concerns out of the model layer
obligations.summary: Database choice, encryption, retention policy, and backup approach are operational concerns and must stay outside the model layer.
obligations.quote: The database, encryption, retention policy, and backup approach are operational concerns outside the model layer.

obligations.title: Version models by code deploy with no version stamping
obligations.summary: The Obligations and Flow models are versioned only by code deploy. Persisted Journeys carry no model version stamp, and evaluators always run against the current model (latest wins).
obligations.quote: **There is no version stamping on the persisted Journey.** The evaluators always run against the current model.
obligations.novel_why: Model versioning strategy for persisted journeys is a new concern not covered by the four spikes.

obligations.title: Tolerate mid-journey model updates
obligations.summary: Model updates can ship between two save-and-continue interactions of the same user within a minute, so the runtime must behave correctly when the model changes mid-journey.
obligations.quote: updates ship whenever the application deploys, which might happen between two "save and continue" interactions of the same user
obligations.novel_why: Mid-journey model-change tolerance is a cross-Flow/versioning concern beyond the static-model spikes.

obligations.title: Silently drop ill-fitting fulfilments
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator must silently drop stored fulfilments whose obligation no longer exists, or whose shape has changed so the stored value no longer fits.
obligations.quote: Silently drops fulfilments for obligations that no longer exist (or whose shape has changed such that the stored value doesn't fit).
obligations.novel_why: Evaluator-level tolerance of stale fulfilments is a novel behaviour of the obligations engine.

obligations.title: Pass through cleanly mapping fulfilments
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator passes through unchanged any stored fulfilments that still map cleanly onto current obligations.
obligations.quote: Passes through fulfilments that still map cleanly.
obligations.novel_why: Part of the novel fulfilment-reconciliation behaviour of the ObligationEvaluator.

obligations.title: Return the amended fulfilment set from the evaluator
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator returns the amended fulfilment set (after drops and pass-throughs) as part of its output.
obligations.quote: Returns the amended set as part of its output.
obligations.novel_why: Evaluator output contract including an amended fulfilment set is unique to this model.

obligations.title: Persist the amended set as new source of truth
obligations.summary: The orchestrator persists the evaluator's amended fulfilment set back to storage, making it the new source of truth for the Journey.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator persists the amended set — it becomes the new source of truth.
obligations.novel_why: Orchestrator write-back of evaluator-amended fulfilments is a novel persistence rule.

obligations.title: Accept silent drop with no drop UX for MVP
obligations.summary: For the MVP, dropping fulfilments is deliberately silent; any user-facing UX explaining that some answers are no longer needed is deferred until concrete cases demand it.
obligations.quote: **Silent drop is deliberate for MVP.** Any UX for "we've updated our requirements; some of your answers are no longer needed" is deferred
obligations.novel_why: Settled MVP posture on a novel fulfilment-drop mechanism.

obligations.title: Log fulfilment drops server-side
obligations.summary: When the evaluator drops fulfilments, the drops should be logged server-side to provide an audit trail.
obligations.quote: **Log drops server-side** so we have an audit trail.
obligations.novel_why: Audit logging requirement for the novel drop behaviour.

obligations.title: Defer user notification of dropped answers
obligations.summary: Notifying users that model changes affected their in-flight journey is deferred; it is a possible extension for services where model changes materially matter.
obligations.quote: **User notification** — deferred; a possible extension for services where model changes materially affect in-flight journeys.
obligations.novel_why: Deferred question about the novel drop mechanism.

obligations.title: Defer per-obligation migration functions
obligations.summary: Per-obligation migration for renames with semantic continuity is deferred. If needed, the likely shape is an optional migrate(old) → new hook per obligation, applied inside the ObligationEvaluator before scope evaluation.
obligations.quote: the likely shape is a per-obligation optional `migrate(old) → new` hook, applied inside the ObligationEvaluator before scope evaluation.
obligations.novel_why: Migration hooks inside the ObligationEvaluator are a novel deferred extension.

obligations.title: Give every obligation both id and name
obligations.summary: Every obligation carries two identifiers: a stable UUID id that never changes and a meaningful name that may change.
obligations.quote: Every obligation carries two identifiers
obligations.novel_why: Dual identifiers on first-class obligation records are unique to the obligations model.

obligations.title: Use id as the stable persistence key
obligations.summary: The obligation id is an opaque UUID used as the persistence key for fulfilments in storage, and it stays stable across all renames of the meaningful name.
obligations.quote: **`id`** is the persistence key — an opaque UUID. Fulfilments in storage are keyed by `id`.
obligations.novel_why: UUID-keyed fulfilment persistence is a novel obligations-model decision.

obligations.title: Use name as the developer-facing surface
obligations.summary: The obligation name is the surface used in code, i18n, templates, and tests — everywhere developers work with obligations — and it may be renamed between deployments.
obligations.quote: **`name`** is the code / i18n / template / test surface. Everywhere developers work with obligations.
obligations.novel_why: Defines the role of the name identifier on novel obligation records.

obligations.title: Enforce name uniqueness within the Obligations model
obligations.summary: Obligation names must be unique within the Obligations model; uniqueness is what allows the same string to map from HTML form input to logical Page to Obligation.
obligations.quote: Unique within the Obligations model — uniqueness is what allows the same string to map from HTML form input → logical Page → Obligation.
obligations.novel_why: A uniqueness invariant on obligation names, a construct absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Support cosmetic renames without data migration
obligations.summary: Renaming an obligation's name (for example dateOfBirth to dob) while leaving id alone must be safe: persisted fulfilments are untouched, code uses the new name, and users see no disruption.
obligations.quote: **Cosmetic renames are safe.** Rename `dateOfBirth → dob` in the model: change `name`, leave `id` alone.
obligations.novel_why: Rename-safety guarantee derives from the novel dual-identifier scheme.

obligations.title: Treat semantic changes as genuine model changes
obligations.summary: If an obligation's meaning changes (validation, scope, cardinality, or source × mutability), no identifier scheme helps; such changes need migration or fall back to silent drop.
obligations.quote: **Semantic changes still need migration** or fall back to silent drop.
obligations.novel_why: Names a specific tricky scenario in the novel versioning/drop machinery.

obligations.title: Generate UUIDs via tooling, not by hand
obligations.summary: Tooling generates obligation UUIDs so authors never type them by hand; the cost is one extra line per obligation in the JSON model file.
obligations.quote: **Author ergonomics.** Tooling generates UUIDs; authors don't type them by hand.
obligations.novel_why: Authoring tooling for obligation UUIDs is a novel obligations-model concern.

obligations.title: Adopt UUID ids up-front, not retrofit
obligations.summary: UUID ids on obligations must be adopted from the start: adding them later would require generating ids for all obligations and migrating all persisted fulfilments to key by id, whereas up-front cost is one JSON field.
obligations.quote: **Adoption is up-front, not retrofit.** Adding UUIDs later would require generating them for all obligations and migrating all persisted fulfilments
obligations.novel_why: Sequencing constraint specific to the novel dual-identifier persistence scheme.

obligations.title: Use meaningful ids only in the Flow model
obligations.summary: The Flow model does not use dual identifiers; Container ids (Sections, SubSections, Pages) and the Flow-level flowId are meaningful identifiers only, because they do not participate in the persistence-key concern.
obligations.quote: The Flow model does not use dual identifiers.
obligations.novel_why: Settled asymmetry between Flow and Obligations identifier schemes is unique to this model.

obligations.title: Keep Container ids out of persisted Journey state
obligations.summary: Container ids must not appear in persisted Journey state: fulfilments are keyed by obligation, not by Page, and no 'current page' pointer is persisted because navigation state is derived on resume via firstUnfulfilledPage.
obligations.quote: Navigation state is derived on resume via `firstUnfulfilledPage`, so no "current page" pointer is persisted either.
obligations.novel_why: Derived-on-resume navigation with no persisted page pointer is a novel persistence rule.

obligations.title: Update Flow presents references atomically with renames
obligations.summary: Flow presents: [obligationName] references are model-to-model links resolved at authoring time; when an obligation's name is renamed, the Flow's presents array must be updated in the same commit.
obligations.quote: if an obligation's `name` is renamed, the Flow's `presents` array updates in the same commit.
obligations.novel_why: Cross-model reference-integrity rule between Flow and Obligations models.

obligations.title: Treat Container id renames as code-only changes
obligations.summary: Renaming a Container id is a purely internal code change, like renaming any identifier, requiring no user-data migration.
obligations.quote: Renaming a Container id is a purely-internal code change — like renaming any identifier — with no user-data migration.
obligations.novel_why: Consequence of the novel decision to exclude Container ids from persisted state.

obligations.title: Flag URL-embedded Container ids as unsolved
obligations.summary: If URLs like /journey/:journeyId/section/:sectionId/page/:pageId are exposed to users, renaming a Container id breaks bookmarks. This is explicitly not solved in the model layer; it would be solvable at the routing layer via slug lookup with historical aliases or hash-based routing regenerated on load.
obligations.quote: **URLs may embed Container ids.** If we build `/journey/:journeyId/section/:sectionId/page/:pageId` URLs and expose them to users
obligations.novel_why: Bookmark-stability caveat arising from the novel identifier decisions.

obligations.title: Defer Flow rename handling
obligations.summary: Because the Journey document stores a single flowId, renaming a Flow would break resumed journeys' ability to find their Flow. Possible solutions (alias table, dual identifiers at Flow level, version-stamped Flows) are deferred because Flow renames are rare.
obligations.quote: If we rename a Flow, resumed journeys can't find their Flow. Solutions if it ever becomes needed: alias table, dual identifiers at Flow level, or version-stamped Flows.
obligations.novel_why: Deferred question about the novel persisted flowId binding.

obligations.title: Derive resume position via firstUnfulfilledPage; persist no current-page pointer
obligations.summary: The Journey document must not store a current-page pointer; on resume, the runtime derives the user's position by running the firstUnfulfilledPage navigation primitive over the stored fulfilments and current model.
obligations.quote: Navigation state is derived on resume via `firstUnfulfilledPage`, so no "current page" pointer is persisted either.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spike docs (options A-D) mention resume behaviour, a current-page pointer, or firstUnfulfilledPage; the pointer-free resume mechanism is introduced only in this persistence section.

obligations.title: Record createdAt/updatedAt/submittedAt lifecycle timestamps, submittedAt null until submission
obligations.summary: The Journey metadata envelope carries createdAt, updatedAt and submittedAt timestamps; submittedAt is explicitly nullable, holding null while the Journey is in-progress and a value only once submitted. No existing item names these base timestamp fields — PERSIST-7 covers only the status enum on the same lines, and PERSIST-11 defers only *additional* date metadata beyond these three.
obligations.quote: "createdAt": "…", "updatedAt": "…", "submittedAt": "…" | null,
obligations.novel_why: Standard document lifecycle timestamps consistent with the persistence-minimalism posture already established across the spikes; not a new mechanism.

obligations.title: Decide which datastore backs the Journey document
obligations.summary: The datastore backing the persisted Journey document is undecided. MongoDB (the workspace default) may not be viable; Redis or another store may be needed. The decision is deferred but flagged urgent because the persistence layer cannot start until it is resolved.
obligations.quote: Which datastore backs the Journey document is **not yet decided**. MongoDB — the workspace's default — may not be viable for this data
obligations.novel_why: The existing spikes have no persistence layer at all, so the datastore question does not arise in them.

obligations.title: Treat the datastore choice as the upstream constraint on all persistence design
obligations.summary: The datastore choice materially constrains schema style, query capability (including secondary indexes such as finding all Journeys for a user), concurrency semantics, retention operations, and backup/disaster-recovery. Every other deferred persistence concern is downstream of it, so none of them can be settled first.
obligations.quote: Every other deferred persistence concern below is downstream of this decision.
obligations.novel_why: Sequencing persistence decisions behind a datastore choice is new territory beyond the storage-free spikes.

obligations.title: Seek decision-maker input and Defra-wide data-store guidance
obligations.summary: Resolving the datastore choice needs input from decision-makers and possibly Defra-wide guidance on data-store standards for services of this classification; it is not a decision the model design can make alone.
obligations.quote: Needs decision-maker input and possibly Defra-wide guidance on data-store standards for services of this classification.
obligations.novel_why: Escalating to organisational data-store standards is outside anything the four spikes cover.

obligations.title: Transition Journey from Fulfilled to Submitted on CYA Submit
obligations.summary: When the user clicks Submit on the check-your-answers page, the Journey's state transitions from Fulfilled to Submitted. This is the trigger point for the submit lifecycle.
obligations.quote: When the user clicks Submit on the CYA page, the Journey transitions from Fulfilled to Submitted.
obligations.novel_why: The spikes end at a quote summary; a formal Fulfilled-to-Submitted journey-state transition is part of the new submit lifecycle.

obligations.title: Flip persisted document status to submitted on Submit
obligations.summary: At the persisted-document level, Submit flips the document's status field from in-progress to submitted.
obligations.quote: `status` flips from `in-progress` to `submitted`.
obligations.novel_why: A persisted document status field is part of the new persistence/submit lifecycle absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Set submittedAt timestamp on Submit
obligations.summary: Submit sets a submittedAt timestamp on the persisted Journey document.
obligations.quote: `submittedAt` timestamp is set.
obligations.novel_why: Recording a submit timestamp on a persisted document is new submit-lifecycle behaviour.

obligations.title: Block writes to submitted documents at the storage layer
obligations.summary: After Submit, fulfilments become immutable. The storage layer must reject any write to a Journey document whose status is submitted.
obligations.quote: **Fulfilments become immutable.** Storage layer must block writes to documents with `status: "submitted"`.
obligations.novel_why: Storage-layer immutability of fulfilments is a first-class-record concern beyond the spikes.

obligations.title: Serve a read-only CYA page as the only post-submit surface
obligations.summary: After submission, the only user-facing page is a read-only check-your-answers page. No other Journey page is accessible to the user post-submit.
obligations.quote: The only post-submit user-facing surface is a **read-only CYA page**. The user cannot navigate elsewhere in the Journey.
obligations.novel_why: Read-only pages and post-submit route constraint are explicitly flagged as novel; the spikes have no submitted state.

obligations.title: Constrain routes from journeyState = Submitted in renderers
obligations.summary: Renderers must observe that journeyState is Submitted and constrain routing accordingly, enforcing the read-only-CYA-only rule rather than relying on ad-hoc checks.
obligations.quote: Renderers observe `journeyState = Submitted` and constrain routes accordingly.
obligations.novel_why: Deriving route constraints from a journey-level Submitted state is engine behaviour none of the four spikes implements.

obligations.title: Exclude post-submit extension points from the core MVP
obligations.summary: Everything beyond the minimal submit behaviour is a possible extension point and is explicitly not part of the core MVP. Implementations must not build these features speculatively.
obligations.quote: **Everything else is a possible extension point** — not part of the core MVP
obligations.novel_why: An explicit MVP boundary on submit lifecycle scope is new to this design; the spikes never reached submit.

obligations.title: Defer post-submit navigation to individual pages
obligations.summary: Allowing users to re-view individual Journey pages beyond the read-only CYA after submission is a deferred extension point, not MVP behaviour.
obligations.quote: **Post-submit navigation.** Re-viewing individual pages beyond CYA.
obligations.novel_why: Post-submit page viewing presupposes a submitted lifecycle the spikes do not have.

obligations.title: Defer downloads (PDF receipt/confirmation)
obligations.summary: Providing a downloadable PDF receipt or confirmation document after submit is a deferred extension point, not MVP behaviour.
obligations.quote: **Downloads.** PDF receipt or confirmation document.
obligations.novel_why: Post-submit artefact downloads are beyond the quote-summary endpoint of the spikes.

obligations.title: Defer amendment workflow; sketch is new Journey referencing the immutable original
obligations.summary: Post-submit corrections are deferred. If adopted, the typical shape is a new Journey holding a reference back to the original, with the original staying immutable; the model provides no amendment machinery today.
obligations.quote: Typical shape: a new Journey with a reference back to the original; the original stays immutable. Model doesn't provide amendment machinery today.
obligations.novel_why: Amendment-by-new-Journey with immutable originals is a lifecycle/versioning concept absent from all four spikes.

obligations.title: Defer withdrawal workflow (Withdrawn state)
obligations.summary: A pre-processing withdrawal that flips the Journey status to Withdrawn is deferred; Withdrawn is not currently part of the state taxonomy.
obligations.quote: **Withdrawal workflow.** Pre-processing withdraw (status flip to Withdrawn). Not currently in the state taxonomy.
obligations.novel_why: A journey-level state taxonomy with a potential Withdrawn state goes beyond the spikes' task-status model.

obligations.title: Defer downstream Submit events; orchestrator may fire a well-defined event
obligations.summary: Notifying external systems on submission (case management such as Dynamics, confirmation email/SMS, audit log, metrics) is deferred. The intended mechanism is that the orchestrator fires a well-defined event on Submit; the payload, subscription mechanism and delivery guarantees are service and infrastructure concerns.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator can fire a well-defined event on Submit; exact payload, subscription mechanism, and delivery guarantees are service and infrastructure concerns.
obligations.novel_why: An orchestrator emitting submit events to downstream systems is new architecture beyond the browser-only spikes.

obligations.title: Defer reference-number generation at Submit
obligations.summary: Generating a reference number at Submit is deferred. The sketched shape is a service-provided function called at Submit, with the result stored on the Journey document and shown on the read-only CYA; the format is service-specific.
obligations.quote: service-provided function called at Submit; stored on the Journey document; shown on the read-only CYA. Format is service-specific.
obligations.novel_why: Service-hook reference numbers stored on a persisted Journey document have no counterpart in the spikes.

obligations.title: Defer Submit atomicity, coupled to the datastore decision
obligations.summary: How to handle partial failure across downstream events at Submit (fail-fast, best-effort, or two-phase) is deferred. The choice interacts with the datastore's transaction capabilities, so it is deferred together with the datastore decision.
obligations.quote: **Submit atomicity.** How to handle partial failure across downstream events (fail-fast / best-effort / two-phase). Choice interacts with the datastore
obligations.novel_why: Atomicity across persisted state and downstream events is a persistence concern the storage-free spikes never face.

obligations.title: Limit MVP Submit to state flip, timestamp, immutability, read-only CYA
obligations.summary: The MVP Submit consists of exactly four things: the state flip to submitted, the submittedAt timestamp, fulfilment immutability, and the read-only CYA page. Extension points are adopted only when a concrete service needs them, and nothing more is built.
obligations.quote: The MVP Submit is: state flip, timestamp, immutability, read-only CYA. Nothing more.
obligations.novel_why: A defined minimal submit lifecycle is entirely new relative to the four spikes.

obligations.title: Defer concurrency handling (multi-tab, multi-device) with auth
obligations.summary: Concurrent access from multiple tabs or devices is deferred, with optimistic locking noted as the likely answer. It is deferred together with authentication because the ownership question motivates the concurrency semantics.
obligations.quote: **Concurrency (multi-tab, multi-device).** Optimistic locking is the likely answer. Deferred with auth
obligations.novel_why: Multi-session concurrency over a persisted Journey document is beyond the spikes' in-memory scope.

obligations.title: Defer storage-layer choice between in-place status flip and copy-to-archive
obligations.summary: Whether the In Progress to Submitted transition keeps the same document with a status flip or copies it to an archive is an operational storage-layer question. It is deferred because it does not block model design.
obligations.quote: Same document with a status flip vs copy-to-archive is operational and not blocking model design. Deferred.
obligations.novel_why: Storage-layout choices for the submitted lifecycle are novel persistence territory.

obligations.title: Defer per-Journey config snapshotting with the config extension
obligations.summary: Snapshotting the journey configuration onto each Journey is deferred. Config itself is a foreseen extension (section I of the doc), so snapshotting is deferred along with it.
obligations.quote: **Config snapshot per Journey.** Config itself is a foreseen extension (§I). Snapshotting deferred with it.
obligations.novel_why: Pinning a config/model version per Journey is a model-versioning concern absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Defer retention policy pending Defra-wide guidance
obligations.summary: How long journeys are stored before deletion is deferred. Distinct policies likely apply to abandoned in-progress journeys, submitted journeys, and backups; retention is statute-driven for most Defra services and probably needs Defra-wide guidance plus a concrete service context rather than a model-level decision.
obligations.quote: distinct policies likely apply to abandoned in-progress journeys, submitted journeys, and backups. Statute-driven for most Defra services
obligations.novel_why: Data retention for persisted journeys has no analogue in the storage-free spikes.

obligations.title: Adopt extension points only on concrete service demand
obligations.summary: Post-submit extension points (navigation, downloads, amendment, withdrawal, events, reference numbers, atomicity) are adopted demand-driven: only when a concrete service needs them, never speculatively built into the model.
obligations.quote: Adopt any of these when a concrete service needs them.
obligations.novel_why: A scoping/adoption principle already implicit in the MVP boundary the spikes exercise; it requires no new spike behaviour, only governs future extension work.

obligations.title: Do not assume MongoDB; Redis or another store may be required
obligations.summary: The workspace-default MongoDB is explicitly flagged as possibly non-viable for Journey data; the datastore decision must genuinely consider Redis or another store rather than defaulting to Mongo.
obligations.quote: MongoDB — the workspace's default — may not be viable for this data; Redis or another store may be required.
obligations.novel_why: No spike code or option doc mentions Redis or questions MongoDB viability; all four spikes are in-memory and silent on store choice — this caveat exists only in obligations.md.

obligations.title: Datastore decision is urgent — persistence implementation is blocked on it
obligations.summary: The datastore choice is deferred but flagged urgent as a sequencing constraint: implementation (not just design) of the persistence layer cannot meaningfully start until the decision is resolved.
obligations.quote: **flagged urgent**: implementation of the persistence layer can't meaningfully start until it's resolved.
obligations.novel_why: The spikes contain no persistence layer or sequencing/prioritisation statements; the urgency flag and implementation-blocking dependency appear only in this doc.

obligations.title: Keep the Obligations model Flow-agnostic
obligations.summary: The Obligations definition must not depend on any particular Flow or UI. Multiple Flows (CRM, public web, mobile, prototype) for the same Service all reference the same Obligations.
obligations.quote: The Obligations model is **Flow-agnostic**. Multiple Flows for the same Service ... all reference the same Obligations.
obligations.novel_why: Existing spikes each bind one config to one journey; a shared obligations layer referenced by multiple Flows is new.

obligations.title: Support multiple coexisting Flows over one Obligations definition
obligations.summary: Two or more Flows (e.g. a skeleton Flow for rapid validation plus a polished production Flow) must be able to coexist over the same Obligations definition simultaneously.
obligations.quote: Two (or more) Flows can coexist over the same Obligations definition
obligations.novel_why: No existing spike supports two concurrent presentations of the same underlying data contract.

obligations.title: Use dual Flows as a behavioural-equivalence regression check
obligations.summary: If two Flows both satisfy the same Obligations, they are behaviourally equivalent at the data-contract level; coexisting Flows double as a regression check for this equivalence.
obligations.quote: doubles as a regression-check: if both Flows satisfy the same Obligations, they're behaviourally equivalent at the data-contract level
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow equivalence checking is explicitly listed as novel territory beyond the four spikes.

obligations.title: Map every user-submitted form field to an obligation
obligations.summary: Anything the user submits on a form, excluding auth and other cross-cutting concerns, must map to an obligation in the Obligations layer.
obligations.quote: anything the user submits on a form (excluding auth and other cross-cutting concerns) should map to an obligation
obligations.novel_why: Obligations as the mandatory first-class record for all submitted data is the new paradigm, not present in the spikes.

obligations.title: Keep presentational pages and UI affordances in the Flow layer
obligations.summary: Pure-presentational pages (start screens, 'what you'll need', confirmation, error pages, hub views) and non-data UI affordances (language toggles, accessibility controls) are Flow furniture and live entirely in the Flow layer, not in Obligations.
obligations.quote: Pure-presentational pages ... and non-data UI affordances ... are Flow furniture and live entirely in the Flow layer.
obligations.novel_why: Depends on the new Obligations/Flow layer split which no spike has.

obligations.title: Treat the Service layer as a pattern, not a runtime construct
obligations.summary: Each application implements the Service pattern in its own code. There is no centralised registry, no shared library across Services, and no runtime multi-Service support.
obligations.quote: **The Service layer is a conceptual pattern, not a runtime construct.** Each application implements the pattern in its own code.
obligations.novel_why: The Service/Flow/Journey layering itself is new; the spikes have no Service abstraction.

obligations.title: Scope each runtime to exactly one Service
obligations.summary: Within one deployment the runtime knows about exactly one Service — its own — so code never has to answer 'which Service'.
obligations.quote: Within one deployment, the runtime knows about exactly one Service — its own
obligations.novel_why: Single-Service runtime scoping is a rule of the new layered architecture, absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Define Layer 1 Obligations as a portable Service-level data contract
obligations.summary: Layer 1 is an obligations.json (or similar) holding identity, type, cardinality, and constraints. It knows nothing about pages, Flows, or how data is acquired, is fully portable, and there is exactly one per Service.
obligations.quote: Service-level data contract. Identity, type, cardinality, constraints. Knows nothing about pages, Flows, or how data is acquired. Fully portable. **One per Service.**
obligations.novel_why: A fully portable page-agnostic data contract is beyond spike-d's schema-first approach, which still couples validity to flow annotations in one artefact set.

obligations.title: Implement the ObligationEvaluator as a pure function
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator must be a pure function with signature (obligations, fulfilments) → { fulfilments (amended), obligations (per-obligation state) }.
obligations.quote: the ObligationEvaluator is pure `(obligations, fulfilments) → { fulfilments (amended), obligations (per-obligation state) }`
obligations.novel_why: Obligations/fulfilments as evaluator inputs/outputs are first-class records unique to this model.

obligations.title: Implement the JourneyEvaluator as pure primitives over the Flow tree
obligations.summary: The JourneyEvaluator is a collection of pure primitives operating on the Flow tree, consuming the ObligationEvaluator's output.
obligations.quote: The JourneyEvaluator is a collection of pure primitives operating on the Flow tree using the ObligationEvaluator's output.
obligations.novel_why: The two-evaluator split (obligation vs journey) over a Flow tree is a new engine architecture; spike-a has pure selectors but no separate journey evaluator layer.

obligations.title: Make the orchestrator the sole side-effecting wrapper
obligations.summary: The orchestrator is the side-effecting wrapper: it triggers system-handled obligations, collects callbacks, writes to fulfilments, and re-runs the evaluators. Purity lives in the evaluators; effects live only here.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator is the side-effecting wrapper that triggers system-handled obligations, collects callbacks, writes to `fulfilments`, and re-runs the evaluators.
obligations.novel_why: System-handled obligations and a callback-collecting orchestrator loop go beyond the spikes' request-scoped evaluation.

obligations.title: Ship one evaluator/orchestrator set per implementation language per Service
obligations.summary: Layer 1.5 (evaluators + orchestrator) is implementation-language code, not portable across languages; each language consumer ships its own set — one per implementation language per Service.
obligations.quote: not portable across languages, but each language consumer ships its own set. **One per implementation language per Service.**
obligations.novel_why: Cross-language re-implementation of the evaluator against a portable model is not a concern any spike addresses.

obligations.title: Define Layer 2 Flow as a static delivery definition referencing obligations by id
obligations.summary: A Flow (e.g. flow.json) is a static definition of how the Service is delivered: it references obligations by id and declares pages, ordering, grouping, per-page hard/soft mandate, copy/labels/hints, and acquisition mechanisms (form input, file upload, etc.).
obligations.quote: References obligations by id; declares pages, ordering, grouping, per-page hard/soft mandate, copy/labels/hints, acquisition mechanisms for user-facing obligations
obligations.novel_why: Referencing a separate obligations layer by id, with mandates and acquisition mechanisms in the Flow, is new relative to the spikes' single-artefact configs.

obligations.title: Allow one or more Flows per Service
obligations.summary: A Service can have one or more Flows — skeleton, polished, re-working, and so on.
obligations.quote: **One or more Flows per Service** — skeleton, polished, re-working, etc.
obligations.novel_why: Multi-Flow cardinality over shared obligations is not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Define Layer 3 Journey as a runtime instance owning the fulfilments map
obligations.summary: A Journey is a specific user navigating a specific Flow at runtime. It owns the fulfilments map, has its own id, and is isolated from other Journeys.
obligations.quote: a specific user navigating a specific Flow. Owns the fulfilments map ... Has its own id; isolated from other Journeys.
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments as a first-class journey-owned record is core to the novel model.

obligations.title: Support zero-many concurrent Journeys per Flow per user
obligations.summary: A user can have zero to many Journeys per Flow at any moment; multiple browser tabs produce multiple concurrent isolated Journeys.
obligations.quote: **Zero-many Journeys per Flow per user at any moment** (multi-tab → multiple concurrent Journeys).
obligations.novel_why: The spikes use single session state; explicit multi-tab concurrent-journey isolation is new.

obligations.title: Model Flow content as a tree of Containers with two shapes
obligations.summary: A Flow's content is a tree of Containers with exactly two shapes: Group (Section or SubSection, holding an ordered list of child Containers) and Page (a leaf holding an ordered list of presents entries).
obligations.quote: A Flow's content is a tree of **Containers**. Two shapes:
obligations.novel_why: The spikes have flat section/task lists; a recursive Container tree is a new structural model.

obligations.title: Support arbitrary nesting of Groups
obligations.summary: The Container tree supports arbitrary nesting: a Section can contain SubSections that contain further SubSections that contain Pages, in any combination.
obligations.quote: Arbitrary nesting is supported. A Section can contain SubSections that contain further SubSections that contain Pages, in any combination.
obligations.novel_why: Arbitrary-depth nesting exceeds the spikes' fixed section→task structure.

obligations.title: Order children per Group; no Flow-global page ordering
obligations.summary: Each Group declares its own ordered child list. There must be no Flow-global Page ordering; sequence is derived from the tree.
obligations.quote: **Each Group declares its own ordered child list; there is no Flow-global Page ordering.**
obligations.novel_why: Existing spikes use a global linear task order; per-Group local ordering is a new rule.

obligations.title: Treat CheckYourAnswers as a special container, not a Section
obligations.summary: CheckYourAnswers appears at the Flow's top level as a special construct that is explicitly not a Section in the Container tree.
obligations.quote: └── CheckYourAnswers   (special; not a Section)

obligations.title: Support both Sectioned and pure-linear (flat) Flow shapes
obligations.summary: A skeleton Flow may be a flat ordered list of Pages with no Groups at all; the model must support both Sectioned and pure-linear shapes.
obligations.quote: the model needs to support both Sectioned and pure-linear shapes
obligations.novel_why: The spikes only implement the sectioned hub shape; a groupless flat Flow is a new supported shape.

obligations.title: Resolve the precise skeleton-Flow shape (deferred)
obligations.summary: The exact shape of a skeleton (groupless) Flow is an open question, deferred to the 'What's still open' section of the doc.
obligations.quote: See §What's still open for the precise skeleton-shape question.
obligations.novel_why: An explicitly deferred question about the new Flow model.

obligations.title: Make Pages declare their presented obligations and map 1:1 to HTML artefacts
obligations.summary: A Page declares which obligations it presents. Pages are the unit of HTML composition and map one-to-one to HTML artefacts in the implementation.
obligations.quote: A Page declares which obligations it presents. Pages are the unit of HTML composition; pages map 1:1 to HTML artefacts in the implementation.
obligations.novel_why: Pages presenting obligation ids (rather than owning fields/validation directly) is specific to the obligations model.

obligations.title: Support static page membership via a presents list
obligations.summary: In the common case a page presents a fixed set of obligations via an ordered 'presents' array of { obligation, mandate? } entries in flow.json.
obligations.quote: Most pages present a fixed set of obligations:
obligations.novel_why: The presents-list-of-obligation-refs page shape is part of the new model.

obligations.title: Support dynamic page membership via presentsForEach for indexed obligations
obligations.summary: For indexed obligations whose fulfilments are projected from state (e.g. per-modification cost), a page can declare presentsForEach with { obligation, fulfilment: '*', mandate }, presenting a variable number of obligation slots — one per fulfilment.
obligations.quote: the same page model presents a variable number of obligation slots — one per fulfilment of the indexed obligation
obligations.novel_why: Dynamic page membership over indexed obligations is explicitly beyond the spikes' fixed-field pages.

obligations.title: Expand presentsForEach to one virtual presentation per in-scope fulfilment
obligations.summary: The runtime must expand presentsForEach into one virtual page-presentation per in-scope fulfilment of the indexed obligation.
obligations.quote: The runtime expands `presentsForEach` to one virtual page-presentation per in-scope fulfilment of the indexed obligation.
obligations.novel_why: Runtime expansion of virtual presentations from indexed fulfilments is new engine behaviour.

obligations.title: Make presentsForEach work for any indexed source
obligations.summary: The presentsForEach shape must work identically for any indexed obligation source: user, derived, or seeded.
obligations.quote: Same shape works for any indexed source (`user`, `derived`, `seeded`).
obligations.novel_why: Seeded/derived/user obligation sources are first-class concepts unique to this model.

obligations.title: Allow a page to mix presents and presentsForEach
obligations.summary: A single page can combine static presents entries and a presentsForEach block — for example a static header plus a dynamic per-fulfilment section.
obligations.quote: A page can mix `presents` and `presentsForEach` — a static header plus a dynamic per-fulfilment section.
obligations.novel_why: Mixed static/dynamic membership on one page has no equivalent in the spikes.

obligations.title: Support read-only Pages via empty or omitted presents
obligations.summary: A Page with an empty or omitted presents is the read-only case, used for intro pages, summaries, and 'what you'll need' interstitials. It participates in the declared narrative order but has no inputs to submit.
obligations.quote: A Page may have an empty `presents` (or omit it entirely) — that's the read-only case
obligations.novel_why: Read-only pages inside the flow model are explicitly named as novel territory.

obligations.title: Give read-only Pages a permanent Not Applicable status
obligations.summary: Because a read-only Page has no inputs and no in-scope obligations to fulfil, its status is always Not Applicable.
obligations.quote: it just has no inputs to submit and no in-scope obligations to fulfil, so its status is always **Not Applicable**.
obligations.novel_why: Deriving a permanent NA status for structural read-only pages is new status semantics.

obligations.title: Derive read-only-ness intrinsically; no readOnly flag in the schema
obligations.summary: Read-only-ness is intrinsic, derived from the absence of presents and presentsForEach. The schema must not carry a separate readOnly flag, to avoid the contradiction of a flag coexisting with a non-empty presents.
obligations.quote: The schema deliberately doesn't carry a separate `readOnly` flag (avoids the contradiction-waiting-to-happen of a flag and a non-empty `presents` together).
obligations.novel_why: A deliberate schema-design rule for the new read-only page concept.

obligations.title: Optionally expose an isReadOnly derived helper
obligations.summary: The runtime may expose a derived helper for code clarity: isReadOnly(page) returns true when presents is empty/absent and there is no presentsForEach.
obligations.quote: The runtime may expose a derived helper for code clarity:
obligations.novel_why: Helper contract for the novel read-only concept.

obligations.title: Route navigation decisions through the status taxonomy, not a read-only check
obligations.summary: Navigation logic must use the status taxonomy (Not Applicable), not a direct read-only check, when deciding how to treat read-only pages.
obligations.quote: Navigation logic uses the status taxonomy (NA), not a read-only check directly
obligations.novel_why: Ties navigation to the new NA status concept rather than page structure — a new rule.

obligations.title: Distinguish dynamic-empty pages from structurally read-only pages
obligations.summary: A Page with presentsForEach that currently resolves to zero in-scope fulfilments is dynamic, not structurally read-only, because state changes can populate it. Its status is Not Applicable while empty.
obligations.quote: it may resolve to zero in-scope fulfilments in current state but isn't structurally read-only, because state changes can populate it
obligations.novel_why: The empty-dynamic-page edge case only exists in the new indexed-obligation model.

obligations.title: Enforce hard mandates at Save and continue
obligations.summary: For a page entry with mandate 'hard': at Save and continue, if the obligation is in scope and unfulfilled at submit, the page re-renders with errors and the user cannot advance (the fullName-on-the-email-gate pattern).
obligations.quote: at Save and continue, if this obligation is in scope and unfulfilled at submit, the page re-renders with errors and the user can't advance
obligations.novel_why: Per-page-entry mandate semantics (hard blocking per obligation pairing) are a new mechanism, though the email-gate behaviour itself exists in the spikes.

obligations.title: Let soft-mandate obligations pass Save and continue but stay on the to-do list
obligations.summary: For a soft mandate (the default, which can be omitted), Save and continue is allowed even if the obligation is in scope and unfulfilled; the Engine keeps it on the still-to-do list so the user encounters it again elsewhere.
obligations.quote: Save and continue is allowed even if the obligation is in scope and unfulfilled. The Engine keeps it on the still-to-do list
obligations.novel_why: Soft mandates with engine-tracked outstanding obligations go beyond the spikes' mandatory-field enforcement.

obligations.title: Default the mandate to soft; require hard to be explicit
obligations.summary: The mandate flag on each page entry is optional and defaults to soft; hard must be declared explicitly. This avoids the 'I forgot to opt out' footgun and matches the expected distribution where most entries are not hard-blocking.
obligations.quote: Soft default + explicit hard. Skips the "I forgot to opt out" footgun
obligations.novel_why: A settled default-direction decision for the new mandate concept.

obligations.title: Exactly one Obligations definition per Service
obligations.summary: Layer 1 carries an explicit cardinality rule: a Service has exactly one Obligations definition. The extraction captured the parallel cardinality rules for evaluators (one per language per Service), Flows (one-or-more per Service) and Journeys (zero-many per Flow per user), but not this bolded one-per-Service constraint on the Obligations contract itself.
obligations.quote: Fully portable. **One per Service.**
obligations.novel_why: The four spikes were single-service, single-flow prototypes with no Service-layer concept; the explicit one-Obligations-per-Service cardinality rule first appears in this architecture section.

obligations.title: Mandate on presentsForEach applies per expanded fulfilment
obligations.summary: When a page uses presentsForEach, the declared mandate is evaluated per expanded fulfilment slot, not once for the page — each in-scope fulfilment of the indexed obligation is individually hard/soft enforced at Save and continue. Existing items cover presentsForEach expansion (ARCH-25/26) and mandate semantics (ARCH-35/36/37) but not this per-fulfilment granularity of enforcement.
obligations.quote: "mandate": "hard" // applies per-fulfilment
obligations.novel_why: No spike implements a mandate concept at all (grep across spike-a..d and shared finds no mandate handling), let alone per-fulfilment mandate semantics for dynamically expanded pages.

obligations.title: Read-only pages stay in the declared narrative sequence
obligations.summary: Read-only pages are not skipped despite their permanent Not Applicable status: they remain part of the Flow's natural narrative order and are shown to the user in declared sequence during linear navigation. Existing items cover how read-only-ness is derived (ARCH-29/31) and its NA status (ARCH-30), but not the behaviour that these pages are still visited in order.
obligations.quote: The Page is part of the natural narrative order and is shown to the user in declared sequence
obligations.novel_why: Spikes contain no read-only/interstitial page concept (grep across spike implementations finds none); the rule that NA-status pages still occupy the declared narrative sequence is new here.

obligations.title: Journeys carry their own id and are isolated from each other
obligations.summary: Each Journey instance has its own id and is isolated from all other Journeys — concurrent Journeys (e.g. multi-tab) share no state. ARCH-15 covers the Journey definition and fulfilments-map ownership and ARCH-16 covers zero-many concurrency, but neither captures the per-Journey identity and isolation guarantee that makes safe concurrency possible.
obligations.quote: Has its own id; isolated from other Journeys.
obligations.novel_why: The four spikes ran a single implicit session journey; explicit per-Journey ids and a cross-Journey isolation guarantee are introduced by this architecture section.

obligations.title: Compose page-level and Engine-level mandates orthogonally
obligations.summary: Each page entry carries a page-level mandate (hard/soft) and each obligation carries an Engine-level Journey-completion mandate (mandatory/optional). The two are independent dimensions that combine per the four-row composition table rather than one overriding the other.
obligations.quote: The Flow's page-level mandate and the Engine's Journey-completion mandate compose orthogonally
obligations.novel_why: The existing spikes have a single mandatory-field notion; a two-axis mandate model composing page and Engine levels is new.

obligations.title: Implement hard x mandatory as most restrictive combination
obligations.summary: An obligation that is page-hard and Engine-mandatory must be filled on that page and is also required for Journey completion. This is the most restrictive of the four mandate combinations.
obligations.quote: Must fill here AND required for Journey completion. Most restrictive.
obligations.novel_why: Behaviour is defined by the novel two-mandate composition matrix, not by the spikes' single mandatory-field rule.

obligations.title: Implement hard x optional as a page-only UX constraint
obligations.summary: A page can hard-mandate an obligation the Engine treats as optional: the user must fill it on that page as a UX choice, even though the Journey can complete without it.
obligations.quote: Must fill on this page (UX choice) even though the Journey doesn't strictly need it.
obligations.novel_why: Page-enforced-but-journey-optional fields have no equivalent in the four spikes' mandatory-field model.

obligations.title: Implement soft x mandatory as the skippable-but-required 90% case
obligations.summary: With a soft page mandate on an Engine-mandatory obligation, the user can skip the field on the page; the Engine keeps the obligation in scope and it must be fulfilled before the Journey can complete. The doc calls this the 90% case.
obligations.quote: Can skip on this page; Engine keeps the obligation in scope; needed before Journey can complete. **The 90% case.**
obligations.novel_why: Skippable-per-page yet completion-blocking obligations tracked by an Engine are beyond the spikes' per-page mandatory enforcement.

obligations.title: Implement soft x optional as unconstrained
obligations.summary: An obligation with a soft page mandate and an optional Engine mandate carries no enforcement at either level; it can be skipped on the page and does not block Journey completion.
obligations.quote: No constraint at either level.
obligations.novel_why: Defined only within the novel two-mandate composition matrix.

obligations.title: Default page-level mandate to soft
obligations.summary: When a page entry does not declare a mandate, the page-level mandate defaults to soft (skippable on the page).
obligations.quote: `soft` (default)
obligations.novel_why: A default value on the novel page-entry mandate field, which the spikes do not have.

obligations.title: Block Save and continue on unfulfilled in-scope hard mandates
obligations.summary: On page POST, every page entry with mandate hard that is currently in scope and unfulfilled blocks Save and continue. Out-of-scope or fulfilled hard entries do not block.
obligations.quote: every page entry with `mandate: 'hard'` that is in scope and unfulfilled blocks Save and continue
obligations.novel_why: Enforcement keys off mandate + obligation scope/fulfilment state, not the spikes' plain per-page mandatory-field checks.

obligations.title: Re-render blocked page with GDS error summary and per-field errors
obligations.summary: When a page POST is blocked by unfulfilled hard-mandated obligations, the page re-renders with a GOV.UK error summary plus per-field error messages.
obligations.quote: The page re-renders with a GDS error summary + per-field errors.

obligations.title: Make Engine output the source of truth at Journey completion
obligations.summary: On a Journey completion attempt (POST to check-your-answers or equivalent), the Engine's output alone decides: any unfulfilled Engine-mandatory obligation blocks completion, regardless of the mandates individual pages declared.
obligations.quote: any unfulfilled Engine-mandatory obligation blocks completion regardless of how individual pages declared their mandates
obligations.novel_why: A journey-wide Engine completion gate over obligation fulfilments, independent of page validation, is beyond the spikes' per-page validation.

obligations.title: Save partial state when non-hard obligations are unfilled
obligations.summary: Save and continue from a page with unfilled non-hard (soft) obligations still persists the partial state that was entered; the skip does not discard what the user did fill in.
obligations.quote: partial state is saved; navigation advances per the navigation algorithm
obligations.novel_why: Persisting partial fulfilment on a mandate-driven skip depends on the novel soft-mandate concept.

obligations.title: Resolve the navigation-advance algorithm (open)
obligations.summary: How navigation advances after Save and continue is explicitly still open; the doc defers it to the navigation algorithm discussion in its What's still open section (item P).
obligations.quote: navigation advances per the navigation algorithm (still open — see §What's still open, P)
obligations.novel_why: An explicitly deferred design question in the new obligations model, not something the spikes settle.

obligations.title: Add static check: hard-mandate must have a rendered input
obligations.summary: Provide a static consistency check over each (page entry, HTML template) pair: if a page hard-mandates obligation X but the template has no input for X, the user would be stuck, so the check must fail.
obligations.quote: if a page hard-mandates obligation X but the actual HTML template doesn't have an input for X, the user is stuck
obligations.novel_why: A model-vs-template static consistency check over hard mandates has no counterpart in the four spikes.

obligations.title: Add warning-level static check for hard-mandate on never-applicable obligation
obligations.summary: Provide a warning-level static check that flags a page hard-mandating an obligation that can never plausibly be in scope when the page is reached, since that constraint is dead code.
obligations.quote: if a page hard-mandates X but X can't plausibly be in scope when this page is reached, the constraint is dead code
obligations.novel_why: Dead-constraint detection over mandate/scope interaction is new tooling beyond anything the spikes check.

obligations.title: Test cross-Flow evaluator equivalence on identical fulfilments
obligations.summary: Given identical fulfilments, every Flow of a Service must leave the ObligationEvaluator in the same end state. This equivalence must be testable across all Flows of the same Service.
obligations.quote: given identical fulfilments, all Flows of a Service should leave the ObligationEvaluator in the same state at the end
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow/Engine equivalence over a shared obligation set is explicitly beyond the single-journey spikes.

obligations.title: Use the skeleton Flow as a reference oracle for the polished Flow
obligations.summary: The cross-Flow equivalence test lets the skeleton Flow act as a reference oracle: the polished Flow's evaluator end-state is verified against the skeleton Flow's for the same fulfilments.
obligations.quote: The skeleton Flow becomes a reference oracle for the polished Flow.
obligations.novel_why: Oracle-based testing between two Flows over one obligation set presumes the novel multi-Flow architecture.

obligations.title: Keep acquisition methods out of the obligation model
obligations.summary: An obligation may be acquirable multiple ways (e.g. manual DOB entry versus NI-lookup-then-extract), but acquisition methods and their UI mechanisms are entirely Flow-layer composition. The obligation only sees a typed value arrive in its field.
obligations.quote: These ways are NOT properties of the obligation — the obligation just sees a typed value arrive in its field.
obligations.novel_why: A strict obligation/acquisition-method separation with obligations as first-class records is new relative to all four spikes.

obligations.title: Store provenance as Flow-side metadata
obligations.summary: Which acquisition method was used to obtain a value (provenance) is recorded as Flow-side metadata, not as a concept on the obligation itself.
obligations.quote: Provenance ("which method was used") becomes Flow-side metadata, not an obligation concept.
obligations.novel_why: None of the spikes model provenance; placing it Flow-side is a new data-model decision.

obligations.title: Collapse obligation kinds into types or journey-side mechanisms
obligations.summary: The earlier list of obligation kinds (question, file-upload, sub-journey, agreement, payment, etc.) is dropped as a discriminant. Each kind collapses into either a data type on the obligation or a journey-side acquisition mechanism.
obligations.quote: Under the cleaner model most collapse into either **data types** on the obligation or **journey-side acquisition mechanisms**
obligations.novel_why: The kinds-collapse restructures the novel obligation data model; the spikes have no kind concept to collapse.

obligations.title: Treat question as the default UI mechanism, not a kind
obligations.summary: Question is not an obligation kind; it is just the default journey-side UI mechanism, since any obligation can be acquired via a form input.
obligations.quote: Not a kind — just the default UI mechanism (any obligation can be acquired via a form input).
obligations.novel_why: Reframes form questions as one acquisition mechanism over type-only obligations, a distinction the spikes do not draw.

obligations.title: Model file uploads by canonical data, not as a kind
obligations.summary: If the canonical data is the file itself, the obligation type is file (or file-set). If the canonical data is something extracted from the file, the obligation type is the extracted type and the upload is a journey-side acquisition method.
obligations.quote: If the canonical data IS the file: obligation type is `file` (or `file-set`).
obligations.novel_why: The spikes have no file-upload modelling; the canonical-data-vs-acquisition split is new.

obligations.title: Handle sub-journeys as orchestrator-handled sub-journey-receipt obligations
obligations.summary: A sub-journey becomes an obligation of type sub-journey-receipt handled by the orchestrator: it fires the sub-journey, awaits the callback, and writes the receipt id into fulfilments. The evaluator then treats it as ordinary input.
obligations.quote: the orchestrator fires the sub-journey, awaits the callback, writes the receipt id into `fulfilments`. The evaluator treats it as ordinary input.
obligations.novel_why: An orchestrator with a callback pattern writing into a fulfilments store is new to all four spikes.

obligations.title: Model agreements as boolean/consent-typed obligations
obligations.summary: An agreement (e.g. T&Cs) is probably an obligation with type boolean (or consent); the checkbox-after-content UI mechanism is journey-side, not part of the obligation.
obligations.quote: Probably an obligation with `type: boolean` (or `consent`). The UI mechanism (a checkbox after content) is journey-side.
obligations.novel_why: Agreements as typed obligations with journey-side UI mechanisms extend beyond the spikes' scenario.

obligations.title: Model payment as a payment-receipt-typed obligation
obligations.summary: Payment is probably its own obligation with type payment-receipt, whose canonical data is the receipt or confirmation id, satisfied by a journey-side payment flow.
obligations.quote: Probably its own obligation with `type: payment-receipt` (the canonical data is the receipt / confirmation id), satisfied by a journey-side payment flow.
obligations.novel_why: Payment obligations and receipt-typed fulfilments have no analogue in the four spikes.

obligations.title: Model scheduling as a time-slot-typed obligation
obligations.summary: A scheduling step becomes an obligation whose type is time-slot or similar; the concrete UI mechanism (calendar picker, list, etc.) lives journey-side.
obligations.quote: Obligation type is `time-slot` or similar. UI mechanism (calendar picker, list, …) is journey-side.
obligations.novel_why: Scheduling obligations are outside the spikes' insurance-quote scenario and rely on the new type-only model.

obligations.title: Handle lookups as orchestrator-handled lookup-result obligations
obligations.summary: A lookup becomes an obligation of type lookup-result handled by the orchestrator: it makes the external call, awaits the response, and writes the result into fulfilments, using the same callback mechanism as sub-journeys.
obligations.quote: the orchestrator makes the external call, awaits the response, writes the result into `fulfilments`. Same mechanism as sub-journeys.
obligations.novel_why: System-handled obligation types resolved by an orchestrator callback are new to all four spikes.

obligations.title: Make type the only obligation discriminant with an open type space
obligations.summary: The obligation's type field is the sole discriminant between obligation varieties, and the set of allowed types is open-ended rather than a closed enum.
obligations.quote: The obligation's **type** becomes the only discriminant, and the type space stays open.
obligations.novel_why: A single open type discriminant on first-class obligation records is a new data-model rule.

obligations.title: Split type handling between journey renderers and orchestrator handlers
obligations.summary: The journey layer translates each user-facing obligation type into a renderable mechanism, while the orchestrator handles system-handled types via the callback pattern.
obligations.quote: The journey-layer translates each user-facing type into a renderable mechanism; the orchestrator handles system-handled types via the callback pattern.
obligations.novel_why: A layered user-facing/system-handled type dispatch with an orchestrator does not exist in any spike.

obligations.title: Extend types without touching the obligation file
obligations.summary: Adding a new obligation type must require only a new journey-side renderer or a new orchestrator handler; the obligation file itself stays unchanged.
obligations.quote: New types = new journey-side renderers OR new orchestrator handlers; the obligation file is unchanged.
obligations.novel_why: An extensibility invariant protecting the obligation data file is a new cross-cutting rule.

obligations.title: Adapt spike C by moving rules.json into the evaluator, keeping authored reasons
obligations.summary: Spike C (rules engine) is the closest existing fit: its authored reason strings and engine.evaluate(answers) approximate obligations plus evaluator. To match the vision, C's rules.json content would shift entirely into the evaluator function, losing its purely declarative framing while keeping the authored-reasons pattern intact.
obligations.quote: C's `rules.json` would shift entirely into the evaluator function (lose its purely-declarative framing), keeping the authored-reasons pattern intact
obligations.novel_why: Prescribes restructuring spike C toward the new obligations/evaluator split with authored-reason surfacing preserved.

obligations.title: Refactor spike A to split obligations data from evaluator and journey
