obligations.summary: Spike A (declarative selectors) needs a significant refactor: it currently embeds journey-concept steps inside journey.json and would need to split an obligations.json data file from an evaluator and from journey.json as the presentation layer.
obligations.quote: Would need to split `obligations.json` (data) from an evaluator and from `journey.json` (presentation).
obligations.novel_why: Defines a new three-way obligations/evaluator/presentation split that spike A does not currently have.

obligations.title: Accept spike B needs one statechart per journey over shared obligations
obligations.summary: Spike B (statechart) fits awkwardly: a statechart is a journey by construction, so supporting multiple journeys over one obligation set means one statechart per journey, all reading shared obligations. The paradigm makes flow primary rather than obligations.
obligations.quote: "multiple journeys over one obligation set" means one statechart per journey, all reading shared obligations
obligations.novel_why: The multiple-journeys-over-one-obligation-set requirement and its statechart consequence go beyond the single-journey spikes.

obligations.title: Map spike D schema to obligation types and annotations to the journey layer
obligations.summary: In spike D (schema-first), the JSON Schema could become the type/constraint layer of obligations with annotations.json as the journey layer. However D's weaknesses persist — no native provenance and value-blind conditionals — and an evaluator function is alien to the schema-first paradigm.
obligations.quote: The schema could become the type/constraint layer of obligations; the annotations the journey layer.
obligations.novel_why: Recasts spike D's schema as the type layer of first-class obligations, and names provenance as a gap.

obligations.title: Build the orchestrator layer as net-new work
obligations.summary: No existing spike ships the obligations / evaluators / orchestrator / journey split; the orchestrator layer — the fixed-point loop and system-handled obligation callbacks — is new work regardless of which spike paradigm is chosen.
obligations.quote: The orchestrator layer (fixed-point loop, system-handled obligation callbacks) is new to all four.
obligations.novel_why: The doc explicitly states the orchestrator with its fixed-point loop and callbacks exists in none of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Evaluator treats orchestrator-written fulfilments as ordinary input
obligations.summary: System-handled obligations (sub-journey-receipt, lookup-result) are satisfied by the orchestrator writing values into fulfilments; the evaluator must consume these exactly like user-entered values, with no special-casing of system-handled types.
obligations.quote: the orchestrator fires the sub-journey, awaits the callback, writes the receipt id into `fulfilments`. The evaluator treats it as ordinary input.
obligations.novel_why: No existing spike has an orchestrator writing fulfilments (line 1145: the orchestrator layer is new to all four), so evaluator-uniformity over system-written values exists in none of them.

obligations.title: Spike D's residual weaknesses persist under the obligation mapping
obligations.summary: Even after mapping D's schema to the type/constraint layer and annotations to the journey layer, D retains no native provenance and value-blind conditionals, and an evaluator function remains alien to the schema-first paradigm — a settled caveat against adopting D.
obligations.quote: D's other weaknesses (no native provenance, value-blind conditionals) don't go away — and the evaluator function is alien to the schema-first paradigm.

obligations.title: Support multiple alternative acquisition methods for a single obligation
obligations.summary: The design must allow the same obligation to be fulfilled via several alternative Flow-side acquisition routes (e.g. manual DOB entry vs NI-lookup-then-extract), with the obligation itself agnostic to which route delivered the typed value — acquisition methods and their UI mechanisms are entirely Flow-layer composition, and provenance (which method was used) is Flow-side metadata, not an obligation concept.
obligations.quote: A single obligation might be acquired multiple ways (manual DOB entry vs NI-lookup-then-extract).
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes supports alternative acquisition routes converging on one datum — each binds a field to exactly one input step/mechanism.

obligations.title: Separate content tree from navigation decisions
obligations.summary: The Flow's Container tree only declares what content exists; a separate navigation algorithm decides what page to show next based on the current Engine state. Both consume the shared status taxonomy.
obligations.quote: The Flow's Container tree says **what content exists**; the navigation algorithm says **what page to show next given the current Engine state**.
obligations.novel_why: Existing spikes derive navigation ad hoc; here navigation is defined as a function of an obligations Engine state over a Container tree.

obligations.title: Compute a status for every Container from Engine output
obligations.summary: Every Container (Page, SubSection, Section) has a status computed from the Engine output, and the same four states apply at every level of the tree.
obligations.quote: Every Container (Page, SubSection, Section) has a status, computed from the Engine output. The same four states apply at every level
obligations.novel_why: Spikes compute task statuses only at the task-list level; a uniform status at every Container level derived from an obligations Engine is new.

obligations.title: Define the four-state status taxonomy
obligations.summary: The status vocabulary is exactly four states: Not Applicable (no in-scope mandatory obligations), Not Started (applicable, no fulfilments), In Progress (some fulfilments but not all required), and Fulfilled (all in-scope mandatory obligations satisfied).
obligations.quote: | **Not Applicable** | Container has no in-scope mandatory obligations
obligations.novel_why: Status definitions are grounded in in-scope mandatory obligations and fulfilments, first-class concepts absent from the four spikes.

obligations.title: Skip Not Applicable Containers in navigation
obligations.summary: A Container whose status is Not Applicable is effectively skipped by the navigation algorithm.
obligations.quote: Effectively skipped by the navigation algorithm.

obligations.title: Apply the status vocabulary to the whole Journey
obligations.summary: The same four-state vocabulary applies to the Journey as a whole, where Journey Fulfilled means every top-level Section is Fulfilled.
obligations.quote: The same vocabulary applies to the Journey as a whole, with **Fulfilled** meaning every top-level Section is Fulfilled.

obligations.title: Do not include a Cannot-start-yet status
obligations.summary: A fifth 'Cannot start yet' state was considered and rejected; the taxonomy stays at four states. Blocked-but-in-scope cases collapse into Not Applicable.
obligations.quote: An earlier draft of the model included a fifth state, **Cannot start yet**, for Containers that were in scope but blocked from being started
obligations.novel_why: A settled design decision specific to the obligations model's status taxonomy, not something any spike addresses.

obligations.title: Model conditional blocked Sections via appliesWhen
obligations.summary: Sections blocked on a prerequisite condition are modelled as conditional via appliesWhen rules: they are Not Applicable until their trigger is met, then become Not Started.
obligations.quote: These collapse to `appliesWhen` rules → **Not Applicable** until triggered, then **Not Started**.
obligations.novel_why: appliesWhen-driven scope over obligations replacing a blocked status goes beyond the spikes' conditional-reveal handling.

obligations.title: Model async external blockers as system-handled obligations
obligations.summary: Genuine async/external blockers (sub-journey callbacks, third-party approvals, external reference-data lookups) are modelled as obligations themselves, typically system-handled obligations managed by the orchestrator. The dependent Container's appliesWhen references the readiness obligation, so it is Not Applicable until the external step completes and then becomes Not Started.
obligations.quote: can be modelled as **obligations themselves** — typically system-handled obligations that the orchestrator manages.
obligations.novel_why: System-handled readiness obligations managed by an orchestrator are a first-class obligation concept none of the spikes have.

obligations.title: Treat everything affecting applicability as an obligation
obligations.summary: A cross-cutting design rule: anything that affects a Container's applicability must be expressed as an obligation, which keeps the status-propagation rules simple (collapsing Cannot-start-yet into Not Applicable removed three mixed-state combinations).
obligations.quote: aligns with the rest of the design — everything that affects applicability is an obligation.
obligations.novel_why: Obligations as the single mechanism for applicability is core to the new obligations paradigm.

obligations.title: Defer signposting of upcoming NA work to an open question
obligations.summary: The UX benefit of the dropped Cannot-start-yet state (signposting upcoming work the user will need to do) is preserved as a renderer-level hint on the Container, but that hint is explicitly left as an open question.
obligations.quote: preserved as a separate concern — a renderer-level hint on the Container — left as an open question
obligations.novel_why: NA-section signposting as a renderer-level hint is explicitly flagged as novel/open territory beyond the spikes.

obligations.title: Page Fulfilled leaf rule
obligations.summary: A Page is Fulfilled if and only if every in-scope mandatory obligation it presents is fulfilled; this is the leaf rule of the recursive definition.
obligations.quote: A **Page** is Fulfilled iff every in-scope mandatory obligation it presents is fulfilled (leaf rule).
obligations.novel_why: Page completeness defined over presented mandatory obligations and their fulfilments is obligations-model-specific.

obligations.title: Group Fulfilled recursive rule
obligations.summary: A Group (Section or SubSection) is Fulfilled if and only if every applicable child Container is Fulfilled; Not Applicable children are excluded.
obligations.quote: A **Group** (Section or SubSection) is Fulfilled iff every applicable child Container is Fulfilled.

obligations.title: Journey Fulfilled rule
obligations.summary: A Journey is Fulfilled if and only if every top-level Section is Fulfilled.
obligations.quote: A **Journey** is Fulfilled iff every top-level Section is Fulfilled.

obligations.title: Task List shows Section-level status; drill in for detail
obligations.summary: Because Fulfilled recurses, parents can trust children's status; the Task List displays only Section-level status and users drill in for the granular per-Page/SubSection view.
obligations.quote: The Task List displays Section-level status; users drill in for the granular view.

obligations.title: Derive Group status from children with NA filtered out
obligations.summary: For Groups, status is derived purely from children's statuses, with Not Applicable children treated as filtered out before applying the propagation rules.
obligations.quote: For Groups, status is derived from their children. The propagation (with "Not Applicable" treated as filtered-out)

obligations.title: All children NA makes parent NA
obligations.summary: If all of a Group's children are Not Applicable, the parent Group is Not Applicable.
obligations.quote: **All children Not Applicable** → parent is **Not Applicable**.

obligations.title: All applicable children Fulfilled makes parent Fulfilled
obligations.summary: If all applicable children of a Group are Fulfilled, the parent Group is Fulfilled.
obligations.quote: **All applicable children Fulfilled** → parent is **Fulfilled**.

obligations.title: All applicable children Not Started makes parent Not Started
obligations.summary: If all applicable children of a Group are Not Started, the parent Group is Not Started.
obligations.quote: **All applicable children Not Started** → parent is **Not Started**.

obligations.title: Any other mix makes parent In Progress
obligations.summary: In any other combination — any child In Progress, or Fulfilled children mixed with Not Started ones — the parent Group is In Progress.
obligations.quote: **Otherwise** (any In Progress, or Fulfilled mixed with Not Started) → parent is **In Progress**.

obligations.title: Implement propagation per the seven-row truth table
obligations.summary: The propagation rules are pinned by an exhaustive truth table over the three booleans hasFulfilled/hasInProgress/hasNotStarted (after filtering Not Applicable), covering all seven non-empty combinations; the pattern is all-F gives F, any IP or F mixed with non-F gives IP, otherwise NS, and NA if everything was filtered out.
obligations.quote: Truth table over the three applicable-child-state booleans (after filtering Not Applicable)

obligations.title: Render Task List with per-Section status links
obligations.summary: The Task List renders each top-level Section with its status and a link into the Section.
obligations.quote: **Task List**: render each top-level Section with its status and a link.

obligations.title: Hide Not Applicable Sections on the Task List by default
obligations.summary: Sections with status Not Applicable are hidden from the Task List by default; a Flow may optionally signpost them as 'coming up' (tied to open question Q).
obligations.quote: Sections with status Not Applicable are hidden by default (the Flow may optionally signpost them as "coming up"
obligations.novel_why: Optional Flow-level signposting of hidden NA sections is part of the novel NA-signposting concern.

obligations.title: Enter a Section at the first Page in declared order
obligations.summary: The default behaviour when entering a Section is to navigate to the first Page in declared order (a depth-first walk of the Container tree), regardless of the Page's status.
obligations.quote: navigate to the **first Page in declared order** (depth-first into the Container tree), regardless of status.

obligations.title: Include read-only Pages in the Section walk
obligations.summary: Read-only Pages — those with no presents obligations — appear in the Section walk like any other Page, because they are part of the narrative.
obligations.quote: Read-only Pages (those with no `presents`) appear in the walk like any other Page — they're part of the narrative.
obligations.novel_why: Read-only Pages defined by an empty presents list are a construct the spikes do not have.

obligations.title: Allow forward, backward, and Task List movement from each Page
obligations.summary: From every Page inside a Section, the user can move forward, backward, or return to the Task List.
obligations.quote: The user can then move forward, backward, or back to the Task List from each Page.

obligations.title: Offer an optional Continue/Resume shortcut
obligations.summary: An optional Continue/Resume shortcut may be exposed (typically on the Task List when a Section is In Progress, or via another renderer-level affordance) that jumps directly to the first applicable Page with status Not Started or In Progress — where the user has work to do.
obligations.quote: An **optional "Continue / Resume" shortcut** may also be exposed ... that jumps directly to the first applicable Page with status **Not Started** or **In Progress**
obligations.novel_why: A status-taxonomy-driven resume shortcut is not implemented by any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Let the shortcut skip via status, not a read-only filter
obligations.summary: The Continue/Resume shortcut skips Not Applicable Pages (which includes all read-only Pages) and already-Fulfilled Pages purely through the existing status taxonomy; no separate read-only filter is needed.
obligations.quote: This naturally skips Pages with status Not Applicable (including all read-only Pages) and Pages already Fulfilled, via the existing status taxonomy
obligations.novel_why: Deriving read-only-page skipping from the obligations status taxonomy rather than a dedicated filter is a novel design rule.

obligations.title: Degrade shortcut to first-Page behaviour when Section Fulfilled
obligations.summary: For a fully Fulfilled Section the Continue/Resume shortcut finds no Not Started or In Progress Page and gracefully degrades to the default enter-at-first-Page behaviour.
obligations.quote: For a fully Fulfilled Section the shortcut has nothing to find and gracefully degrades to default first-Page behaviour.
obligations.novel_why: Edge-case behaviour of the novel resume shortcut.

obligations.title: Validate hard mandates on Page POST
obligations.summary: On a Page POST, the runtime validates hard mandates before accepting the submission.
obligations.quote: **On Page POST**: validate hard mandates
obligations.novel_why: Mandates (hard vs soft) as the validation primitive are first-class obligations-model records the spikes lack.

obligations.title: Write fulfilments and recalculate Engine on successful POST
obligations.summary: On successful Page POST validation, the runtime writes the submitted fulfilments to the Journey state and then recalculates the Engine from the new state.
obligations.quote: on success, write the submitted fulfilments to the Journey state; **recalculate the Engine** from the new state
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilments as first-class written records plus a full Engine recalculation per POST is unique to the obligations paradigm.

obligations.title: After POST, walk to next applicable non-Fulfilled Container
obligations.summary: After a successful POST and Engine recalculation, navigation walks to the next applicable, non-Fulfilled Container in the current Section's subtree; if there is none, the user is returned to the Task List.
obligations.quote: then walk to the next applicable non-Fulfilled Container in the current Section's subtree. If none, return to the Task List.

obligations.title: Keep the Task List reachable from every page
obligations.summary: The user can navigate back to the Task List at any point; it is always reachable from the page header.
obligations.quote: **At any point** the user can navigate back to the Task List (it's always reachable from the page header).

obligations.title: Make CYA reachable before the Journey is Fulfilled
obligations.summary: Check-your-answers is reachable before the Journey is Fulfilled; the page renders with soft prompts for missing items so the user sees current state and can navigate to fill the gaps.
obligations.quote: **CYA is reachable** earlier than Fulfilled — the page renders with soft prompts for missing items
obligations.novel_why: Early-reachable CYA with soft-mandate prompts for gaps goes beyond the spikes' change-links-only CYA.

obligations.title: Enable CYA Submit only when Journey is Fulfilled
obligations.summary: The Submit button on the check-your-answers page is enabled if and only if the Journey status is Fulfilled.
obligations.quote: The Submit button on CYA is enabled iff the Journey is Fulfilled.

obligations.title: Re-check Journey-Fulfilled server-side on CYA Submit
obligations.summary: On CYA Submit the Engine re-checks Journey-Fulfilled: if yes, the Journey transitions to Submitted; if no, CYA re-renders with the missing items called out. The disabled button is not trusted alone.
obligations.quote: **On CYA Submit**: the Engine re-checks Journey-Fulfilled; if yes, transition to Submitted. If no, re-render CYA with the missing items called out.
obligations.novel_why: A Submitted lifecycle transition guarded by an Engine re-check is part of the novel submit lifecycle.

obligations.title: Never hard-code next-page; always query the Engine
obligations.summary: The runtime must never determine the next page in a hard-coded way; it always asks for the next applicable, non-Fulfilled descendant Page in the current Container subtree given the current Engine state. This makes navigation correct by construction under structural Flow changes — adding/removing a Page or changing an obligation's appliesWhen adapts navigation without code changes.
obligations.quote: The runtime never asks "what's the next page" in a hard-coded way; it always asks "what's the next applicable, non-Fulfilled descendant Page
obligations.novel_why: Correct-by-construction navigation derived entirely from obligation applicability and Engine state, resilient to dynamic page membership, exceeds the spikes' flow logic.

obligations.title: Return to Task List when the Section subtree is exhausted
obligations.summary: After a successful Page POST, if the walk finds no remaining applicable non-Fulfilled Container in the current Section's subtree, the runtime returns the user to the Task List. This is the end-of-Section exit branch, distinct from STATUS-31's walk-to-next behaviour.
obligations.quote: If none, return to the Task List.
obligations.novel_why: Spike A already ships this fallback shape: selectors.js returns { terminal: 'hub' } when no next live step exists, though not over a Container subtree.

obligations.title: Read-only Pages always carry status Not Applicable
obligations.summary: Pages with no `presents` (read-only/narrative Pages) have status Not Applicable by construction, which is why status-driven skipping covers them without any separate read-only filter and why they can never block a parent's Fulfilled propagation.
obligations.quote: This naturally skips Pages with status Not Applicable (including all read-only Pages)
obligations.novel_why: No spike has a read-only (presents-less) Page concept; A-D compute not-applicable only from conditional applicability, never from a page presenting no obligations.

obligations.title: CYA renders soft prompts for missing items before Fulfilled
obligations.summary: When CYA is visited before the Journey is Fulfilled, the page must render soft prompts for the missing items so the user can see current state and navigate to fill the gaps — reachability (STATUS-33) alone is not enough; the incomplete view has defined content.
obligations.quote: the page renders with soft prompts for missing items, so the user sees current state and can navigate to fill the gaps
obligations.novel_why: None of spikes A-D implements a check-your-answers page at all (no CYA/submit surface in any spike runtime), let alone soft-prompt rendering of gaps.

obligations.title: Transition the Journey to Submitted on successful CYA Submit
obligations.summary: When the server-side re-check on CYA Submit confirms Journey-Fulfilled, the Journey transitions to a Submitted state — a lifecycle state beyond the four-state per-Container taxonomy, marking the terminal outcome of the Journey.
obligations.quote: the Engine re-checks Journey-Fulfilled; if yes, transition to Submitted.
obligations.novel_why: No spike models a submission lifecycle; A-D stop at per-step complete/partial statuses with no Submitted terminal state or transition.

obligations.title: Re-render CYA with missing items called out when Submit re-check fails
obligations.summary: If the server-side Journey-Fulfilled re-check fails on CYA Submit (e.g. state changed since the page rendered), the runtime does not error or redirect elsewhere — it re-renders CYA with the missing items explicitly called out.
obligations.quote: If no, re-render CYA with the missing items called out.
obligations.novel_why: No spike has a CYA submit path, so none defines this stale-state failure branch.

obligations.title: Container is Not Applicable when all its obligations are served elsewhere in the Flow
obligations.summary: The NA status has two distinct triggers: no obligations in scope yet, or every obligation the Container presents is already served by another Container elsewhere in the Flow. Status computation must therefore recognise cross-Container serving — a Container with in-scope obligations still becomes Not Applicable (and is skipped) if those obligations are all fulfilled/served via other Containers.
obligations.quote: Container has no in-scope mandatory obligations — either none of its obligations apply yet, or all are served elsewhere in the Flow.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes ships an obligations/evaluator layer at all (line 1141), and no spike models an obligation presentable on multiple Containers with cross-Container serving collapsing a Container to Not Applicable; spike navigation is step-list driven, not obligation-dedupe driven.

obligations.title: Expose three pure navigation primitives over the Container tree
obligations.summary: The journey model must expose exactly three pure navigation functions (firstApplicablePage, firstUnfulfilledPage, firstPagePresentingObligation) that operate over the Container tree and together answer every runtime navigation question.
obligations.quote: The model exposes three pure functions over the Container tree.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes factor navigation into a small set of pure, rooted traversal primitives as a formal model surface.

obligations.title: Make primitives depth-first and rooted at any Container
obligations.summary: All three navigation primitives traverse depth-first and can be invoked rooted at the Flow root, a Section, or a SubSection, so the same function serves whole-flow and sub-tree navigation.
obligations.quote: All are depth-first and rooted-traversal (can be invoked at the Flow root, a Section, or a SubSection).
obligations.novel_why: Rooted sub-tree traversal supporting SubSection sub-hubs goes beyond the flat task/section structure of the existing spikes.

obligations.title: Guarantee primitives are deterministic
obligations.summary: Each navigation primitive must be deterministic: given identical inputs (tree and state) it always returns the same Page.
obligations.quote: All are deterministic given identical inputs.

obligations.title: firstApplicablePage: status-blind depth-first including read-only Pages
obligations.summary: firstApplicablePage(root) returns the first Page in declared depth-first order, ignoring status entirely and including read-only Pages; it is used for default Section entry. It returns null for an empty Container.
obligations.quote: Status-blind: depth-first to the first Page in declared order (read-only Pages included). Used for default Section entry.
obligations.novel_why: First-class read-only pages that must not be skipped on first entry are outside what the existing spikes implement.

obligations.title: firstUnfulfilledPage: status-filtered to first NS/IP Page
obligations.summary: firstUnfulfilledPage(root, state) returns the first Page whose status is Not Started or In Progress in depth-first order; NA (read-only) and Fulfilled Pages are skipped purely via the status filter. It is used for Resume/Continue shortcuts and takes ObligationState as input.
obligations.quote: Status-filtered: depth-first to the first Page with status NS or IP. Read-only (NA) and Fulfilled (F) Pages are skipped naturally
obligations.novel_why: Skipping via an NA/read-only page status derived from obligation state is part of the novel obligations/status model.

obligations.title: firstPagePresentingObligation: locate Page by obligation id
obligations.summary: firstPagePresentingObligation(flow, obligationId) returns the first Page in depth-first traversal whose `presents` list includes the given obligation id; it powers CYA Change links.
obligations.quote: depth-first to the first Page whose `presents` includes the given obligation id. Used for CYA Change links.
obligations.novel_why: Pages declaring `presents` of first-class obligation ids, and navigation keyed by obligation reference, is unique to the obligations model.

obligations.title: Unit-test each primitive in isolation
obligations.summary: Each of the three primitives must be independently unit-testable as a pure function, with no browser, no orchestrator, and no Engine state beyond the arguments passed in.
obligations.quote: Each is independently unit-testable — no browser, no orchestrator, no Engine state beyond what's passed in.

obligations.title: Declare sectionEntryMode in flow.json
obligations.summary: The Flow config declares which primitive resolves a default Section click via a Flow-level `sectionEntryMode` field whose values are 'firstApplicablePage' or 'firstUnfulfilledPage'.
obligations.quote: "sectionEntryMode": "firstApplicablePage", // or "firstUnfulfilledPage"
obligations.novel_why: A declarative, portable entry-mode field on the flow definition goes beyond the hardcoded entry behaviour of the spikes.

obligations.title: Support per-Section entryMode override
obligations.summary: An individual Section may declare an optional `entryMode` that overrides the Flow-level `sectionEntryMode` for clicks on that Section.
obligations.quote: "entryMode": "firstApplicablePage", // optional per-Section override
obligations.novel_why: Per-section configurable entry resolution is not present in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Default sectionEntryMode to firstApplicablePage
obligations.summary: When not declared, sectionEntryMode defaults to firstApplicablePage, chosen as the safer default so read-only intro Pages are not accidentally skipped on first-time Section entry.
obligations.quote: Default for `sectionEntryMode` is `firstApplicablePage` — safer (read-only intro Pages aren't accidentally skipped on first-time entry).
obligations.novel_why: The default exists to protect first-class read-only intro pages, a concept absent from the existing spikes.

obligations.title: Renderer invokes the resolved entry mode without forked logic
obligations.summary: The renderer must simply read the resolved entry mode (per-Section override or Flow default) and invoke the matching primitive; there must be no branching entry logic implemented inside the renderer itself.
obligations.quote: The renderer reads the resolved mode and invokes the matching primitive — no fork in renderer logic.
obligations.novel_why: Pushing all entry-mode resolution into the model so the renderer is mode-agnostic is a new architectural rule.

obligations.title: Allow multiple parallel navigation widgets
obligations.summary: A Flow/renderer may surface multiple navigation widgets simultaneously (for example, a default Section click using firstApplicablePage plus a Continue affordance using firstUnfulfilledPage); the model supports any combination, and which widgets to render is a Flow/renderer decision.
obligations.quote: The Flow / Renderer can also expose **multiple navigation widgets in parallel**
obligations.novel_why: The spikes each offer a single fixed entry affordance per task, not composable parallel navigation widgets.

obligations.title: Resolve Task List Section clicks via configurable entry mode
obligations.summary: A Task List click on a Section invokes firstApplicablePage or firstUnfulfilledPage according to the Flow sectionEntryMode with per-Section override; this trigger is configurable at both levels.
obligations.quote: `firstApplicablePage` OR `firstUnfulfilledPage`, per Flow `sectionEntryMode` (with per-Section override)

obligations.title: Sub-hub SubSection clicks inherit Section entry behaviour rooted at the SubSection
obligations.summary: In Pattern C drill-in renderings, clicking a SubSection on a sub-hub uses the same entry-mode resolution as a Section click but with the traversal rooted at the SubSection; the mode is inherited rather than separately configurable.
obligations.quote: Same as Section click, rooted at the SubSection
obligations.novel_why: Sub-hub/SubSection drill-in navigation has no equivalent in the flat hub structure of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Resume/Continue always uses firstUnfulfilledPage
obligations.summary: The Resume/Continue affordance, when offered alongside the default entry, always invokes firstUnfulfilledPage; this binding is fixed and not configurable.
obligations.quote: `firstUnfulfilledPage` | No — always status-filtered

obligations.title: CYA Change links always use firstPagePresentingObligation
obligations.summary: A CYA Change link for an obligation always resolves via firstPagePresentingObligation; this binding is fixed and not configurable.
obligations.quote: **CYA Change link** for an obligation | `firstPagePresentingObligation` | No
obligations.novel_why: Change links resolved by obligation id rather than a static page mapping depends on the first-class obligations model.

obligations.title: Container-jump widgets choose their traversal mode per widget
obligations.summary: Special widgets that jump to a specific Container invoke firstApplicablePage or firstUnfulfilledPage rooted at the target Container, and the renderer chooses which mode per widget UX.
obligations.quote: `firstApplicablePage` or `firstUnfulfilledPage` rooted at the target Container | Renderer chooses mode per widget UX
obligations.novel_why: Arbitrary container-rooted jump widgets exceed the fixed hub-and-task navigation of the spikes.

obligations.title: Page-jump widgets navigate directly without traversal
obligations.summary: Special widgets targeting a specific Page navigate to that Page directly; no traversal primitive is invoked.
obligations.quote: **Special widget — jump to a specific Page** | Direct navigation — no traversal

obligations.title: Renderer decides whether to hide a redundant Resume affordance
obligations.summary: When sectionEntryMode is firstUnfulfilledPage, the default click and Resume do the same thing; the renderer may hide Resume or keep it for cross-Flow consistency — this is a renderer choice, and the model simply exposes both primitives.
obligations.quote: The renderer may choose to hide the Resume affordance in that case, or keep it for consistency across Flows — that's a renderer choice.

obligations.title: Unit-test firstApplicablePage structural cases
obligations.summary: firstApplicablePage tests must cover: a flat Section; a Section with SubSections; multi-level nesting; a Section containing only SubSections with no top-level Pages; a single-Page Section; an empty Section returning null; and invocation rooted at any Container in the tree.
obligations.quote: flat Section; Section with SubSections; multi-level nesting; only SubSections (no top-level Pages); single-Page Section; empty Section returns null
obligations.novel_why: Tests over arbitrarily nested Container trees exceed the flat structures the spikes exercise.

obligations.title: Unit-test firstUnfulfilledPage status-filtering cases
obligations.summary: firstUnfulfilledPage tests must cover: all children Fulfilled returns null (so callers can fall back to firstApplicablePage or CYA); one IP among Fulfilled returns the IP; mixed F/NS/IP across SubSections returns the first NS/IP depth-first; NA Pages (read-only or dynamically empty) skipped by the filter; deep nesting with an incomplete leaf found correctly; a Fulfilled SubSection ahead of an incomplete one skipped wholesale.
obligations.quote: all children Fulfilled returns null (so callers can fall back to `firstApplicablePage` or to CYA)
obligations.novel_why: Cases like dynamically-empty NA pages and wholesale skipping of Fulfilled SubSections depend on the novel status/obligation model.

obligations.title: Unit-test firstPagePresentingObligation match cases
obligations.summary: firstPagePresentingObligation tests must cover: a single match; multiple matching Pages returning the first in depth-first traversal; a deeply nested match; and no match returning null as a defensive case, since in-scope obligations should always have at least one presenting Page.
obligations.quote: single match; multiple matches return first in depth-first traversal; nested deep match; no match returns null
obligations.novel_why: Testing lookup by obligation reference is specific to the first-class obligations model.

obligations.title: In-scope obligations must have at least one presenting Page
obligations.summary: The model carries an invariant that every in-scope obligation is presented by at least one Page; the null return from firstPagePresentingObligation exists only as a defensive fallback.
obligations.quote: defensive — in-scope obligations should always have ≥ 1 matching Page
obligations.novel_why: An invariant linking indexed obligations to page membership is part of the novel obligations model.

obligations.title: Test equivalence of the two entry primitives on fresh state
obligations.summary: An integration test must assert that firstApplicablePage(section) equals firstUnfulfilledPage(section, state) when the state contains no fulfilments yet.
obligations.quote: `firstApplicablePage(section) === firstUnfulfilledPage(section, state)` when state has no fulfilments yet
obligations.novel_why: An equivalence property between navigation primitives over fulfilment state is a new kind of test not in the spikes.

obligations.title: Test that sectionEntryMode does not affect final evaluator state
obligations.summary: A test must show that the choice of sectionEntryMode does not change the final ObligationEvaluator state given identical scripted fulfilment sequences; this is a specialisation of the cross-Flow equivalence test.
obligations.quote: the choice of `sectionEntryMode` doesn't affect final ObligationEvaluator state given identical scripted fulfilment sequences
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow/Engine equivalence testing over an ObligationEvaluator is unique to the obligations model.

obligations.title: CYA lists every Fulfilment with values and a Change link
obligations.summary: The check-your-answers page must list every Fulfilment with its values and provide a Change link for each.
obligations.quote: CYA lists every Fulfilment with values + a Change link.
obligations.novel_why: CYA driven by first-class Fulfilment records rather than raw answers is part of the novel obligations model.

obligations.title: Change link targets the first depth-first Page presenting the obligation (settled)
obligations.summary: When multiple Pages present the same obligation, the CYA Change link goes to the first matching Page in depth-first Flow traversal. This is settled as option (a) of the amend-link question, so it is a requirement not an open item.
obligations.quote: the Change link goes to **the first matching Page in depth-first Flow traversal** (settled — option (a) of the amend-link question)
obligations.novel_why: Multiple pages presenting the same obligation, with a deterministic amend target, has no counterpart in the spikes.

obligations.title: Use the ?change=1 pattern for Change mode
obligations.summary: The Change flow reuses the existing prototype's ?change=1 query-parameter pattern: clicking Change renders the target page in change mode.
obligations.quote: The Change flow uses the `?change=1` pattern from the existing prototype

obligations.title: Return to CYA after a change-mode submit
obligations.summary: When a page submitted in change mode succeeds, the runtime returns the user to CYA instead of advancing to the next Page in the Section.
obligations.quote: On submit, the runtime returns to CYA instead of advancing to the next Page in the Section.

obligations.title: Keep change-mode behaviour at runtime level, not declared per page
obligations.summary: The change-mode round-trip is a runtime-level behaviour; the Flow definition must not declare it per page.
obligations.quote: This is a runtime-level behaviour; the Flow doesn't declare it per page.
obligations.novel_why: An explicit model/runtime separation rule about what the portable Flow definition may not contain is a new architectural constraint.

obligations.title: Make Back navigation contextual by arrival source
obligations.summary: The Back link is contextual: if the user arrived at the Page from the Task List or CYA, Back returns there; if they arrived from another Page in the same Section, Back returns to the previous Page in the Section.
obligations.quote: If the user arrived at this Page from the Task List or CYA, Back returns there.

obligations.title: Treat breadcrumbs as an optional renderer concern
obligations.summary: Breadcrumbs are optional per Flow design, offering extra cross-Section/Page navigation; they are best treated as a renderer concern informed by the Container tree, not a separate model concept.
obligations.quote: Best treated as a renderer concern informed by the Container tree, not a separate model concept.
obligations.novel_why: Deriving breadcrumb navigation from the Container tree rather than modelling it is a new decision beyond the spikes' hub-only navigation.

obligations.title: Define journey-level Not Started status
obligations.summary: The Journey as a whole is Not Started when the user has reached the Flow but has no fulfilments.
obligations.quote: **Not Started** — user has reached the Flow but has no fulfilments.
obligations.novel_why: Journey status derived from the presence of fulfilment records is part of the novel obligations model.

obligations.title: Define journey-level In Progress status
obligations.summary: The Journey is In Progress when some Sections have In Progress or Fulfilled status.
obligations.quote: **In Progress** — some Sections have In Progress / Fulfilled status.

obligations.title: Define journey Fulfilled status and gate CYA Submit on it
obligations.summary: The Journey is Fulfilled when all top-level Sections are Fulfilled, and only then is the CYA Submit button enabled.
obligations.quote: **Fulfilled** — all top-level Sections are Fulfilled; CYA Submit is enabled.
obligations.novel_why: A distinct journey-level Fulfilled state that gates submission is part of the Fulfilled-to-Submitted lifecycle absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Make Submitted final with no further changes
obligations.summary: Clicking Submit on CYA puts the Journey into a Submitted state that is final: no further changes to the Journey are allowed.
obligations.quote: **Submitted** — user clicked Submit on CYA. Journey is final; no further changes.
obligations.novel_why: A submit lifecycle with a terminal state is beyond the spikes, which end at a quote summary with no submission.

obligations.title: Show only a read-only CYA after submit
obligations.summary: After submission the only user-facing surface is a read-only CYA page; the user cannot navigate anywhere else in the Journey.
obligations.quote: The only post-submit user-facing surface is a **read-only CYA page**; the user cannot navigate elsewhere in the Journey.
obligations.novel_why: A post-submit read-only navigation lockdown is a new behaviour not covered by the four spikes.

obligations.title: Defer richer post-submit features to the Persistence submit-lifecycle extension point
obligations.summary: Richer post-submit capabilities — PDF download, per-page re-view, amendments, withdrawal, downstream event emission, reference-number generation — are named as possible extension points deferred to the Persistence section's Submit lifecycle, not requirements of this prototype.
obligations.quote: Anything richer (PDF download, per-page re-view, amendments, withdrawal, downstream event emission, reference-number generation) is a possible extension point
obligations.novel_why: Submit-lifecycle extension points are part of the novel persistence/submit lifecycle territory.

obligations.title: Allow free movement between In Progress and Fulfilled; only Submitted is one-way
obligations.summary: A Journey can move back and forth between In Progress and Fulfilled any number of times (for example, fulfilling everything, amending an answer to drop back to In Progress, then fulfilling again); Submitted is the only one-way transition.
obligations.quote: A Journey can move between In Progress and Fulfilled freely ... Only Submitted is a one-way transition.
obligations.novel_why: Explicit reversibility rules over journey lifecycle states, with a single terminal transition, extend beyond the spikes' status handling.

obligations.title: Callers fall back to firstApplicablePage or CYA when a Container is fully Fulfilled
obligations.summary: firstUnfulfilledPage must return null when all children are Fulfilled — stated as a unit-test case for the primitive — with the rationale that this null return enables callers to fall back to firstApplicablePage or to CYA.
obligations.quote: all children Fulfilled returns null (so callers can fall back to `firstApplicablePage` or to CYA)
obligations.novel_why: The existing spikes have no status-filtered navigation primitive, so no fallback contract for the fully-Fulfilled Container case exists in them; it is introduced with the new primitive API.

obligations.title: Three primitives are sufficient for all runtime navigation resolution
obligations.summary: The primitive set is declared complete: every runtime 'where do I navigate to?' decision must be answerable by firstApplicablePage, firstUnfulfilledPage, or firstPagePresentingObligation — no additional traversal mechanism is needed or introduced.
obligations.quote: The three primitives together cover every "where do I navigate to?" question the runtime needs to answer.
obligations.novel_why: The existing spikes resolve navigation ad hoc per route/controller; a closed, sufficient primitive set as a design constraint is new to this model.

obligations.title: firstPagePresentingObligation returns null defensively on no match
obligations.summary: When no Page presents the given obligation id, the primitive must return null (not throw), and this defensive no-match case must be unit-tested even though in-scope obligations should always have a presenting Page.
obligations.quote: no match returns null (defensive — in-scope obligations should always have ≥ 1 matching Page)
obligations.novel_why: The primitive itself does not exist in the four spikes, so its defensive no-match contract has no prior-spike counterpart.

obligations.title: Implement static reachability test
obligations.summary: Provide a fixture-only test that walks the Obligations and Flow JSON and asserts every obligation is presented by at least one Flow page. It is a pure JSON walk needing no engine or browser.
obligations.quote: **Static reachability** — every obligation is presented by at least one Flow page
obligations.novel_why: Depends on obligations as first-class records with `presents` mappings, which no existing spike has.

obligations.title: Implement model-to-HTML alignment test class
obligations.summary: Provide a test class that parses HTML templates and compares them to the Flow page model, asserting the two agree via multiple sub-checks. Inputs are the Flow model plus the HTML templates only.
obligations.quote: **Model ↔ HTML alignment** — page model and HTML templates agree (multiple sub-checks)
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes statically cross-check templates against a declarative page model.

obligations.title: Implement dynamic reachability/completability test class
obligations.summary: Provide property-based or state-enumeration tests asserting that for any plausible Engine state, the navigation algorithm yields pages presenting every in-scope unfulfilled obligation. Requires Obligations, Flow, the Engine, and the navigation algorithm, but no browser.
obligations.quote: for any plausible Engine state, the navigation algorithm yields pages that present every in-scope unfulfilled obligation
obligations.novel_why: Property-based completability testing over an obligation Engine goes beyond the spikes' page-level tests.

obligations.title: Implement cross-Flow evaluator-equivalence test class
obligations.summary: Provide fixture-driven tests asserting that, given identical scripted fulfilments, all Flows of a Service leave the ObligationEvaluator in the same final state. Requires the Obligations model, at least two Flows, evaluators, and scripted fulfilments.
obligations.quote: **Evaluator-equivalence across Flows** — given identical fulfilments, all Flows of a Service leave the ObligationEvaluator in the same state
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow Engine equivalence is explicitly flagged as novel; no spike compares multiple presentations of one model.

obligations.title: Keep browser end-to-end happy-path suite
obligations.summary: Retain a browser-level end-to-end happy-path test (Playwright or similar) against the live app to prove the rendered service actually works. This is the existing prototype Playwright suite.
obligations.quote: **End-to-end happy-path** — the rendered service actually works

obligations.title: Keep static and alignment tests CI-fast
obligations.summary: The static-reachability and model-HTML alignment test classes must run from fixtures only, fast enough for CI; dynamic reachability may use the Engine and navigation algorithm but must not need a browser.
obligations.quote: The first two are CI-fast (fixture-only). The third needs the Engine and navigation algorithm but no browser.

obligations.title: Run alignment sub-checks as one walker per page
obligations.summary: All four model-HTML alignment sub-checks run from the same inputs (Flow plus HTML), structured as one walker per page entry asserting alignment.
obligations.quote: All four run from the same inputs (Flow + HTML); one walker per page entry asserts alignment.
obligations.novel_why: The per-page walker over a Flow model with `presents` entries has no analogue in the existing spikes.

obligations.title: Forward alignment: every presents entry has an HTML input
obligations.summary: Test that every `presents` entry declared on a page in the Flow model has a matching input in that page's HTML template.
obligations.quote: **Forward alignment** — every `presents` entry on a page has a matching HTML input.
obligations.novel_why: Relies on the novel `presents` page-to-obligation mapping.

obligations.title: Treat hard mandate with no input as a strict failure
obligations.summary: Within forward alignment, a hard-mandated obligation whose page has no corresponding HTML input is a strict-case failure, because the user would be provably stuck.
obligations.quote: Hard-mandate variant is a strict case: a hard mandate with no input means the user is provably stuck.
obligations.novel_why: Mandates (hard/soft) are a novel obligations-model concept.

obligations.title: Reverse alignment: every HTML input has a presents entry
obligations.summary: Test that every HTML input on a page has a matching `presents` entry in the Flow model, catching orphan form fields that exist in templates but not in the model.
obligations.quote: **Reverse alignment** — every HTML input has a matching `presents` entry (catches orphan form fields not in the model).
obligations.novel_why: Reverse static template-model auditing depends on the novel Flow model.

obligations.title: Type alignment: widget can satisfy obligation type
obligations.summary: Test that each HTML widget can produce values satisfying its obligation's declared type — for example a `date` obligation must map to a date input or three-part date widget, and `email` to `type="email"`.
obligations.quote: **Type alignment** — the HTML widget can produce values that satisfy the obligation's type
obligations.novel_why: Depends on obligations carrying typed value contracts, a novel model feature.

obligations.title: A11y/required alignment for hard mandates
obligations.summary: Test that for every page entry with `mandate: 'hard'`, the HTML appropriately signals the field as required — for example `aria-required` and error-message id wiring.
obligations.quote: for `mandate: 'hard'` entries, the HTML signals "required" appropriately (`aria-required`, error-message id wiring, etc.)
obligations.novel_why: Ties accessibility markup checks to the novel mandate concept.

obligations.title: State-aware reachability over generated fulfilments
obligations.summary: Test that for property-generated fulfilment combinations, the ObligationEvaluator produces a state and the navigation algorithm yields a page-path the user can follow to satisfy every in-scope unfulfilled obligation.
obligations.quote: for property-generated fulfilment combinations, the ObligationEvaluator produces a state and the navigation algorithm yields a page-path
obligations.novel_why: Property-generating fulfilment combinations against an ObligationEvaluator is beyond any spike's testing.

obligations.title: Detect hard mandates that are never applicable
obligations.summary: Test that for every page entry with `mandate: 'hard'`, the obligation is in scope in at least one plausible Engine state when the page is reached; otherwise the hard-mandate constraint is dead code.
obligations.quote: for every page entry with `mandate: 'hard'`, is the obligation in scope in any plausible Engine state when this page is reached?
obligations.novel_why: Dead-code detection for mandates against Engine scoping is a novel obligations-model check.

obligations.title: Keep plain reachability check in the static class
obligations.summary: The plain 'every obligation has a page' check belongs in the static-reachability test class, not the dynamic class, because it does not need the Engine.
obligations.quote: (The plain "every obligation has a page" check lives in the static- reachability row above, since it doesn't need the Engine.)

obligations.title: Cross-Flow equivalence proves the data-contract claim
obligations.summary: The cross-Flow Engine equivalence test is the formal proof of the central architectural claim that Obligations are the data contract and Flows are interchangeable presentations; without it the claim is aspirational.
obligations.quote: **Obligations are the data contract; Flows are interchangeable presentations**. Without it, the claim is aspirational.
obligations.novel_why: The obligations-as-data-contract / interchangeable-Flows architecture is the core novelty over all four spikes.

obligations.title: Write equivalence scripts at the obligation level
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow test scripts must be written at the obligation level, not the page level — each step names an obligation and a value (e.g. `{ obligation: 'email', value: 'alex@example.com' }`).
obligations.quote: Scripts are written at the **obligation level**, not the page level
obligations.novel_why: Obligation-level scripting presumes obligations as first-class addressable records.

obligations.title: Script-runner walks each Flow via the navigation algorithm
obligations.summary: The equivalence script-runner uses the navigation algorithm to walk each Flow, delivering scripted values to whichever pages present the relevant obligations, then compares the final Engine state as the single point of comparison across Flows.
obligations.quote: The script-runner uses the navigation algorithm to walk each Flow ... Engine state at the end is the single point of comparison.
obligations.novel_why: A navigation-driven multi-Flow script-runner with Engine-state snapshots exists in no spike.

obligations.title: Use the skeleton Flow as a regression oracle
obligations.summary: During development the skeleton Flow serves as a regression oracle for the polished Flow: replaying the same obligation-level scripts against both must produce identical Engine state.
obligations.quote: Skeleton Flow becomes a regression oracle for the polished Flow.
obligations.novel_why: Skeleton-vs-polished Flow oracle testing is unique to the multi-Flow architecture.

obligations.title: New Flows get free coverage by replaying scripts
obligations.summary: Any new Flow added to a Service is covered by replaying the existing obligation-level scripts against it, with no new test authoring required.
obligations.quote: Free coverage for any new Flow added to the Service — replay the same scripts.
obligations.novel_why: Script reuse across interchangeable Flows depends on the novel obligations contract.

obligations.title: Equivalence tests guard Flow-migration data safety
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow equivalence tests also serve as refactor safety (rework the polished Flow and prove data unchanged), migration safety (users hopping Flows mid-flight suffer no state corruption), A/B integrity (experiments vary presentation not behaviour), and multi-channel coherence.
obligations.quote: Migration safety: users hopping Flows mid-flight don't suffer state corruption.
obligations.novel_why: Mid-flight Flow hopping and A/B presentation-only variation are novel multi-Flow scenarios.

obligations.title: Equivalence failures signal logic leaking into Flows
obligations.summary: A cross-Flow equivalence failure usually indicates that a Flow has smuggled in business logic — presentation is doing too much — so the test doubles as a layering-smell detector.
obligations.quote: Surfaces "Flow has snuck in business logic" smells: failure usually means presentation is doing too much.
obligations.novel_why: Detecting layering violations via cross-Flow state divergence is unique to this architecture.

obligations.title: Run cross-Flow scripts only when there is more than one Flow
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow scripts are required whenever multiple Flows are live, A/B experiments run, a mid-flight Flow migration is happening, or a skeleton Flow exists alongside the polished one during development; they are not needed for a single mature Flow with no A/B or multi-channel.
obligations.quote: Single mature Flow in production, no A/B, no multi-channel | No — nothing to compare against
obligations.novel_why: The applicability matrix only makes sense in the novel multi-Flow world.

obligations.title: Build equivalence infrastructure once, reusable across Services
obligations.summary: The cross-Flow infrastructure — script runner, snapshot machinery, and the obligation-language script format — is built once during the prototypes or first productionisation, is reusable across all Services, and is cheap to keep around.
obligations.quote: **Infrastructure** (script runner, snapshot machinery, obligation- language script format): build once during the prototypes / first productionisation.
obligations.novel_why: A reusable obligation-language script format and snapshot machinery is new tooling beyond all spikes.

obligations.title: Maintain per-Service scripts only while multi-Flow
obligations.summary: Per-Service equivalence scripts are only worth maintaining while the Service has multiple Flows to compare: retire the scripts when a Service goes single-Flow and resurrect them when it becomes multi-Flow again.
obligations.quote: Retire scripts when a Service goes single-Flow; resurrect them when it goes multi-Flow again.
obligations.novel_why: Script lifecycle tied to Flow count presumes the novel multi-Flow model.

obligations.title: Treat cross-Flow equivalence as an architectural-invariant test
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow Engine equivalence is an architectural-invariant test proving the layering claim holds; during prototypes and early productionisation it is the mechanism for discovering whether (and where) the architecture is sound.
obligations.quote: Cross-Flow Engine equivalence is an **architectural invariant test** — it proves the layering claim holds.
obligations.novel_why: The layering-invariant framing exists only in the new obligations architecture.

obligations.title: Plan a replacement before retiring the equivalence test
obligations.summary: Once trust in the architecture is established, cross-Flow tests may be scaled down for single-Flow Services, but only if a replacement enforcement mechanism is planned — static/lint checks flagging Flow code doing Engine work (e.g. scoping logic), code-review discipline with the layering docs, or periodic spot-checks. Without one, the layering rots quietly.
obligations.quote: The cross-Flow test was an enforcement mechanism; if you retire it, plan its replacement.
obligations.novel_why: Guarding the Flow/Engine layering boundary is specific to the novel architecture.

obligations.title: Maintain a separate script-coverage discipline
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow tests only verify what the scripts exercise; keeping the script set adequately covering the obligation space is a separate discipline the team must maintain.
obligations.quote: Cross-Flow tests only test what the scripts cover. A discipline of script coverage is separate.
obligations.novel_why: Obligation-level script coverage as its own discipline is new with this test class.

obligations.title: Land dynamic-reachability tests before cross-Flow tests
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow tests depend on the navigation algorithm being correct — a navigation bug makes them fail for the wrong reason — so the dynamic-reachability tests must land first, in that order.
obligations.quote: A bug in nav = the test fails for the wrong reason. So dynamic-reachability tests should land first.
obligations.novel_why: This sequencing constraint between two novel test classes has no spike precedent.

obligations.title: Compare canonical fulfilment values, not per-Flow identifiers
obligations.summary: Cross-Flow snapshot comparison must target the canonical fulfilment values, not per-Flow identifiers, because Engine output includes provenance reasons and fulfilmentIds that differ per Flow for indexed obligations — `user`-source fulfilmentIds are orchestrator-generated UUIDs per Flow.
obligations.quote: Comparison should target the canonical fulfilment _values_, not per-Flow identifiers.
obligations.novel_why: Fulfilment records, indexed obligations, provenance reasons and orchestrator-generated fulfilmentIds are all novel model concepts.

obligations.title: Dynamic test class runs with Engine and navigation algorithm but no browser
obligations.summary: The dynamic reachability/completability test class requires the Engine and navigation algorithm as infrastructure but must not need a browser, keeping it below the Playwright tier.
obligations.quote: The third needs the Engine and navigation algorithm but no browser.
obligations.novel_why: The four existing spikes only had the browser-based Playwright happy-path suite; a browserless Engine-level dynamic test tier is introduced here as a distinct infrastructure requirement.

obligations.title: Treat the code-generation pipeline as an uncommitted stretch goal
obligations.summary: The two-stage code-generation pipeline (obligations to Flow, Flow to HTML) is captured as an aspiration only. It is not part of the core model and there is no commitment to build it; it must not be treated as required scope.
obligations.quote: Captured here as an aspiration — not part of the core model, no commitment to build it
obligations.novel_why: No existing spike has any code-generation pipeline concept.

obligations.title: Keep the layered architecture codegen-friendly
obligations.summary: The layered architecture (obligations model separate from Flow, Flow separate from rendering) is deliberately designed to remain friendly to a future code-generation pipeline, so layering decisions should preserve that possibility.
obligations.quote: worth keeping in mind because the layered architecture is deliberately friendly to it
obligations.novel_why: Designing the layer boundaries specifically to enable generation tooling goes beyond what any spike does.

obligations.title: Define Stage 1: obligations.json to skeleton flow.json plus tests
obligations.summary: Stage 1 of the pipeline takes a Service's obligations.json as input and outputs a skeleton flow.json that delivers those obligations in a simple usable journey, plus generated tests.
obligations.quote: Output: a skeleton flow.json that delivers those obligations in a simple, usable journey, plus generated tests.
obligations.novel_why: Generating a Flow from a first-class obligations file presupposes the novel obligations model.

obligations.title: Accept that Stage 1 is judgement-heavy
obligations.summary: Stage 1 is classified as judgement-heavy: a purely deterministic generator can only produce a trivial baseline (one Page per obligation, alphabetical, all soft mandates), not a usable skeleton.
obligations.quote: A purely-deterministic generator can produce a trivial baseline ("one Page per obligation, alphabetical, all soft")
obligations.novel_why: The auto/deterministic-vs-judgement split for Flow generation, including soft mandates, is not covered by any spike.

obligations.title: Require GDS-convention pattern matching in a usable Stage 1 skeleton
obligations.summary: A usable Stage 1 skeleton generator must pattern-match its output against good GDS journey conventions rather than producing arbitrary structure.
obligations.quote: Pattern-matching against good GDS journey conventions
obligations.novel_why: No spike involves generating journeys, let alone convention-aware generation.

obligations.title: Require related-field clustering in Stage 1
obligations.summary: The Stage 1 generator must cluster related obligation fields into Sections, for example grouping name, email and date of birth into an 'About you' Section.
obligations.quote: Clustering related fields (name + email + DOB → "About you")
obligations.novel_why: Automatic grouping of obligations into Sections is a generation capability absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Require conditional-group detection in Stage 1
obligations.summary: The Stage 1 generator must detect groups of obligations that are conditional (only apply given other answers) and reflect that in the generated Flow.
obligations.quote: Detecting conditional groups
obligations.novel_why: Deriving conditionality structure from an obligations model is unique to the new design.

obligations.title: Require draft copy generation in Stage 1
obligations.summary: The Stage 1 generator must draft sensible page copy and labels for the skeleton journey, not just structural output.
obligations.quote: Drafting sensible copy
obligations.novel_why: Copy drafting as a tooling requirement does not exist in any spike.

obligations.title: Implement Stage 1 as an AI skill using a reference implementation
obligations.summary: The likely Stage 1 tooling is an AI skill (for example a Claude Code skill) pointed at a reference implementation — a previously built end-to-end Service — and it produces files matching that reference's patterns for the new Service.
obligations.quote: an AI skill (e.g. a Claude Code skill) pointed at a reference implementation
obligations.novel_why: AI-driven scaffolding from an exemplar Service is beyond anything the four spikes implement.

obligations.title: Define Stage 2: flow.json plus obligations.json to HTML scaffold
obligations.summary: Stage 2 takes a flow.json and obligations.json as input and outputs HTML templates (one per Page), form-input wiring, basic validation, and generated tests.
obligations.quote: Output: HTML templates (one per Page), form-input wiring, basic validation, generated tests.
obligations.novel_why: Template scaffolding driven by the obligations plus flow model pair is new tooling.

obligations.title: Generate one template file per Page in Stage 2
obligations.summary: The Stage 2 generator maps each Page in the Flow to exactly one HTML template file.
obligations.quote: Each Page → one template file
obligations.novel_why: A mechanical Page-to-template generation rule is not part of any spike.

obligations.title: Generate one form input per presents entry, typed from the obligation
obligations.summary: Each `presents` entry on a Page maps to one generated form input, with the input type derived from the corresponding obligation's type.
obligations.quote: Each presents entry → one form input (type derived from the obligation's type)
obligations.novel_why: Relies on the novel presents relationship and typed obligations as the generation source.

obligations.title: Wire required attributes and validation from hard mandates
obligations.summary: For entries with `mandate: 'hard'`, the Stage 2 generator emits the required attribute on the input plus form-level validation wiring.
obligations.quote: mandate: 'hard' entries → required-attribute + form-level validation wiring
obligations.novel_why: Hard/soft mandates as a first-class model property driving generated validation is novel.

obligations.title: Generate a template loop for presentsForEach
obligations.summary: Each `presentsForEach` entry (indexed/repeating obligations, such as an add-another loop) maps to a generated template loop in the Page's HTML.
obligations.quote: presentsForEach → a template loop
obligations.novel_why: presentsForEach over indexed obligations is a novel model construct the spikes lack.

obligations.title: Make the Stage 2 generator deterministic
obligations.summary: Stage 2 tooling should be a deterministic template-based generator: the same Flow input always produces the same HTML output, keeping it testable, predictable and fast, with maintenance cost bounded to the template library.
obligations.quote: a deterministic template-based generator. Same Flow in → same HTML out.
obligations.novel_why: Determinism guarantees for HTML scaffolding tooling are outside the spikes' scope.

obligations.title: Keep AI polish optional and structural generation deterministic
obligations.summary: An AI polish layer may optionally refine copy, layout micro-decisions and accessibility nuances of generated HTML, but the structural code generation must remain deterministic.
obligations.quote: An AI polish layer could optionally refine copy, layout micro-decisions, and a11y nuances — but the structural code generation stays deterministic.
obligations.novel_why: Splitting AI polish from deterministic structure is a new architectural rule for the tooling.

obligations.title: Use cross-Flow Engine equivalence tests as the oracle for generated Flows
obligations.summary: When an AI generates a skeleton Flow alongside a hand-curated polished Flow, the cross-Flow Engine equivalence tests act as the behavioural oracle: one set of scripts written in obligation-language is replayed against both Flows and the Engine state at the end must be identical.
obligations.quote: Replayed against the AI-generated skeleton AND handcrafted polished Flows... Engine state at the end must be identical
obligations.novel_why: Cross-Flow Engine equivalence testing in obligation-language is explicitly a novel capability.

obligations.title: Catch AI Flow divergence via rendering-independent deterministic tests
obligations.summary: If the AI-generated skeleton Flow produces different Engine state from the polished Flow, a deterministic test that does not depend on rendering must catch the divergence immediately, making AI-assisted Stage 1 safe to iterate on despite non-deterministic AI output.
obligations.quote: the divergence is caught immediately by a deterministic test that doesn't depend on rendering
obligations.novel_why: A rendering-independent behavioural oracle for AI output has no counterpart in the spikes.

obligations.title: Generate tests deterministically with hard-coded expectations
obligations.summary: In the hybrid tooling recommendation, tests must be deterministic and are generated with hard-coded expectations, never AI-fuzzy ones.
obligations.quote: | Tests | Must be deterministic | Generated with hard-coded expectations |
obligations.novel_why: Deterministic generated tests with baked-in expectations is a new tooling constraint.

obligations.title: Keep evaluators pure functions and models pure data
obligations.summary: The evaluators must be pure functions and the static models pure data, because that purity is what makes the several levels of interrogation tooling naturally available.
obligations.quote: The evaluators + static models being pure data / pure functions makes several interrogation-tooling levels naturally available.
obligations.novel_why: Purity as an enabling constraint for an interrogation-tooling ladder over an ObligationEvaluator goes beyond spike-a's pure selectors.

obligations.title: Export ObligationEvaluator and JourneyEvaluator as Node module APIs
obligations.summary: Level 1 interrogation: the ObligationEvaluator and JourneyEvaluator primitives (e.g. evaluateObligations, firstUnfulfilledPage) are exported functions in a Node module, interrogable directly from a REPL or unit test with zero incremental work if exported cleanly.
obligations.quote: The ObligationEvaluator and JourneyEvaluator primitives are exported functions in a Node module.
obligations.novel_why: A first-class ObligationEvaluator API surface is part of the novel obligations model.

obligations.title: Wrap evaluator functions in CLI tools (tim or dedicated binary)
obligations.summary: Level 2 interrogation: wrap the evaluator module functions in the workspace's existing tim CLI or a dedicated obligations/journey binary, supporting scripted use, fixture-driven experiments and batch operations over many fixtures. Modest build effort.
obligations.quote: Wrap the module functions in tim (the workspace's existing CLI, per the workspace CLAUDE.md) or a dedicated obligations / journey binary.
obligations.novel_why: CLI interrogation of obligation/journey state is tooling no spike provides.

obligations.title: Serve obligations.json and flow.json at well-known endpoints
obligations.summary: Level 3 interrogation: serve obligations.json and each flow.json directly at well-known endpoints (e.g. /schema/obligations/:serviceId.json, /schema/flows/:flowId.json) or ship them as static assets in the frontend build, making the models externally inspectable by linters, visualisers and diff-viewers.
obligations.quote: obligations.json and each flow.json served at well-known endpoints (e.g. /schema/obligations/:serviceId.json, /schema/flows/:flowId.json)
obligations.novel_why: Publishing the portable model files for external tooling consumption is a new surface.

obligations.title: Add a config-gated evaluator introspection API
obligations.summary: Level 4 interrogation: expose routes like /dev/obligations/:serviceId/evaluate and /dev/journey/:flowId/evaluate on the frontend service behind a config flag (e.g. EXPOSE_EVALUATOR=true, off in production) to query live Journey state over HTTP for debugging in dev/staging. A production safety story is required and this is the highest-effort level.
obligations.quote: Routes like /dev/obligations/:serviceId/evaluate and /dev/journey/:flowId/evaluate behind a config flag (e.g. EXPOSE_EVALUATOR=true, off in production).
obligations.novel_why: A live obligation-evaluation HTTP introspection API has no equivalent in the spikes.

obligations.title: Sequence interrogation levels: 1 free, 2-3 early, 4 deferred
obligations.summary: The interrogation-tooling investment order is settled: Level 1 (module APIs) is essentially free given the right module structure, Levels 2 and 3 (CLI, served JSON) are cheap early adds, and Level 4 (introspection API) waits until a real debugging need exists with appropriate safety controls.
obligations.quote: Level 1 is essentially free if the module structure is right. Levels 2 and 3 are cheap early adds. Level 4 waits for a real debugging need
obligations.novel_why: The staged investment plan for the interrogation ladder is unique to this design.

obligations.title: Decide regenerability: one-shot scaffolder vs ongoing build step
obligations.summary: Open future-work question: when a Service's obligations change, should the tooling regenerate the Flow and HTML, or is the change hand-applied? The answer determines whether the tools are one-shot scaffolders or ongoing build steps.
obligations.quote: if a Service's obligations change, does the tooling regenerate the Flow / HTML, or is the change hand-applied?
obligations.novel_why: Regeneration-on-obligation-change is a lifecycle question the spikes never face.

obligations.title: Require human polish of AI copy before it goes user-facing
obligations.summary: AI-generated copy must receive human polish before it goes user-facing: skeleton-quality copy might be acceptable as an intermediate artifact, but polished user-facing copy cannot be raw AI output.
obligations.quote: AI-generated copy needs human polish before going user-facing. Skeleton might be OK as-is; polished isn't.
obligations.novel_why: A human-review quality bar on AI-generated journey copy is a new process rule.

obligations.title: Build the first Service without AI tooling as the reference
obligations.summary: The first Service has to be built without AI tooling (or with hand-crafted templates) because the AI skill needs a reference implementation; subsequent Services then use that first Service as a template.
obligations.quote: the first Service has to be built without AI tooling (or with hand-crafted templates). Subsequent Services use it as a template.
obligations.novel_why: The bootstrap-the-exemplar-first sequencing rule only exists because of the novel AI-skill tooling plan.

obligations.title: Compare non-AI alternatives for Stage 1 if AI quality fails
obligations.summary: Deferred question: if AI quality or determinism for Stage 1 does not hold up, compare alternatives — a deterministic template generator with interactive wizard prompts, an annotation-driven generator where obligations carry hints, or a generated YAML stub edited by a human and converted to JSON.
obligations.quote: deterministic template generator with interactive wizard prompts; annotation-driven generator (obligations carry hints); generated YAML stub + human edits + JSON conversion.
obligations.novel_why: Fallback generation strategies, including hint-carrying obligations, extend beyond anything in the spikes.

obligations.title: Defer hybrid source/mutability combinations
obligations.summary: Indexed obligations can in principle mix source and mutability in hybrid ways — seeded but edit-only (audit data), derived but user-can-add-extras, or user-driven with min/max count constraints. The design for these hybrids is explicitly deferred until a concrete journey makes a hybrid case real; only the structural indexedBy shape and per-source fulfilmentId shapes are settled elsewhere.
obligations.quote: Deferred until a concrete journey makes a hybrid case real.
obligations.novel_why: Indexed obligations with seeded/derived sources and mutability combinations are obligations-model concepts absent from all four existing spikes.

obligations.title: Defer cross-obligation references between fulfilments
obligations.summary: When one fulfilment refers to another (e.g. a claim pointing at a specific driver's fulfilmentId across two indexed obligations), two stances exist: a model-level foreign key with runtime-enforced referential integrity (block/cascade deletes), or plain data that the evaluator handles with integrity treated as a scoping concern. The doc leans towards the second as simpler and consistent with 'scoping is a function', but defers the decision until a concrete cross-reference case emerges.
obligations.quote: The second is simpler and consistent with the "scoping is a function" position.
obligations.novel_why: Cross-references between fulfilment records of indexed obligations have no counterpart in the existing spikes' add-another loops.

obligations.title: Do not model evaluator configuration today
obligations.summary: Evaluator configuration (feature flags, statute/rule version, regional rules, A/B cohorts, environment tier) is deliberately not modelled now. Evaluators take no config; the config shape stays a foreseen extension point rather than a speculative commitment, with nothing pinned until a concrete journey needs it. This is a settled decision.
