obligations.quote: The evaluators currently take no config; the shape stays a foreseen extension point rather than something we commit to speculatively.
obligations.novel_why: An explicit no-config policy for an ObligationEvaluator extension point goes beyond the static declarative config the spikes use.

obligations.title: Inject future config at evaluator construction time
obligations.summary: When evaluator configuration does become needed, it must be injected at construction time (createObligationEvaluator({ config })) rather than passed as an argument to evaluate(obligations, fulfilments). This is the settled shape for the extension point.
obligations.quote: it will be injected at construction time, not passed as a function argument
obligations.novel_why: A constructor-injected ObligationEvaluator factory pattern is a new engine mechanism not present in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Keep constructed evaluator pure in (obligations, fulfilments)
obligations.summary: The constructor-injection pattern must preserve the pure-function property: a constructed evaluator remains same-in/same-out for the (obligations, fulfilments) inputs, and tests vary behaviour by constructing evaluators with different configs rather than by mutating shared state.
obligations.quote: the constructed evaluator remains same-in / same-out for `(obligations, fulfilments)` — while letting tests vary behaviour by constructing with different configs
obligations.novel_why: It pins a purity contract on the obligation-evaluator abstraction, which is first-class only in the new model.

obligations.title: Extend construction injection to all evaluator dependencies
obligations.summary: Any other evaluator dependency that becomes injectable — such as a clock (now), randomSeed, logger, or metrics — must follow the same construction-time injection pattern, and the pattern applies to both the ObligationEvaluator and the JourneyEvaluator.
obligations.quote: The same pattern extends to any other evaluator dependency that becomes injectable (`now`, `randomSeed`, logger, metrics, …), for both the ObligationEvaluator and the JourneyEvaluator.
obligations.novel_why: A uniform dependency-injection rule spanning ObligationEvaluator and JourneyEvaluator is architecture beyond the existing spikes.

obligations.title: Record closed-over evaluator context for deterministic replay
obligations.summary: Whatever a constructed evaluator closes over (config, clock, seed, etc.) must become part of the recorded context for a Journey, so that replays are deterministic. The detailed mechanism is deferred until replay is a concrete need.
obligations.quote: whatever the constructed evaluator closes over becomes part of the recorded context for a Journey so replays are deterministic
obligations.novel_why: Recorded-snapshot replay fidelity of evaluator context is a persistence concept none of the spikes have.

obligations.title: Return reasons as coded records
obligations.summary: The ObligationEvaluator returns reasons as coded records with the shape { reasons: [{ code: string, explanation?: string, values?: object }] } — a machine-readable code, an optional developer-facing explanation, and optional interpolation values. This is settled.
obligations.quote: Reasons come back from the ObligationEvaluator as **coded records** — a machine-readable code plus an optional developer-facing explanation and optional interpolation values
obligations.novel_why: Structured coded reason records go beyond spike-c's authored reasons, which are inline strings rather than code+explanation+values records.

obligations.title: Use dot-separated naming convention for reason codes
obligations.summary: Reason codes follow a dot-separated naming convention that keeps them readable and predictable, e.g. obligation.modification.type.applicable.reason.hasModification.
obligations.quote: **The code** follows a dot-separated naming convention that keeps it readable and predictable
obligations.novel_why: A naming convention for machine-readable reason codes is part of the new authored-reason surfacing design.

obligations.title: Resolve reason codes via the frontend i18n mechanism
obligations.summary: Each reason code doubles as the i18n message key; the frontend's existing i18n mechanism (English and Welsh) resolves the code to user-facing display text. The evaluator never carries the display text itself.
obligations.quote: The code doubles as the i18n message key: the frontend's existing i18n mechanism (English + Welsh) resolves it to display text.
obligations.novel_why: i18n-key resolution of evaluator reason codes, including Welsh, is not implemented in any existing spike.

obligations.title: Carry a developer-facing explanation with each reason
obligations.summary: The optional explanation field on a reason is a freeform developer-facing string, never shown to end users. It travels with the reason so anyone reading evaluator output (logs, debug UIs, tests, replay traces) can understand it without cross-referencing i18n files.
obligations.quote: The explanation** is a freeform developer-facing string. Not shown to end users. Travels with the reason
obligations.novel_why: A dual-audience reason record (developer explanation alongside a user-facing i18n code) extends beyond spike-c's single authored-reason string.

obligations.title: Carry interpolation values with each reason
obligations.summary: The optional values field on a reason holds interpolation params for the resolved UI message, e.g. { amount: 500 } for a template like 'Modifications costing over £{amount} must be professionally fitted'.
obligations.quote: The values** are interpolation params for the resolved UI message
obligations.novel_why: Parameterised reason messages with interpolation values are part of the new coded-reason design, absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Keep the evaluator locale-agnostic
obligations.summary: Because Welsh support is a statutory requirement for Defra services, evaluator functions must never contain inline English literals for user-facing reasons; the whole reason surface must be locale-agnostic, with locale resolution done downstream by the frontend's i18n machinery.
obligations.quote: Welsh support is a statutory requirement for Defra services, so inline English literals in evaluator functions were never viable — the reason surface has to be locale-agnostic.
obligations.novel_why: A statutory bilingual constraint shaping the evaluator's reason surface is entirely new relative to the English-only spikes.

obligations.title: Leave translated-string location to the frontend
obligations.summary: Where the translated reason strings live is a frontend concern — wherever the app keeps its other translated copy (e.g. locales/en.json, locales/cy.json). The model layer must not pin the location of translated content.
obligations.quote: wherever the app keeps its other translated copy (e.g. `locales/en.json`, `locales/cy.json`). Not something the model layer pins.
obligations.novel_why: It draws a model-vs-frontend boundary for reason copy that only exists once reasons are first-class coded records.

obligations.title: Hold reasonCodes declaration as an optional extension
obligations.summary: A possible future extension would let obligation records declare a reasonCodes: string[] field naming the codes the obligation might emit, enabling content-design reviewability and unused-code linting. It is explicitly not required today and should be introduced only if content-design or QA asks for it.
obligations.quote: Introduce only if content-design or QA asks for it.
obligations.novel_why: Declaring emittable reason codes on obligation records is an obligations-model extension with no spike counterpart.

obligations.title: Make staleness uniform across all fulfilment sources
obligations.summary: Whatever staleness mechanism is eventually chosen, it must apply uniformly to user-entered fulfilments and system-handled results (lookup results and sub-journey receipts) — there must not be separate staleness regimes per source.
obligations.quote: Staleness needs to be **uniform** across user-entered fulfilments and system-handled results (lookups, sub-journey receipts).
obligations.novel_why: Staleness of stored fulfilments is an explicitly novel concept none of the four spikes handle.

obligations.title: Defer the staleness mechanism choice
obligations.summary: Two candidate staleness shapes are on the table: a per-obligation TTL in the data (orchestrator invalidates the stored fulfilment on expiry so the obligation re-enters in-scope-but-unsatisfied), or an activation predicate over freshness ('in scope if missing OR older than N') that folds staleness into existing activation machinery. The second is more uniform but probably harder to author; the choice is deferred pending a colleague discussion and ideally a concrete staleness case.
obligations.quote: Deferred pending a discussion with a colleague and, ideally, a concrete staleness case to reason against.
obligations.novel_why: TTL-driven or freshness-predicate invalidation of fulfilments goes beyond the spikes' answer-change invalidation.

obligations.title: Defer per-fulfilment metadata fields
obligations.summary: Per-fulfilment metadata is entangled with staleness: candidate fields are fetchedAt/enteredAt (when the value landed, needed by any TTL rule), sourceRef (external system record id for re-fetching, audit and idempotency), raw (original response body for audit/debugging), and versionStamp (rule/model version the fulfilment was captured under, for rule-version invalidation). None are needed by the current MVP — silent tolerate-and-amend covers cosmetic drift — and pinning them speculatively risks choosing wrong, so they are deferred.
obligations.quote: Pinning any of them speculatively risks choosing wrong when the concrete staleness case arrives.
obligations.novel_why: Per-fulfilment provenance/version metadata presumes fulfilments as first-class stored records, which the spikes lack.

obligations.title: Resolve metadata shape within the staleness discussion
obligations.summary: The per-fulfilment metadata shape must be resolved as part of the same colleague discussion that settles the staleness mechanism, not decided separately, because the two are entangled.
obligations.quote: Metadata shape should be resolved as part of the same discussion, not separately.
obligations.novel_why: It sequences two novel fulfilment-model decisions (staleness and metadata) that do not exist in the spikes.

obligations.title: Store system-handled fulfilments in the unified fulfilments map
obligations.summary: System-handled fulfilments — lookup results and sub-journey receipts — live in the same fulfilments map as user-provided fulfilments, keyed by obligation id. There is no separate storage partition for system-provided data. This is settled.
obligations.quote: System-handled fulfilments (lookup results, sub-journey receipts) live in the same fulfilments map as user-provided ones, keyed by obligation `id`.
obligations.novel_why: A unified fulfilments map holding lookup results and sub-journey receipts alongside user answers is a new storage model.

obligations.title: Distinguish fulfilment provenance by obligation type and source
obligations.summary: System-handled and user-provided fulfilments are distinguished not by where they are stored but by the owning obligation's declared type (lookup-result, sub-journey-receipt) and source (user, derived, seeded) fields.
obligations.quote: Distinguished not by storage location but by the obligation's declared `type` (`lookup-result`, `sub-journey-receipt`) and `source` (`user`, `derived`, `seeded`).
obligations.novel_why: Obligation-level type/source provenance fields (seeded vs derived vs user) are obligations-model concepts beyond the spikes.

obligations.title: Treat system-handled obligations as first-class obligations
obligations.summary: System-handled obligations (lookups, sub-journey receipts) are first-class obligations in the model: they use the same declaration surface, the same scope evaluation, and the same tolerate-and-amend behaviour as user-facing obligations. Storage partitioning would contradict this consistency.
obligations.quote: System-handled obligations are first-class obligations in the model — same declaration surface, same scope evaluation, same tolerate-and-amend behaviour.
obligations.novel_why: First-class system-sourced obligations sharing the user-obligation lifecycle are absent from all four spikes.

obligations.title: Handle privacy, access and serialisation per-field, not per-source
obligations.summary: Concerns that might have motivated partitioned storage are ruled per-field instead: privacy is per-field not per-source (a looked-up address is PII just like a user-typed one), access control is per-field/per-role not per-source, and serialisation to different downstream targets is a projection concern, not a storage concern.
obligations.quote: Privacy is per-field, not per-source (a looked-up address is PII just as much as a user-typed address).
obligations.novel_why: It establishes cross-cutting privacy/access/serialisation rules over the unified fulfilments store, which the spikes never confront.

obligations.title: Read provenance from the obligation model
obligations.summary: Any consumer that needs to know whether the user or the system provided a fulfilment must read the obligation's type and source fields — provenance travels via the obligation model, never via storage location.
obligations.quote: Any consumer that needs to know "did the user or the system provide this?" reads the obligation's `type` / `source` fields.
obligations.novel_why: A model-carried provenance contract for consumers depends on the novel obligation type/source fields.

obligations.title: Ship MVP without per-fulfilment metadata
obligations.summary: For the MVP, fulfilments carry no per-fulfilment metadata. Candidate fields like fetchedAt (staleness), sourceRef (external record id) and raw (original response for audit) will likely be needed when the staleness mechanism (K) or failure policies (M) land, and should be revisited then rather than pinned speculatively today.
obligations.quote: Revisit then rather than pinning speculatively today.
obligations.novel_why: It is a settled scoping decision about fulfilment-record metadata, a structure the existing spikes do not have.

obligations.title: Welsh language support is a statutory constraint on the reason surface
obligations.summary: Welsh support is a statutory requirement for Defra services; therefore inline English literals in evaluator functions were never a viable option, and the reason surface must be authorable in both English and Welsh via downstream i18n resolution.
obligations.quote: Welsh support is a statutory requirement for Defra services, so inline English literals in evaluator functions were never viable
obligations.novel_why: None of the four existing spike documents (option-a/b/c/d, spike-a/b/c/d) mention Welsh, locale, or i18n; the statutory bilingual constraint appears only in obligations.md, and no already-extracted item names it — OPEN1-13 and OPEN1-10 capture its consequences (locale-agnostic evaluator, i18n key resolution) but not the driving legal requirement.

obligations.title: Construction injection applies to the JourneyEvaluator as well as the ObligationEvaluator
obligations.summary: The construction-time dependency-injection pattern is explicitly scoped to both evaluators: the JourneyEvaluator, not just the ObligationEvaluator, takes injectable dependencies (config, now, randomSeed, logger, metrics) at construction. No existing item names the JourneyEvaluator as in scope for this pattern.
obligations.quote: The same pattern extends to any other evaluator dependency that becomes injectable (`now`, `randomSeed`, logger, metrics, …), for both the ObligationEvaluator and the JourneyEvaluator.
obligations.novel_why: The two-evaluator scope of construction injection is settled only in this open-items write-up; none of the four spike implementations demonstrate injecting dependencies into a JourneyEvaluator.

obligations.title: Defer recorded-snapshot fidelity details until replay is a concrete need
obligations.summary: While the principle that closed-over evaluator context becomes part of a Journey's recorded context is settled, the mechanics of recorded-snapshot fidelity are explicitly deferred until deterministic replay becomes a concrete need — OPEN1-7 states the recording requirement but not this deferral of its details.
obligations.quote: Details defer until replay is a concrete need.
obligations.novel_why: The deferral disposition on replay-snapshot mechanics exists only in this document; the spikes neither implement nor schedule replay recording.

obligations.title: Decide on-disk Container schema shape
obligations.summary: The JSON schema the model author writes for the Container tree is undecided between (a) three distinct kinds (Section, SubSection, Page) and (b) a unified recursive Container where every node has id, title, and either children (Group form) or presents (Page form). The doc leans towards (b) for clean recursion and JSON portability, noting (a) might surface useful distinctions like top-level Sections defaulting onto the Task List.
obligations.quote: Leaning (b) for clean recursion and JSON-portability; (a) might surface useful distinctions
obligations.novel_why: A recursive Container tree with arbitrary nesting (Sections/SubSections/Pages) goes beyond the flat task-list structure of all four existing spikes.

obligations.title: Decide in-memory runtime representation of Containers
obligations.summary: The Node in-memory representation of Containers is open for discussion with a Node-expert colleague, with three viable patterns: discriminated union plus pure selector functions (most aligned with the evaluator's pure-function style, no hydration step), an abstract Container class with concrete subclasses (needs hydration and toJSON), or factory-plus-closures. It need not match the on-disk schema shape.
obligations.quote: open for discussion with a Node-expert colleague. Three viable patterns

obligations.title: Keep schema choice independent of runtime choice
obligations.summary: The on-disk schema decision and the in-memory runtime representation decision are decoupled: any schema option can pair with any runtime pattern (e.g. unified recursive schema with a class hierarchy) provided hydration cost is acceptable.
obligations.quote: Schema choice doesn't constrain runtime choice — schema (b) paired with classes is a perfectly viable combination

obligations.title: Apply settled status-propagation truth table for mixed-state Group children
obligations.summary: How a Group's status derives from mixed-state children is settled via a truth table (documented in the Navigation and status / Status-propagation rules sections). The table was simplified by collapsing the cannot-start-yet case into Not Applicable.
obligations.quote: Settled — the truth table simplified after collapsing the CSY case into NA
obligations.novel_why: Status propagation across a nested Container/Group tree goes beyond the flat per-task statuses the existing spikes compute.

obligations.title: Model blocked Sections as Not Applicable via appliesWhen
obligations.summary: There is no separate cannot-start-yet status: most Sections that would be 'blocked' are instead Not Applicable via an appliesWhen condition, so the status taxonomy has no CSY state.
obligations.quote: most "blocked" Sections become NA via `appliesWhen`
obligations.novel_why: appliesWhen-driven NA section applicability is new model vocabulary not present in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Model async blockers as obligations whose dependents are NA
obligations.summary: Genuine asynchronous blockers (rather than answer-dependent gating) are modelled as obligations; any Section or Page that depends on such an obligation reports Not Applicable until the obligation is fulfilled.
obligations.quote: genuine async blockers become obligations whose dependents are NA until fulfilled
obligations.novel_why: Obligations as first-class blocking records with dependent-status effects are core to the new model and absent from all spikes.

obligations.title: Enter a Section at its first Page in declared order by default
obligations.summary: Settled: the default Section-entry behaviour navigates to the first Page in declared order regardless of status, so read-only intro Pages are seen on the user's first walk through the Section.
obligations.quote: **Default**: first Page in declared order, regardless of status (so read-only intro Pages are seen on the first walk)

obligations.title: Provide optional Continue/Resume shortcut to first incomplete Page
obligations.summary: Settled: an optional Continue/Resume shortcut navigates to the first applicable Page with status Not Started or In Progress, which naturally skips read-only (NA) and Fulfilled Pages using the existing status taxonomy.
obligations.quote: first applicable Page with status Not Started or In Progress, which naturally skips read-only (NA) and Fulfilled Pages
obligations.novel_why: A status-taxonomy-driven resume affordance does not exist in any of the four spikes.

obligations.title: Derive read-only Pages from empty presents
obligations.summary: Settled: a Page is read-only when its presents array is empty; there is no separate read-only flag in the model.
obligations.quote: Read-only Pages are derived from empty `presents` (no separate flag)
obligations.novel_why: Read-only pages derived from the presents list are a new model concept beyond the spikes' form-only pages.

obligations.title: Decide where the UI exposes the Resume shortcut
obligations.summary: It remains open where and when the renderer exposes the Resume shortcut — e.g. on the Task List for In Progress Sections, as a power-user keyboard shortcut, or per-Section opt-in. Explicitly left as a renderer/UX concern.
obligations.quote: where / when the UI exposes the Resume shortcut (Task List for In Progress Sections? A power-user keyboard shortcut? Per-Section opt-in?)
obligations.novel_why: Concerns surfacing of the novel resume affordance, which no spike implements.

obligations.title: Expose depth-first firstApplicablePage and firstUnfulfilledPage runtime primitives
obligations.summary: Settled: first-incomplete navigation over deeply nested containers is depth-first, and the model exposes two pure runtime primitives — firstApplicablePage(root) and firstUnfulfilledPage(root, state) — for renderers to invoke.
obligations.quote: The model exposes both `firstApplicablePage(root)` and `firstUnfulfilledPage(root, state)` as pure runtime primitives
obligations.novel_why: Pure navigation primitives operating over an arbitrarily nested Container tree with fulfilment-aware traversal go beyond the spikes' flat next-task logic.

obligations.title: Declare Section-entry mode via Flow sectionEntryMode with per-Section override
obligations.summary: Settled: the Flow declares a sectionEntryMode (with per-Section override) that determines which navigation primitive (first-page or first-unfulfilled) the default Section click invokes on the Task List.
obligations.quote: the Flow's `sectionEntryMode` (with per-Section override) declares which the default Section click invokes
obligations.novel_why: Configurable per-Flow/per-Section entry mode is new model vocabulary not present in any spike.

obligations.title: Allow both entry primitives to be exposed simultaneously
obligations.summary: Settled: both navigation primitives can be surfaced at the same time via different UI widgets — for example the default Section click plus a separate Resume affordance.
obligations.quote: Both can be exposed simultaneously via different widgets (e.g. default click + Resume affordance)
obligations.novel_why: Dual entry affordances (default plus resume) per Section are beyond what the spikes' task lists offer.

obligations.title: Require every Flow to have at least one top-level Section
obligations.summary: Settled: a Flow always contains at least one top-level Section; there is no flat top-level pages array alternative in the schema.
obligations.quote: A Flow always has at least one top-level Section — no flat top-level `pages: [...]` alternative
obligations.novel_why: A structural invariant of the new portable Flow model, which none of the spikes formalise.

obligations.title: Use one shape for skeleton and polished Flows
obligations.summary: Settled: skeleton Flows are typically structurally simpler (often a single Section with a flat list of Pages, sometimes using SubSections) but must use exactly the same schema shape as polished Flows — no separate skeleton format.
obligations.quote: they use the same shape as polished Flows
obligations.novel_why: The skeleton-vs-polished Flow lifecycle sharing one schema is a new-model concept absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Let the renderer suppress Task List for single-Section Flows
obligations.summary: Settled: when a Flow has only one Section, the renderer may suppress Task List affordances entirely; this is explicitly a renderer choice, not a model choice.
obligations.quote: The renderer may suppress Task List affordances when only one Section exists; that's a renderer choice, not a model choice
obligations.novel_why: Renderer-side hub suppression for single-Section skeleton journeys is not something any spike does — all four always show the hub.

obligations.title: Defer Task List rendering of nested SubSections
obligations.summary: How the Task List renders nested SubSections is deferred as a possible extension point; four rendering patterns were catalogued (A flat Task List, B indented Task List, C drill-in recursive hubs, D linear walk with Section headings). The Container tree, status taxonomy, navigation algorithm, and tests are all unchanged by whichever resolution eventually lands.
obligations.quote: Deferred — a possible extension point. Doesn't block other progress
obligations.novel_why: Nested SubSection rendering presupposes the recursive Container tree, which no spike has.

obligations.title: Record suggested SubSection-rendering approach: convention plus taskListVisible hint
obligations.summary: If/when SubSection rendering lands, the suggested model-side approach is convention (top-level Sections on the Task List, SubSections only on drill-in views) plus an optional per-Group taskListVisible boolean override; a Flow-level displayPattern ('flat' | 'indented' | 'drill-in') was also considered. Rendering-pattern choice stays a renderer concern unless real journeys need explicit control.
obligations.quote: **Suggested approach when revisited**: convention + optional `taskListVisible` per Group override
obligations.novel_why: taskListVisible/displayPattern are proposed new Flow vocabulary for the nested Container model, beyond anything the spikes carry.

obligations.title: Resolve drill-in sub-hub cross-cutting questions when SubSection rendering lands
obligations.summary: Three cross-cutting questions resurface alongside SubSection rendering: whether Back from a two-level drill-in goes to the parent sub-hub or the top Task List; how status is displayed at drill-in versus top level; and whether nesting of three or more levels is realistic for anticipated journeys.
obligations.quote: sub-hub back-navigation (e.g. drilled in two levels — does Back go to parent sub-hub or top Task List?)
obligations.novel_why: Sub-hub navigation and multi-level status display only exist because of the nested Container tree, absent from all spikes.

obligations.title: Keep NA-Section signposting out of the Engine and status taxonomy
obligations.summary: Signposting Not Applicable Sections on the Task List (showing e.g. Payment or Confirmation as 'coming up' rather than hiding them) is a presentation concern: the Engine must still report the Section as Not Applicable, and the renderer alone reads any signpost hint and decides whether to show the Section.
obligations.quote: This is a **presentation concern** that doesn't belong in the Engine or status taxonomy
obligations.novel_why: NA-section signposting is explicitly a new-model concern with no counterpart in the four spikes.

obligations.title: Formalise the signpost property shape when a real journey needs it
obligations.summary: The likely (not yet formalised) shape for signposting is a signpost property on a Section — a boolean for default treatment or an object with label/hint for custom copy. It replaces the 'Cannot start yet' UX label the old 5-state taxonomy provided, and should only be implemented once a real journey has a concrete need, since copy and behaviour are not yet agreed.
obligations.quote: A `signpost` property on a Section (boolean for default treatment; object with `label` / `hint` for custom copy)
obligations.novel_why: Signpost model vocabulary for surfacing NA sections is new and flagged as a novel-model concept.

obligations.title: Defer differentiated signpost rendering for reset-after-visit Sections
obligations.summary: Edge case deferred: a Section that was previously visited (had fulfilments) can become Not Applicable after an upstream answer changes, with its data wiped per the scope-exit rule. Whether the signpost should render differently for 'visited before but reset' versus 'never visited' is deferred until the renderer needs to differentiate.
obligations.quote: Should the signpost render differently for "visited before but reset" vs "never visited"? Defer until the renderer needs to differentiate.
obligations.novel_why: Combines fulfilment staleness/scope-exit data wiping with NA signposting — both novel-model concepts absent from the spikes.

obligations.title: Define per-obligation failure policies for orchestrator-handled obligations (deferred)
obligations.summary: When a system-handled obligation fails (e.g. a lookup times out or a sub-journey errors), the orchestrator needs a failure policy declared per obligation. Candidate policies considered: block (halt, show error, retry), degrade (treat as unsatisfied, offer fallback path), default (pretend a known default value returned), and stale-ok (use last known good value with a marker). Deferred pending a concrete failing-lookup case and operational-policy input — the shape should not be pinned before real failure modes are known.
obligations.quote: the orchestrator needs a policy declared per-obligation
obligations.novel_why: Orchestrator-executed obligations with declared failure policies are first-class obligation machinery no spike has.

obligations.title: Encode failure policy in the obligation, opaque to the evaluator
obligations.summary: Direction of travel (still deferred with the rest of item M): the failure policy is encoded on the obligation record itself, executed by the orchestrator, and completely opaque to the evaluator — the evaluator never reasons about failure handling.
obligations.quote: encoded in the obligation, executed by the orchestrator, opaque to the evaluator
obligations.novel_why: Presupposes obligations as first-class records and an evaluator/orchestrator split, neither of which exists in the four spikes.

obligations.title: Record leaning toward unified recursive Container schema (option b)
obligations.summary: The doc records a leaning toward schema option (b) — a single recursive Container type with either children or presents — for clean recursion and JSON-portability, while noting option (a)'s distinct types might surface useful distinctions such as top-level-Section Task List defaults. This recorded direction is distinct from the open decision itself (OPEN2-1), mirroring how OPEN2-18 records the suggested approach separately from the OPEN2-17 deferral.
obligations.quote: Leaning (b) for clean recursion and JSON-portability; (a) might surface useful distinctions
obligations.novel_why: No spike declares a unified recursive Container schema; each spike uses its paradigm's native structures, and this leaning exists only in the design doc.

obligations.title: Record discriminated-union plus pure selectors as the most-aligned runtime candidate, pending Node-expert discussion
obligations.summary: Among the three viable runtime patterns, the doc explicitly marks the discriminated-union-plus-pure-selector-functions pattern as most aligned with the rest of the design (matches the evaluator's pure-function style, JSON-portable end-to-end, no hydration step), and states the choice is open for discussion with a Node-expert colleague. This recorded preference and the consultation step are distinct from the open decision itself (OPEN2-2).
obligations.quote: Matches the evaluator's pure-function style; JSON-portable end-to-end; no hydration step. Most aligned with the rest of the design.
obligations.novel_why: None of the four spikes implements a Container runtime representation; the alignment ranking and Node-expert consultation step exist only in the design doc.

obligations.title: SubSection-rendering resolution must leave Container tree, status taxonomy, navigation algorithm and tests unchanged
obligations.summary: The P.6 deferral carries an explicit invariance constraint: whichever Task List rendering pattern is eventually chosen for nested SubSections, the Container tree, status taxonomy, navigation algorithm and existing tests are all unchanged by the resolution — rendering is purely additive at the renderer layer. This constraint on future work is distinct from the deferral itself (OPEN2-17) and the suggested approach (OPEN2-18).
obligations.quote: the Container tree, status taxonomy, navigation algorithm, and tests are all unchanged by the eventual resolution
obligations.novel_why: The invariance guarantee is a design-doc constraint on future rendering work; no spike implements nested-SubSection Task List rendering to have established it.

obligations.title: Render signposted NA Sections on the Task List as coming up, not hidden
obligations.summary: A Section carrying a signpost hint, while still Not Applicable, is displayed on the Task List as "coming up" instead of the default behaviour of hiding NA Sections.
obligations.quote: The signposted Section shows on the Task List as "coming up" rather than being hidden.
obligations.novel_why: No spike implements signposting; existing spikes simply hide or show sections, with no coming-up affordance for not-yet-applicable sections.

obligations.title: Deliver the Cannot-start-yet label via signposting, not a status state
obligations.summary: The UX affordance the dropped 5-state CSY status would have provided must be recovered through the signpost rendering hint when signposting is formalised, keeping the status taxonomy at four states.
obligations.quote: The "Cannot start yet" UX label that the previous 5-state taxonomy would have provided is the main reason to formalise signposting.
obligations.novel_why: Existing spikes have no Cannot-start-yet concept at all; this maps a removed status state onto a presentation-layer hint, a mechanism absent from all four spikes.
